Omus decima pars regiõis z regniiz quid operis faciat natus z accipitur in die ac nocte a saturno in lună z proiicitur ab ascés déte. C-Pars regis atop regni accipit in die a marte i lunáin no cte ecouerſo:z proiicit ab ascen.z est alia pars similis parti pas trum.C-Pars operis z regni Fm Uelliuzaccipit in die ac nocte a sole in gra duz medij celi:z proicitur ab ascen. C-Pars significans utrú sit causa regni aut non accipitur in die ac nocte a sole in gradu medij.celi:z proicitur a io ue. C-Pars martis accipitur in die a venere in luná: in nocte econuerſo:2 proiicitur ab ascendente. Ndecima domus eſt pars amicorum a accipiť in die ac nocte a luna in mercurium: proiicitur ab asceń. Et dixit alendezgod ac cipitur in nocte econuerſo. Pars significás effectú amicop z amicicie accipitur in die a parte fortune in partem futurozum: zin nocte econuerſo z cetera. Aodecima domus eſt pars inimicorű km bermeté z accipitur in die ac nocte a domino domus inimicorú vsqz in gradum z mi nutum domus inimicorum: proiicit ab ascen. Et Fm quosdam antiquorum accipitur in die z in nocte a saturno in martem zć. 7 be sunt ptes.i2.00morze. De sunt alie ptes qbovtunt magistri iudiciop astrop multú.Ex qbus est pars que significat médaciú rumox qn ceciderit in signum tortuofum vel mobile:aut dominus domus fuerit retrograd vel impeditus:aut afpexerint eum mali vel ei sint cõiuncti.Et significat veritaté rumorum eorum si ceciderit cum fortunis aut in domibus seu terminis cap yel afpexerint cum aut fuerit in signo directo: 7 accipitur indie a mercurio ==End of OCR for page 5==
translation: The fourth house is the part of the fathers. It is taken by day from the Sun to Saturn, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the father's death is taken by day from Saturn to Jupiter, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the years is taken by day from the lord of the Sun's house to Saturn, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. And if the Sun or any of Saturn's houses is in its own house, take it by day from the Sun to Saturn, and by night the reverse, and project it from the ascendant, and do not consider whether Saturn is apparent or under the rays. The part of inheritances and possessions is taken by day and night from Saturn to the Moon, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the native's nobility, and of him about whom there is doubt whether he is the son of that father to whom he is attributed, or of another, is taken by day from the degree of the Sun to the degree of its exaltation, and it is projected from the ascendant. But if the Sun is in the degree of its exaltation by day, and the Moon by night in the degree of its exaltation, the part will be in the degrees of the ascendant, and it will be the signification of the degree of the ascendant. The part of the end of things is taken by day and night from Saturn to the lord of the house of conjunction or arrival, and it is added above the degrees of the ascendant, and it is projected from the ascendant. The fifth house is the part of children, and it is taken by day from Jupiter to Saturn, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part that signifies the time when a son should be born, and the number of males or females, and what the woman will bear, is taken by day and night from Mars to Jupiter, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the knowledge of a male and female child is taken by day and night from the Moon to Jupiter, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part by which it is known whether the native about whom the question is asked is male or female is taken by day from the lord of the Moon's house to the Moon, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part signifying the son when Jupiter reaches him, that is, to the degree of the part signifying the son, is taken by day and night from Mars to Jupiter, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of affection and joy is taken by day and night from Venus to Saturn, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the knowledge of the existence of children is taken by day and night from the Moon to Venus, and it is projected from the ascendant. The sixth house is the part of infirmity and azemena, that is, inseparable weakness of some member, and it is taken by day from Saturn to Mars, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of servants is taken by day from Mercury to the Moon, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. And Alendezgod said that it is taken by day from Mercury to the Part of Fortune, and by night the reverse. And he said: it is necessary to use both parts together. The seventh house is the part of the betrothal of men or marriage, and it is taken by day and night from the Sun to Venus, and it is projected from the ascendant. And this part is similar to the part of the mandates of rumors. And the part of the betrothal of women or marriage, according to Hermes, is taken by day and night from Venus to Saturn, and it is projected from the ascendant. And this part is congruent with the part of the cultivation of the earth. Also, the part of the betrothal of women according to Velius is taken by day and night from the Sun to Mars, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of affection and pleasure is taken by day and night from Venus to the degree of the seventh, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of marriages is taken by day and night from Saturn to Venus, and it is projected from the ascendant. The eighth house is the part of death, and it is taken by day and night from the Moon to the degree and minute of the eighth house, and it is added above what Saturn has washed in its sign, and it is projected from the beginning of Saturn's sign. The part of the planet that kills is taken by day from the lord of the ascendant's degree to the Moon's degree, and by night the reverse. The part of the year in which the native fears death, and poverty, impediment, and destruction, is taken by day and night from Saturn to the degree of the lord of the house of conjunction or prevention that occurred before the native's birth. The part of bondage and imprisonment, whether one is freed from it or not, is taken by day from Saturn to the Part of Fortune, and by night the reverse. The ninth house is the part of peregrination, and it is taken by day and night from the lord of the ninth house to the degree and minute of the ninth house, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of travel by water is taken by day from Saturn to the 15th degree of the sign of Cancer, and by night the reverse. And if Saturn is in the 15th degree of Cancer, the degree of the part will be the degree of the ascendant. The part of religion is taken by day from the Moon to Mercury, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The tenth house is the part of region and kingdom, and what work the native does, and it is taken by day and night from Saturn to the Moon, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the king and kingdom is taken by day from Mars to the Moon, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. And there is another part similar to the part of the fathers. The part of work and kingdom according to Velius is taken by day and night from the Sun to the degree of the Midheaven, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part signifying whether there is a cause for the kingdom or not is taken by day and night from the Sun to the degree of the Midheaven, and it is projected from Jupiter. The part of Mars is taken by day from Venus to the Moon, and by night the reverse, and it is projected from the ascendant. The eleventh house is the part of friends, and it is taken by day and night from the Moon to Mercury, and it is projected from the ascendant. And Alendezgod said that it is taken by night the reverse. The part signifying the effect of friends and friendship is taken by day from the Part of Fortune to the part of future things, and by night the reverse, and so on. The twelfth house is the part of enemies according to Hermes, and it is taken by day and night from the lord of the house of enemies to the degree and minute of the house of enemies, and it is projected from the ascendant. And according to some ancients, it is taken by day and night from Saturn to Mars, etc. And these are the parts of the 12 houses. And these are other parts that the masters of astrological judgments use much. Among these is the part that signifies false rumors when it falls in a tortuous or mutable sign, or the lord of the house is retrograde or impeded, or malefics aspect it or are conjoined with it. And it signifies the truth of rumors if it falls with benefics or in their houses or terms, or if they aspect it, or if it is in a direct sign, and it is taken by day from Mercury.

author: Alchabitius
title: Parts of Kingdoms and Revolutions
text: in veneremtin nocte econuerſo z proiicitur z. Et Fm albumafar in die a mer curio in lună z in nocte ecóuerſo rc. Cpars rationis 7 pfúditatis ſenſus ac cipitur in die a faturno in lună z in nocte ecóuerso zc pars ſapientie z di ſcipline accipit in die ac nocte a faturno in ioué z proicit zć. Pars guer re z preliorum accipitur in die ac nocte a faturno in luna3:2 proiicit ab afcé. pars pacis z cócordie exercituú accipit in die ac nocte a luna in mercu riuz z proucitur ab aſcédéte. Pars aſpiciédi in reuolutione anni accipiť in Die a luna in venerem z in nocte ecouerfo a proiicitur a fole. De partibus revolutionú in caufis regnorum. I quia auxiliante deo introduximus bas partes que conueniuntin rebus particularibus profequemur partes que conueniüt in reuos Alutione annorum mundi z cõiunctionum figificantium caufas regni stabilitatem seu durationem eius. Ex quibus est pars que dicitur pars res gni ſeu imperij que exercetur in revolutione mundi:accipitures a marte in lunam z proiicitur ab aſcendente cóiunctionis que ſignificat mutationem regni. Sic quoß alio modo accaptur.f. gradu aſcendentis coniunctionis in gradum coniunctionis a proiicitur a gradu aſcendentis revolutionis. Jtez fit alio modo accipitur a gradu medij celi reuolutióis vis ad ſolez z proicat aioue.i.a gradu iouis. Pars téporis regis ideſt qidiu debet durare acci piť bora electióis regis a ſole in.is.gradú ſigni leonis z piciť a luna deide ac cipit a luna in.is.graduz figni cancri z proicit a ſole. Alia pars de tpe electi onis regis accipitur bora electionis regis in die a ioue in faturnum zin nos cte econuerfo z proiicitur ab aſcendente revolutionis anni in quo furrexerit rex o fi fuerit iupiter in ſigno cõi z fuerit reuolutio diurna: fueritqz iupiter cadens ab angulis tunc accipitur a ſaturnoin ionem:z adduntur defuper. 30.gradus z proiicitur ab aſcédente. Si vero ſaturnus z iupiter fuerit fibi op pofiti z fuerint ambo cadentes ab aſcendente mediatur & exierit inter eos. i accipiatur medietas graduuz quifunt inter eos: proiicitur ab aſcendente Et ſi fuerit iupiter in exaltatione ſua:z fuerit reuolutio in nocte numerat ab eo in ſaturnum z proiicitur ab aſcendente. Ue partes maxime funt ex qbus extrabiť tempus electionis regis z eius durationis Prima earú eft vt aſpicias bora ele Ectionis regis quapuenit profectus anni a cõiunctione triplici tatis que fignificauit ſectam ex numero quodatur oibus.30. Bigradibus annus 7 oibus gradibus duobus cuz dimidio més fis vnus: cum ſciueris in quo gradu vel ſigno fit hic ſerua eum:qa bic eft lo cus a quo equabis pmam premiz cum volueris aptare cum aptabis aſcens dens revolutionis anni in quo furrexit ipſe electus:poſt hoc aſpice a planes ta orientali a ſole er faturno z ioue in ipſo anno vis ad gradum equationis ptis pme oz ſeruatir prolice ab aſcedente revolutionis anni z quo perue nit numerus ipfe est locus pme ptis. C-Pars ſecuda fic inueniť accipe in có iunctione in qua furrexerit rex vel cepit regnúad quod ſignum vel gradum puenit pfectio anni ex numero quo datur oibus.30.gradibus ann 7.2.gia dib cum dimidio méus vſe ad dié quo furrexerit rexzz ipſe erit locus equa tionis fecunde partis:ſerua quos eum poſt hoc afpice a planeta occidens tali a fole ex ſaturno z ioue vis ad locum equationis partis fecunde quam feruafti:z prolice eum ab afcendente revolutionis:z quo peruenerit erit los cus ptis fecúde.be fût ptes que ſignificant fortitudine regis z ei duratióem Lem funt alie partes quibusinytuntur:revolutionibus annorű mundi:z ſcitur per eas quid graue de rebus in f010:7 quid erit leue in precio:quidue preciofum feu vile:multum ſeu paruz:hoc est vt afpicias vbi ceciderit pars ideſtin cuius planete domo exs altatione termino vel triplicitate:qui planeta ſi fuerit retrogra dus vel cõbustus vel in malo loco vilefcit illa res z erit parui predij. Si vero fuerit in loco fortitudinis aut in angulor maxime in medio celi grauabitur illa res z erit magni precij:z ſi peruenerit dominus domus ad locum defcés ſionis fue vilefcit illa res z aſpice aſpectus fortunarum 7 planetarum malo rum ad ea necnon zad lunar dim eius:7 vide quo modo a quis aſpiciat ip fam partem ſi fortuna z luna afpexerint illa partem multiplicabitur illa res: fi'afpexerint eam mali detrimentuz patietur. Pars diſpoſitionis anni a luna in mercurium. Pars aque a luna in venere. C-Pars ordei a luna in ionem. C-Pars ciceris eft quoddam genus leguminis a venere in folem. Pars lentis a martein ſaturnum.C-Pars fabarum a faturno in martem Pars almusi.i.milija ſaturno in martem. C pars ceparuma ſaturno in martem.C-Pars tritici a fole in marté. C-Pars nucuza mercurio in marte Pars zucari a mercurio in venerem. Pars dactilorum a fole in venes rem. Pars mellis a luna in ſolez. C-Pars alroz.i.ris q est genus farris a ioue in faturnum.C-Pars oliuarum a mercurio in lunam. C Pars vuaruz a faturno in venerem. C-Pars bombicis a mercurio in venere. C-Pars alo nicem.i.fifami quoddam genus ſeminis albi in ſimilitudine ſeminis lini: T vtuntur eo medici.fit autem ex eo vngétum vtile a mercurio in ionem vel a Saturno in venere. Pars que funt albuceflex idest mellones qui funt mas gni a crocei a mercurio in faturnum.C-Pars ciborum acrium a faturno in martem. C-Pars ciborum dulcium a ſole in venerem.C-pars ciborum fa poris apij vel buiufmodi herbarum faporem habentium a marte in ſatur, num.CPars ciboum amarozum a mercu.in ſaturnum. Pars medicas minú acrium a ſaturno inionem. Pars medicaminum falſorum a marte in lunam.C-Pars medicaminum venenatoruza genzabar.i.a capite dracos nis in ſaturnum:z proicianť vniuersa bec ab afcendente.revolutionis anni. Dec funt vniuersa que ceciderüt nobis ex ptib?:itroduxim quoqz bas pres nouiffimas:z ſi eſt i eis narratio debilis:ne dimitterem aligdy poffet eé in troductouű ad magisterin indiciorum aſtroi quin pferamus illiud. Isbula amicicie z in imicicie ſignorum. Amici 四土石 X४ㅍㅍ Inimici
translation: in Venus at night, and conversely, and it is projected. And according to Albumasar, in the day from Mercury to the Moon, and at night conversely, etc. The part of reason and profundity of sense is taken in the day from Saturn to the Moon, and at night conversely, etc. The part of wisdom and discipline is taken in the day and night from Saturn to Jupiter, and it is projected, etc. The part of war and battles is taken in the day and night from Saturn to the Moon, and it is projected from the Ascendant. The part of peace and concord of armies is taken in the day and night from the Moon to Mercury, and it is projected from the Ascendant. The part of looking in the revolution of the year is taken in the day from the Moon to Venus, and at night conversely, and it is projected from the Sun. Concerning the parts of revolutions in the causes of kingdoms. Because, with God's help, we have introduced these parts which are suitable for particular matters, we will pursue the parts which are suitable for the revolutions of the years of the world and the conjunctions signifying the causes of the kingdom's stability or its duration. Among these is the part which is called the part of the kingdom or empire, which is exercised in the revolution of the world: it is taken from Mars to the Moon, and it is projected from the Ascendant of the conjunction which signifies the change of the kingdom. Likewise, it is taken in another way, namely, from the degree of the Ascendant of the conjunction to the degree of the conjunction, and it is projected from the degree of the Ascendant of the revolution. Also, it is taken in another way from the degree of the Midheaven of the revolution to the Sun, and it is projected from Jupiter, i.e., from the degree of Jupiter. The part of the king's time, that is, how long he should endure, is taken at the hour of the king's election from the Sun to the 15th degree of the sign of Leo, and it is projected from the Moon; then it is taken from the Moon to the 15th degree of the sign of Cancer, and it is projected from the Sun. Another part concerning the time of the king's election is taken at the hour of the king's election in the day from Jupiter to Saturn, and at night conversely, and it is projected from the Ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the king arose. If Jupiter was in a common sign and the revolution was diurnal, and Jupiter was cadent from the angles, then it is taken from Saturn to Jupiter, and 30 degrees are added above, and it is projected from the Ascendant. But if Saturn and Jupiter were opposite to each other and both were cadent from the Ascendant, the midpoint between them is taken, i.e., half the degrees between them are taken, and it is projected from the Ascendant. And if Jupiter was in his exaltation, and the revolution was at night, it is counted from him to Saturn, and it is projected from the Ascendant. These are the parts from which the time of the king's election and its duration are primarily extracted. The first of these is to look at the hour of the king's election, which comes from the annual profection from the conjunction of the triplicity which signified the sect, from a certain number, for every 30 degrees a year, and for every two and a half degrees a month. When you know in what degree or sign this is, keep it, because this is the place from which you will equate the first part. And when you want to apply it, you will apply the Ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the elected one arose. After this, look from the oriental planet, from the Sun, and Saturn, and Jupiter in that year, to the degree of the equation of the first part, which is kept. Project it from the Ascendant of the revolution of the year, and where the number arrives, that is the place of the first part. The second part is found thus: take at the conjunction in which the king arose or began to reign, to what sign or degree the profection of the year arrived, from the number given for every 30 degrees a year, and 2 and a half degrees a month, up to the day the king arose, and that will be the place of the equation of the second part. Keep it. After this, look from the occidental planet, from the Sun, and Saturn, and Jupiter, to the place of the equation of the second part which you kept, and project it from the Ascendant of the revolution, and where it arrives will be the place of the second part. These are the parts which signify the strength of the king and his duration. There are other parts which are used in the revolutions of the years of the world, and by them it is known what is heavy in the market, and what will be light in price, or what is precious or vile, much or little. This is to look at where the part fell, that is, in whose planet's house, exaltation, term, or triplicity. If that planet is retrograde or combust or in a bad place, that thing will be vile and of little value. But if it is in a place of strength or in the angles, especially in the Midheaven, that thing will be heavy and of great price. And if the lord of the house comes to the place of its fall, that thing will be vile. And look at the aspects of the benefics and malefics to them, and also to the Moon and its ruler. And see how and by whom that part is aspected. If a benefic and the Moon aspect that part, that thing will be multiplied. If malefics aspect it, it will suffer detriment. The part of the disposition of the year from the Moon to Mercury. The part of water from the Moon to Venus. The part of barley from the Moon to Jupiter. The part of chickpeas (which is a kind of legume) from Venus to the Sun. The part of lentils from Mars to Saturn. The part of beans from Saturn to Mars. The part of millet from Saturn to Mars. The part of onions from Saturn to Mars. The part of wheat from the Sun to Mars. The part of nuts from Mercury to Mars. The part of sugar from Mercury to Venus. The part of dates from the Sun to Venus. The part of honey from the Moon to the Sun. The part of rice (which is a kind of grain) from Jupiter to Saturn. The part of olives from Mercury to the Moon. The part of grapes from Saturn to Venus. The part of cotton from Mercury to Venus. The part of sesame (which is a kind of white seed similar to flax seed, and doctors use it, and a useful ointment is made from it) from Mercury to Jupiter or from Saturn to Venus. The part of melons (which are large and yellow) from Mercury to Saturn. The part of sour foods from Saturn to Mars. The part of sweet foods from the Sun to Venus. The part of foods with the taste of celery or similar herbs from Mars to Saturn. The part of bitter foods from Mercury to Saturn. The part of sour medicines from Saturn to Jupiter. The part of salty medicines from Mars to the Moon. The part of poisonous medicines from the Dragon's Head to Saturn. And all these are projected from the Ascendant of the revolution of the year. These are all the parts that have come down to us. We have also introduced these last parts, and if there is a weak narration in them, it is so that I would not omit anything that could be introduced to the mastery of astrological judgments, but rather prefer it. The table of friendship and enmity of the signs. Friends: Aries, Leo, Sagittarius, Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn, Gemini, Libra, Aquarius. Enemies: Cancer, Scorpio, Pisces.

author: Alchabitius
title: The Wise Man Rules the Stars
text: Lomentum Joanis de ſaxonia ſuper textu Alcbabicij. Fr ſapiens dominabitur aftris.Dicit Ptholes meus in fapientijs almagesti. Et potest decla rari fic. Jlle dominabiť aftris q effectus pues nientes ex ipfis aftris potest impedire vel pro hibere:fed hoc poteft facere vir fapiés quare ze. Maior est manifeſta:q ille dominat alteri qui poteft fibi dicere fac boc z facit: 7 dimitte boc z dimittit. Minor pbat auctoritate Pto lemei in guta ppoſitione centiloquii vbi dicita Optimus astrologus multum malum phibe repot qo km ſtellas venturuni eſt cú eius nas turam prefciuerit.fic enim premuniet eum cui malum venturum eſt vt cum venerit poffit il lud pati. Et cófirmatur auctoritate eiusdem in ppofitione octaua eiufdé vbi dicit.Anima ſapiens ita adiuuabit opus ſtella rum quéadmodum ſeminator fortitudines naturales. Sed aduertédú eft de modo per qué ſapiens potest impedire vel adiuuare opus ſtellar.Lertu eſt e nos nó poffumus fimplr impedire influētiam celeftem ficut nec cobu ſtionem ignis:fed poffumus difponere paſſuz ad recipiendum alio vel alio modo influétiam celestem. Uidemus enim idem calor folis glaties diffol uit a cóftringit lutum. Ponit baly de boc familiare exemplum in comento preallegate ppoſitidis. Optimus astrologus zĉ.Dicens ſi ſciuerimus q des beat alicui euenire egritudo calida z ficca de natura martis:poterimus ipz ante aduentum illius influétie mutare ad oppofitum.f.ad frigiditatem z hu miditatem:z fic influétia que deberet ſibi facere egritudinem reducet ipſum ad temperamentum. Patet etiam cuilibet & poffumus impedire actióez ignis ne comburat aliquod combustibile determinatum.f.remouendoipm ab igne vel pijciēdo aquam ſuper ipſum.CCú igitur ſciétie diuidatur ſecus dum diuifionem rerum de quibus funt: quilibet dicatur ſapiens in ſciétia ſua accipiédo large ſapiétiam pſciétia ficut accipit albumazar in multis los cis fui introductozij:vt videamus de quibus ſapiétibus fint dicta verba pa pofita:poffumus diftinguere inter ſapiétes z ſapiétes modo quodá vniuer fali. De numero igitur ſapiétum adam funt ſapiétes veraciter:qda appa renter. Danc diftinctionem tangit Ariftoteles in primo elenchorů vbi dicit. Quidam funt pulchrippter decorem:gdam autem fingut componētesſe. Et ifti.f.appareter ſapiétes funt bipartiti.quoniá gdam eop apparent ſapié tes folum ſecúdum estimationem vulgi ficut diuites huius mundi. 7gdam apud prouectos ficut ſophiste. Primi funt habentes diuitias huius mundi de quibus dicit Albumazar in introductorio ſuo magno vbi loquitur de ſe ctis cótradicentibus astronomie. Et füt illi de noua fecta.Dicit ibi Albumas zar. Dignior apud vniuerfitatem vulgi eſt cui fuerit plus ſubſtantie. ficcs fit quia dignitas ſubſtatie apud ipos eſt plus dignitate sciétie. Et dicut & cum fuerit bo bis ſubſtatia no impedit en ſi fuerit imperitus in ſciétia aftrorű:z medicine z ceteran ſciar pbates pbatióe falſiſſima:q: pbant ſciamp fubſta tiam. Et bec pbatio erroris eft pbatio falfiffima:qires pbátur p genus fuú ip cofimile.i.ſapiétia p ſapiétiaz ſuba pſubamiz nopbank p nó cofimile fibi ergo nó pbat ſapiétia p Baz. Alij apparét ſcientes vel ſapiētes apo aliquas liter puectos ficut ſophiste. De qbus dicit Hyin smo eléchoy.qbuſdá ma gis opere pciú est videri z nő exiftere qz'existere z nó videri:z magiſter Alae nus de infula loqués de eis dicit. Puius ſcolas vitát paupes legiſte:veras lites agitat:fiunt agoniste. Dic pbat bic improbat:huic pcluditiste. Atq3 040 centű cóclamat ore ſophifte. Uere ſciêtes differunt km magis z minus cer tum:vt dicit Lométator ſcão methapbifice. Scie mathematice ſur in pgra du certitudinis. Naturales vo ſequutur eas. Jſti minus certi..logici z natus rales nó tráfferétes se ad alias ſcias nó bitde phia nifi oba: de iftis et dicit magifter Alanus. Jſte ſemp clamitat z argumétak:dú Ariftotelicas latebras rimaf.Sed fi qras quair aut qd epulat mens studio viuit fed veter philofo phat. Magis certi funt mathematici. Et illi fűt biptiti qmqda cop ſtudétin motibus tím z nó curát de iudicijs: vel negat ea. Et ifti füt de gbus dicit Al bumazar in örta z quta ſecta dicētiú iudiciis imo videbit poftea. Secúdi aut ſtudét in motibus z in iudicijs:de gbus dicit Alanus loqués de ipfo Al bumazar. Illic aftra polos celú ſeptéq3 planetas cófulit Albumazar terrifos reportat coy.Cófiliú armás terras firmaſqz caduca. Lótra celestes iras fus perűs furosé. Deistis Ptolemeus dicit iba ppofita. Uir ſapiens dñabitur akris. In qbus vbis Prolemeus rágit tres pprietates veladitiões quas 83 verus philofophus here: maxime astronomus kmäs tria gña boium ab astronomia repellunt. Prima códitio eſt ſtabilitas intétionis. Scda é bas bilitas diſponis. Lertia eft abdicatio terrene poffeffionis. Primátangit cuz dicit vir.Secuda cú dicit:ſapiēs. Zertiá cú dicit:oñabiť aftris. Quatuad pmá pditioné dicit Ptolemeus in pma ppoſitióe cetiloquij. Scia ſtellaruz ex te z ex illis est. Et haly in comento eiufdé dicit. Qui res futuras ſcire desi derat duab vijs opz incedere. Una vt accipiat motű ftellay z opa que fiunt in rebus Fm motú ear. Et afpiciat libros quos antig ſcripferit de ſignifica tiõe motuű ipſapiz adiūgat bis cunos pbauit in the fuo. Lû ergo magnus fit laboz in aſpiciédo motus ſtellay nó pot aſtronomus eé effeminat nec red miſſus.Et cu multú tps regrať ad refpiciendú libros antiquop nó pót eé va gabudus. Opz ergo vt astronomus fit firmus z ſtabilis in meditatióe. Et fic effeminati z remiffi ab astronomia fut repulfi. Quantú ad ſcram códitios nem dicit Ptolemeus in feptima ppoſitione cétiloquij. Nemo poterit dare certa iudicia Fm ſtellay cóplexioné: niſi bó qvim aie z coplexione naturalez bene cognouerit vult ergo q bó naturals d3 effe difpofitus ad iftamſciam 9d3 pficere in ea. Doc fm 9 mibi vr bziſta ſcia pre oibo ſcientijs. Uidi eniz bonos clericos in logica z in naturali pbia q nullo mo poterat aligd cape & astronomia:imo nec algorismu. Patet ergo o naturaliter indiſpoſiti repulfi fut ab astronomia.de qbus Albumazar facit fectam im & videbitur ſtatiz. Quantú ad tertia coditioné dicit Paly in cométo pme ppoſitionis cétilos d
translation: The commentary of John of Saxony on the text of Alchabitius. The wise man will rule the stars. My Ptolemy says in the wisdom of the Almagest: And it can be declared thus: He will rule the stars who can impede or prohibit the effects coming from them. But a wise man can do this, therefore, etc. The major premise is manifest: that he rules another who can tell him to do this and he does it, and to leave this and he leaves it. The minor premise is proven by the authority of Ptolemy in the first proposition of the Centiloquium, where it is said: The best astrologer can prohibit much evil that is to come according to the stars, when he has foreknown its nature. For thus he will forewarn him to whom evil is to come, so that when it comes, he may be able to endure it. And it is confirmed by the authority of the same in the eighth proposition of the same, where he says: A wise soul will assist the work of the stars just as a sower assists natural strengths. But it must be noted concerning the way in which a wise man can impede or assist the work of the stars. It is certain that we cannot simply impede celestial influence, just as we cannot impede the combustion of fire; but we can dispose the patient to receive celestial influence in one way or another. For we see that the same heat of the sun dissolves ice and hardens clay. Haly gives a familiar example of this in his commentary on the aforementioned proposition: The best astrologer, etc., saying: if we know that someone is to suffer a hot and dry illness of the nature of Mars, we can, before the advent of that influence, change him to the opposite, that is, to coldness and humidity; and thus the influence that should have caused him illness will reduce him to a temperate state. It is also clear to everyone that we can impede the action of fire from burning a specific combustible, that is, by removing it from the fire or by pouring water over it. Since therefore knowledge is divided according to the division of the things of which they are, everyone is called wise in his own knowledge, taking wisdom broadly for knowledge, as Albumazar takes it in many places of his Introduction: so that we may see of which wise men the proposed words are spoken: we can distinguish between wise men and wise men in a certain universal way. Therefore, among the number of wise men, some are truly wise, others apparently so. Aristotle touches on this distinction in the first book of the Elenchi, where he says: Some are beautiful because of their beauty; others, however, pretend to be so. And these, that is, apparently wise men, are divided into two parts: for some of them appear wise only according to the estimation of the common people, like the rich of this world. And some among the advanced, like the sophists. The first are those who have the riches of this world, of whom Albumazar speaks in his great Introduction where he discusses the sects contradicting astronomy. And these are of the new sect. Albumazar says there: He is more worthy in the eyes of the generality of the common people who has more substance. Thus it is because the dignity of substance among them is greater than the dignity of knowledge. And they say that when a man has substance, it does not hinder him if he is unskilled in the science of the stars, and medicine, and other sciences, which he proves with a most false proof: for they prove science by substance. And this proof of error is a most false proof: for things are proven by their genus and by what is similar to them, that is, wisdom by wisdom, substance by substance, and not by what is dissimilar to them, therefore it does not prove wisdom by substance. Others appear learned or wise among some advanced people, like the sophists. Of whom Haly says in the first book of the Elenchi: For some it is more worthwhile to seem and not to be than to be and not to seem; and Master Alanus de Insulis, speaking of them, says: Poor jurists avoid his schools: he stirs up real disputes: they become antagonists. Here he proves, here he disapproves: to this he concludes, to that. And the sophist shouts with a hundred mouths. Truly learned men differ according to more and less certainty, as the Commentator says in the second book of Metaphysics. Mathematical sciences are in the highest degree of certainty. Natural sciences follow them. These are less certain, that is, logicians and natural philosophers not transferring themselves to other sciences, they have nothing of philosophy except words: of these also Master Alanus says: He always shouts and argues, while he searches the hidden places of Aristotle. But if you ask what or how much the mind lives on by study, but rather by philosophizing. Mathematicians are more certain. And these are divided into two parts: for some of them study the motions of the times and do not care about judgments, or deny them. And these are of whom Albumazar speaks in the sixth and fifth sect of those who speak of judgments, indeed it will be seen later. The second, however, study motions and judgments: of whom Alanus speaks, referring to Albumazar himself: There Albumazar consults the stars, the poles, the sky, and the seven planets, and brings back their reports to the earth. He arms the lands and the perishable things with counsel. Against celestial wrath and the fury of the gods. Of these Ptolemy says in the proposed words: A wise man will rule the stars. In these words Ptolemy touches on three properties or conditions that a true philosopher must have, especially an astrologer, by which three kinds of men are repelled from astronomy. The first condition is stability of intention. The second is ability of disposition. The third is the renunciation of earthly possessions. He touches on the first when he says: man. The second when he says: wise. The third when he says: he will rule the stars. As for the first condition, Ptolemy says in the first proposition of the Centiloquium: The science of the stars is from you and from them. And Haly in his commentary on the same says: He who desires to know future things must proceed in two ways. One is to accept the motion of the stars and the works that are done in things according to their motion. And to look at the books that the ancients wrote about the signification of their motions, and to add to these what he has proven in his own theory. Therefore, since there is great labor in observing the motions of the stars, an astrologer cannot be effeminate or remiss. And since much time is required to consult the books of the ancients, he cannot be a vagabond. Therefore, it is necessary that an astrologer be firm and stable in meditation. And thus the effeminate and remiss are repelled from astronomy. As for the second condition, Ptolemy says in the seventh proposition of the Centiloquium: No one will be able to give certain judgments according to the complexion of the stars, unless he is a man who has well known the strength of the soul and the natural complexion. Therefore, he wants a man to be naturally disposed to this science so that he may profit in it. This, according to what seems to me, is this science above all sciences. For I have seen good clerics in logic and natural philosophy who could in no way grasp anything of astronomy, not even algorism. Therefore, it is clear that those who are naturally indisposed are repelled from astronomy, of whom Albumazar makes a sect, as will be seen immediately. As for the third condition, Haly says in the commentary on the first proposition of the Centiloquium

author: Alchabitius
title: On the Conditions for Astrological Knowledge
text: quij. Quia oportet nos follicitos eé circa mudana aufert a nobis futuroni cognitio:ſed ſi a műdanis oib? ſeparemur poſſemus futura prognofcere.fi cur videmus beremitay of plures q futura predicút. Et epilenticor qdam dum epilétia torquétur futura pdicunt:qa tuc corpeis ſenſibus nó vtunt fed ſola vi anime. Pater ergo philofophus no dzeſſe diues teſte philoſopho ſeptimo politice. Non ops philofophum eſſe dim terre z maris:ſed fufficit vt habeat famulum miniftratem fibi olera. Et fic babentes diuitias bui mű di ab astronomia funt repuli. Quod aduertés Socrates aurum proiecit in mari ficut narrat Ualerius. Jſta conditio nó placet multis: nec etiam mibi. Quátum ad iftas coditiones conſolatur nos Albumazar in introducto rio:z ſpaliter quátum ad vltima:quia mutat eam ad oppofitum.Dicit fic. Et fortaffis no ſufficiunt aliqui ad ſapiétiam huius magisterij dum afpiciút in quibufdam rebus peruenire ex veritate a comprehéfione earum ad hoc vt poffint errorem a ſe repellere pp breuitatem exercitij cop in cognitióe oiuz rerum.quarú cognitio neceſſe eſt illis in illa eadem intétione:quátum ad pri má conditiões. Et paucis interpofitis dicit. Et hoies subtiles 7 profunde ſa piétie forfitan nó ſufficiút multi corum qui vtunk eis comprehédere eas:cú os fuerit ſciétia buius magiſtery ceterorus magiſterior que neceffaria fút in preſentia rerum pre profunditate quá diximus a ſenſibus longinquan tum ad ſecudam códitionem. Lunc coſolatur nos z dicit. Non debet annul lari quod ex eius ſciétia poteft pertingi ppter illud quod nó poteſt pertingi. Et paucis interpofitis dicit quia modicu ſapiétie multi profectus. Et in boc mutat tertiam conditionem ad oppofitum. Et probat o maior vtilitas fit in astronomia iz in aliqua alia ſcientia. Et ſi contingat in ea error tolerabilion est z minus nocet qž in ceteris ſcientijs Et dicit fic. Uidemus nāca medicos errarein prefcientia infirmitati morboy dum predicunt que accidit infir mo ex grauitate morbi z eius leuitate z exceleritate ſanitatis z eius tarditas te:eius quoqz ſanitate uel morte:fed illud modicum erroris medicomm nó impedit cos ab appetitu ſui magisterij z ab eius diſpoſitióe z exercitio: nec generať reprehéfio vel dubium boium erga eos q viderút ex modico erros ris in ſuo magiſterio querere vtilitatem z profectus per eori curationem z acquiefcere eop medicaminibus. Similr 7 diſpoſitores navium nó dimittűt magisterium fuum nec dimittut boies curſum maris ppter modicú errous nautarum. Similr ceterarű ſapiétiarum z magiſteriorum auctores uix pof. funt euadere accidentia erroris feu aduentum alicuius impedimenti quod euenit eis:fed ppter hoc no annullatur eorum magiſteriű. Et borum omniú que diximus erros impediméti plus eſt of erroz astrologorum:qr medicus cum erraueritin prefcientia infirmitatum:aut curatōe:aut in medicaminib fortaffis erit boc cauſa perditionis animaru. Et cum errauerint naute for taſſis erit boc cauſa perditionis nauium 7 earum naufragium z eorum qui in eis funt interitus. Et ſi errauerint paſtores z oceptionis animaliú aucto res erit boc foraffis prouocatio perditionis eiufdem generis animaliú. At veroſiaſtrologi errauerint hoc magis afcribit ignoratie astrologi in pſcien tia reizomittit enim inquifitor precauere borribilia anteqž ueniat a propter
translation: Because it is necessary for us to be solicitous about worldly things, the knowledge of future things is taken away from us. But if we were separated from all worldly things, we could prognosticate future things. For this reason, we see many hermits who predict future things. And some epileptics, while tormented by epilepsy, predict future things, because then they do not use bodily senses but only the power of the soul. Therefore, the philosopher, our father, does not say that a rich man is a philosopher, as the philosopher says in the seventh book of Politics. It is not necessary for a philosopher to be the lord of land and sea, but it is sufficient for him to have a servant who ministers vegetables to him. And thus, those who have the riches of this world are repelled from astronomy. Socrates, realizing this, threw gold into the sea, as Valerius narrates. This condition does not please many, nor even me. As for these conditions, Albumazar consoles us in his Introduction, and especially as to the last one, because he changes it to the opposite. He says thus: "And perhaps some are not sufficient for the wisdom of this mastery, while they look at certain things to arrive from truth and their comprehension, so that they can repel error from themselves due to the brevity of their exercise in the knowledge of all things, the knowledge of which is necessary for them in that same intention, as to the first conditions." And after a few intervening words, he says: "And subtle men and those of profound wisdom, perhaps many of those who use them are not sufficient to comprehend them, since the science of this mastery will be the science of other masteries that are necessary in the presence of things due to the profundity that we have said is far from the senses, as to the second condition." Then he consoles us and says: "That which can be attained from its science should not be annulled because of that which cannot be attained." And after a few intervening words, he says that a little wisdom is of much profit. And in this, he changes the third condition to the opposite. And he proves that there is greater utility in astronomy than in any other science. And if error occurs in it, it is more tolerable and less harmful than in other sciences. And he says thus: "For we see physicians err in the prescience of infirmities and diseases, while they predict what happens to the sick from the gravity of the disease and its lightness, and the swiftness of health and its slowness, and also their health or death. But that little error of physicians does not prevent them from the pursuit of their mastery and from its disposition and exercise, nor does it generate blame or doubt among men towards them, who have sought utility and profit from a little error in their mastery through their cure and acquiesce to their medicines. Similarly, shipmasters do not abandon their mastery, nor do men abandon the course of the sea because of a little error of sailors. Similarly, the authors of other wisdoms and masteries can hardly escape the accidents of error or the advent of some impediment that happens to them; but because of this, their mastery is not annulled. And among all these things that we have said, the error of impediment is greater than the error of astrologers, because when a physician errs in the prescience of infirmities, or cure, or in medicines, perhaps this will be the cause of the perdition of souls. And when sailors err, perhaps this will be the cause of the perdition of ships and their shipwreck and the death of those who are in them. And if shepherds and the authors of animal deception err, perhaps this will be the provocation of the perdition of that same kind of animals. But if astrologers err, this is more attributed to the ignorance of the astrologer in the prescience of things, for the inquirer omits to guard against horrible things before they come and because of

author: Alchabitius
title: The Dignity of Astrology
text: hoc fortaffis erit illis dimifſio cautele puocatio eorüdem bonibílium adue nientium z fortaſsis erit in eis perditio z fortaſsis no. In ceteris autem magi steriis nó eſt ita:qı maxima po erroris comm auctozum est perditio z impe dimentum absqs dubio. Et in hoc quoq qo diximus dignitas z dignitatib scientie astrozum:fi ergo est magisteriuz astrozű dignius ac nobilius ceteris magisterijs:z ſemita eius magisterijin errore in quibufdam horis est infra ſemitam ceterorū magisteriozum 7 cox errore sanior z minus impedimente: eoz veritas est maiozis pficui: quare equum est quantum decet oés hoies intellectuales z sapiêtes recipere astrologos z vti eis z acquieſcere veritati eorum in his que dicüt. Hec aut scientia maxime illa pars que est de iudicijs multos habet emulos z aduerfarios. Quod aduertés Ptolemeus in qua dripartito ſuo cófirmat iudicia astrozű z Albumazar in magno introductor rio ſuo:z conueniût ambo in modo cófirmádí:nifi & Albumazar plura po nit qz Ptolemeus 7 pluribus vtitur rónibus. Ambo in confirmando iudi cia incipiunt ab opere folis. Dicit enim Ptolemeus in tredecima propoſi tione prime partis. Quonía sol cum aere operat in rebus omnibus exiſten tibus in terra. Et Paly in comento exponens verba Ptolemei dicit Ptoles meus vult nobis oftédere q sphera ignis z aeris que mutatur per corpora celestia mutat res oés que sunt in terris. Et Paly in comento secunde pposi tionis eiufdem dicit q radices huius sciétie fut adeo maifeſte q populares nihil ſciéntes sciunt z intelligút eas inſpiciendo z experiêdo ipsas. Et proles meus in.2i, in ppoſitione dicit q neſcij populares sciút res anteqz accidát. Et q magis est dicit ipse q aialia muta sciunt res anteqz accidant. Albumas zar etiá confirmat scientiam iudiciorú per multas ratiões quas núc omnes narrare effet valde longű. Specialiter aut Albumazar dispatat cótra nega tes iudicia aſtrop z diuidit ipsos in.io.sectas. Et tangá breuiter de qualibet secta duo aut tria verba. Prima secta dicit q planetis no eét aliqua signifi icatio supres a fiat in hoc múdo q eſt ſub circulo lune. Quibus dicit Albu mazar q oés phi cócordati sunt q ois ſubſtantia q mouet motu naturali effi cit in essentijs rerum fibi cóiunctax per naturá puerſiones naturales:z hoc Declarat in actione ignis. Sed corpoza celestia mouentur motu naturali:i6 efficiunt in qttuor elementis fibi coniunctis per naturam conuerfiones nas turales. Danc rónem innuit Albumazar in multisverbis. Posset ét assumi illam sectam dictum Ariftotelis primo metberorum. Necesse est mundú in feriozem contiguú esſe lationib superioribº:vt tota eius virtus inde guber netur. Secúda secta est eorú qui dixerüt q planetis essent significationes tantú super res vniuerſales:vt sunt quattuor eleméta:z sunt genera z specia es.Contra quos Albumazarponit inter alia talem rónem. Totum non est totum:nisi per partes fuas:ergo si significant super totum scilicet super spe ciem oportet q significent super indiuidua que sunt partes specierú.Ter tia secta est quorúdam disputatium qui dixerüt qº planetis no esset signifi icatio super poffibile:sed solum sup necessarium z impossibile. Et dicit Albu mazar q dissoluit rõnes istopphilofophus:z affirmauit possibile per mul tas ratiões. Et inter alias ponit illam quá ponit Aristoteles in primo periars dij menias.ſ.quod no oportet confiliari vel auxiliari. Quarta secta est quorū dam qui aspexerunt in scia totius que est sciétia circulorú z esse eorú. Iſti di xeruntq planetis non esset significatioſuper res que fiút z eueniunt’ in hoc mundo er indiuiduis aialium z seminum ac metallorú.Etq eorú significa tio eet sup corruptione vel mutationé tépoy tátú. Et dicit Albumazar q iſti nequérút negare hanc quatitate.ſ. mutationé tépozú eé ex fortitudine opis planetarú qe hoc ex eorú ope iueniť z apparet z ei? repulsio est dedecus.Et postea arguit cótra eos ex cóceſsis. Quinta secta est eorú qui aspexerút in scientia totius z anulauerit sciam iudicioz ex pte experimétori. Et dixerunt qiudicia astronomie no sunt vera qe res ptingunt per experiméta z minus pôt pcipi veritas rei per expiméta est dum iuenit hisin vno eé.Et hoc est ipossibile in planetis:qr planeta cú fuerit in aliquo loco ex signis z fuerint ce teri planete aspicientes ipm vel cõiuncti ei nó reuertunt ad ipsu:ex ex signis ni si post mita milia annoz z nó puenit vita vni hois ad tátá qtitaté anoz quo mó ergo est poſsibile hoi vt iueniat planetas bis sup vnū ee ötenus pbetex reditide eorú ad loca eop qd significet. Quibus dicit Albumazar q antig no uerút planetarú nãos z eorú significationes ex multis rebz z diuersis quaruz qdá sunt priculares apparentes:qdá vo vniuersales. Particulares appa réntes sunt quéadmodú iuenit ex opone solis in calefactione z lune:in humi ditate z putredine:z ex operib? planetay in corruptione z mutatione aeris in oi die ac nocte. Universales do quéadmodú iueniť ex eorú significatione in reuolutide annoz múdi z natiuitatú.Et breuiter ftus vborn'est q hó in mos dicotpe pôt experiri illa que coiter occurrút:sed quátú ad alia que raro fiút: sic sunt pinctiões magne debet nobis sufficiere dicta pdeceſſor noſtroz.Sexta secta est qūda aritmethicox qui pigritabat aspicere numerű stellay z equas tioné ipsay ex libro in quo est scia totius certissime.i.almagesti quem Ptoles meus edidit.sed qsifuerút loca planetarű per diuerfas tabulas 7 iuenerút ta bulas differre quátú ad loca planetay.ió dixerunt q iudicia no sunt vera ex quo nó habent vera loca planetay. Quib? dicit albumazar q astronomus cú de aliq reuoluerit indicare in hoc debet cófidere vt aspicat naturas plas netarŭ ac pprietates eorú a dim signi vniufcuiusq; eorú 7 ſue exaltatióis atq oños triplicitatis fue.locum quos eius ex angulo z succedétibus eius a cas ſu eius ab angulo 7 pitia eius in domo substiſtie z domo fratrumz ceteris domibus circuli z postea iudicet. Gradibus vero signop est significatio pricu laris qua vtunt astrologi in quibusda rebus pprijs. Si aut fuerit in locopla nete erros ex minutis vel gradu vno nó impedít hoc magiſterium iudiciop.Et oponet euz qui exercet magisteriú iudiciop cú dederit ei calculator loca pla netaz ex signis in abusda tpibus vt iudicet per ipsa loca in qbus sunt planes te si significent illud velillud. Certitudo vero gradus in quo loco sint:ex si gnis suis secundum veritaté erit sup calculatores z ab eis exiget. Et ponit exemplum de medicis dicés: medicus tenet iudicare naturam vniuscuiusq; medicaminis 7 cui rei sit vtilis z qbus infirmitatib psiciat. Inquisitio vero ſpes cierū in regionibus z carú tritura a medicis nó est exigéda:sed ab auctoubo ſpecier. Ita est dicit ipse de iudicijs astroy. Ad illuz qui exercetindicia perti net solum defendere q planete significant sup res huius mundi:z qd signifi cent:z pertinet ad ipsum scire pprietates z naturas ipsorum.Ad calculato rem vero pertinet verificare gradus eor. Septima secta est eorú q repule runt hanc scientiam eo q aspexissent in ea z impossibile fuit eos pùenire ad id quod voluerúnt.Suntqinuidentes huius magisterij sectatores ſup scié ntia cop in ipsa:z repellunt scientiaz magisterij iudiciop ppter inuidiaz. Iſtis non est cótradicendú vt dicit Albumazar cú sit eorú negatio ad hoc magiste rium. Nam negator no debet constringi:nisi per qo vincatur a cogat venire ad veritaté pstaté. Octaua secta est quorúda qui se iactat de medicinali sci entia:vt acgrant per eam.non aut medici pbi z sapiétes in magisterio medis cine q iam legerut libros antiquorum in scientia medicine ac ſciuerút radis ces magisterij cop 7 diuerfitate elemétop seu tempor ac naturay:corruptio nem quos cop z cetera que fut eis necessaria in magisterio eop ex rebus sin gularibus z rebus compositis. Isti nouerunt dignitates sciétie iudicioum astrop. Et sciunt q sciétia astrorum sit principium scientie medicine. Et utú tur magisterio iudicioq in magisterio medicine vsu ferequenti in cognitióe morborum z augumétatione eorú z diminutione z in temporibus curatídis. Multiplicaturqs eorum recta intétio in magisterio eop: liberatur infirmi per manus copiac multipli
translation: This perhaps will be a dismissal of caution, a provocation of those good things to come, and perhaps there will be loss in them, and perhaps not. In other arts, however, it is not so, because the greatest error of authors is loss and impediment without doubt. And in this, too, what we have said is the dignity and the dignities of the science of the stars. If, therefore, the art of the stars is more dignified and nobler than other arts, and the path of its art in error at certain times is below the path of other arts, and their error is sounder and less impeded, their truth is of greater profit. Therefore, it is right that all intellectual and wise people should receive astrologers and use them and acquiesce to their truth in what they say. This science, especially that part which is about judgments, has many rivals and adversaries. Ptolemy, noticing this in his Quadripartitum, confirms the judgments of the stars, and Albumasar in his great Introductorium. And both agree in the method of confirmation, except that Albumasar posits more than Ptolemy and uses more reasons. Both, in confirming judgments, begin with the operation of the sun. For Ptolemy says in the thirteenth proposition of the first part: "Because the sun operates with the air in all things existing on earth." And Paley, in his commentary, explaining Ptolemy's words, says: "Ptolemy wants to show us that the sphere of fire and air, which is changed by celestial bodies, changes all things that are on earth." And Paley, in his commentary on the second proposition of the same, says that the roots of this science are so manifest that common people, knowing nothing, know and understand them by observing and experiencing them. And Ptolemy, in the 21st proposition, says that ignorant common people know things before they happen. And what is more, he says that mute animals know things before they happen. Albumasar also confirms the science of judgments by many reasons, which it would be very long to narrate all now. Specifically, however, Albumasar disputes against those who deny the judgments of the stars and divides them into ten sects. And I will briefly touch on two or three words about each sect. The first sect says that the planets have no significance over things that happen in this world, which is below the circle of the moon. To these, Albumasar says that all philosophers agree that every substance that moves by natural motion produces natural changes in the essences of things connected to it by nature. And he declares this in the action of fire. But celestial bodies move by natural motion; therefore, they produce natural changes in the four elements connected to them by nature. Albumasar alludes to this reason in many words. The saying of Aristotle in the first book of Meteorology could also be assumed for that sect: "It is necessary for the inferior world to be contiguous to the superior motions, so that all its power may be governed from there." The second sect is of those who said that the planets have significations only over universal things, such as the four elements, and are genera and species. Against these, Albumasar puts forth, among other things, such a reason: "The whole is not the whole except through its parts; therefore, if they signify over the whole, namely over the species, it is necessary that they signify over the individuals which are parts of the species." The third sect is of certain disputants who said that the planets have no signification over the possible, but only over the necessary and the impossible. And Albumasar says that the philosopher dissolved the reasons of these and affirmed the possible by many reasons. And among others, he posits that which Aristotle posits in the first book of Peri Hermeneias, namely, that one should not deliberate or assist. The fourth sect is of some who looked into the science of the whole, which is the science of circles and their being. These said that the planets have no signification over things that happen and occur in this world from individuals of animals and seeds and metals. And that their signification is only over the corruption or change of times. And Albumasar says that these could not deny this quantity, namely, the change of times, to be from the strength of the work of the planets, because this is found and appears from their work, and its repulsion is a disgrace. And afterwards he argues against them from concessions. The fifth sect is of those who looked into the science of the whole and annulled the science of judgments from the part of experiments. And they said that the judgments of astronomy are not true because things happen by experiments, and the truth of a thing cannot be perceived by experiments unless it is found to be twice in one. And this is impossible in the planets, because when a planet is in some place among the signs, and the other planets are aspecting it or conjoined to it, they do not return to it from the signs except after many thousands of years, and the life of one man does not reach such a quantity of years. How then is it possible for a man to find the planets twice in one place so that he may prove from their return to their places what they signify? To these, Albumasar says that the ancients knew the natures of the planets and their significations from many and diverse things, some of which are particular and apparent, others universal. Particular apparent things are, for example, what is found from the opposition of the sun in heating and the moon in humidity and putrefaction, and from the works of the planets in the corruption and change of the air every day and night. Universal things are, for example, what is found from their signification in the revolutions of the years of the world and nativities. And briefly, the meaning of these words is that man in a short time can experience those things that commonly occur; but as for other things that rarely happen, such as great conjunctions, the sayings of our predecessors should suffice us. The sixth sect is of some arithmeticians who were lazy to look at the number of the stars and their equation from the book in which the science of the whole is most certain, namely, the Almagest, which Ptolemy published. But they sought the places of the planets by diverse tables and found the tables to differ as to the places of the planets. Therefore, they said that the judgments are not true since they do not have the true places of the planets. To these, Albumasar says that when an astrologer is about to make a judgment, he must rely on looking at the natures of the planets and their properties from the degree of each sign and its exaltation and the lords of its triplicity, and its place from the angle and its succedents and its fall from the angle and its presence in the house of substance and the house of brothers and the other houses of the circle, and then he judges. The degrees of the signs, however, have a particular signification which astrologers use in certain specific matters. But if there is an error in the place of a planet by minutes or one degree, this does not impede the art of judgments. And it is necessary for him who practices the art of judgments, when the calculator has given him the places of the planets from the signs at certain times, to judge by those places in which the planets are, whether they signify this or that. The certainty of the degree in which they are, from their signs according to truth, will be upon the calculators and will be demanded from them. And he gives an example of physicians, saying: "The physician must judge the nature of each medicine and for what it is useful and what diseases it benefits. But the inquiry into species in regions and their preparation is not to be demanded from physicians, but from the authors of species." So it is, he says, concerning the judgments of the stars. To him who practices judgments, it pertains only to defend that the planets signify over the things of this world, and what they signify; and it pertains to him to know their properties and natures. To the calculator, however, it pertains to verify their degrees. The seventh sect is of those who rejected this science because they looked into it and it was impossible for them to achieve what they wanted. And these are envious followers of this art over their knowledge in it, and they reject the science of the art of judgments because of envy. These are not to be contradicted, as Albumasar says, since their denial is for this art. For a denier should not be constrained except by that by which he is overcome and compelled to come to manifest truth. The eighth sect is of some who boast of medical science, so that they may acquire through it. Not, however, good and wise physicians in the art of medicine who have already read the books of the ancients in the science of medicine and have known the roots of their art and the diversity of elements or times and natures, and their corruption, and other things that are necessary for them in their art from simple and composite things. These have known the dignities of the science of the judgments of the stars. And they know that the science of the stars is the beginning of the science of medicine. And they use the art of judgments in the art of medicine almost frequently in the knowledge of diseases and their augmentation and diminution and in the times of healing. And their right intention in their art is multiplied, and the sick are freed by their hands and multiplied.

author: Alchabitius
title: The Ten Sects and Astrology's Divisions
text: cantur pfectus hominú per eos. Quidam uero ex medicis ex his videlicet quoy imperitia abundat:ratio auté abbreviatur in scientia quorūdam que necessaria sunt illis ad hoc contédunt in magistes rio suo vt preponát acquisitioné z postponát scientiam: deniq; córradixerút sciétie'iudicioyz dixerút q non sit motibus planetarum in hoc múdo for titudo.Contra quos Albumazar allegat dictum Dipocratis dicés. Et iaz di xit Dipocras in libro aeris dum métionem faceret de diuerfitate aeris z ele mentop seu naturarum o res quas diximus de diuerfitate aeris sunt in sci entia astrorum.Et q sciétia astrorum nó est modica pars scientie medicine. Nona secta é vniuersitatis vulgi. Et iſti diuiditur in duas sectas: qvna est cox qui nesciunt dignitatem iudiciorum ceterarūq; scientiarū neqz digni tatem presciétie rerum. digniors hominib apud eos est cui fuerit plus sub stantie. De iftis fuit prius dictú. Contra quos dicit Albumazar q substantia z fortuna aptátur sapienti z infipienti: forti quos debili:z homo no est lau dabilis super hoc qò aptat ei ex hoc qe hoc non est aptatum ei psciétia suaz neosp infipientia:neq; per fortitudinem seu debilitatez suaz sed laudat super sapientia z cognitóe:quia dignitas hominis sup cetera animalia non sit nisi p sapientiam z cognitionem z per cognitioné rerú que fuerunt z q future sur. Et quibusdam interpositis dicit q dignius rerum in homine est futurorum scientia. Decima secta est etia vniuersalis vulgi. Et isti repulerunt sciétiam huius magisterij propter hoc q viderût de multitudine erroris cox qui se iactant de ea:eo q vulgus recipit res p cognitioné apparentie. Lunqz vidif sent multitudine erroris cox qui se iactant de bac scíétia in his que interro gantur ex scientia iudiciorum astrozum médacem eam putates repulerunt: z referunt magistros huius sciétie ad insipientia z dixerunt q est scia caſſa. Diat Albumazar q iſti non sunt culpandi in contradictione magisteriorum d iij huius scientie:quia plurimi cox qui se iactant de ea sunt homines infipiétes z imperiti atq; caduci. Et quibufdam interpolitis dicit p iſti exercent sub nos mine huius magisterij varias seductiones quibus seducunt homines debis les vult dicere q quidam operantur geomaticam:vel per sortilegia sub no mine huius scientie. Iſti non querunt nisi lucrum. Et dicit cum iſti interrogá tur de aliqua retendunt in reſponſionem ad rem qua gratulatur querens: z sibi congruit volentes eum letificare causa cupiditatis sue. Dec sufficiat de decé sectis quas ponit Albumazar. Et posset addi vna decima secta. Et sunt illi qui dicunt q ista sciétia sit cótra fidem. Quibus dico q ipsi nó legerút li bros sapiétium antiquorum:quos si legissent scirent qec sciétia non est có tra fidem:imo p fide. Ponunt enim doctores huius sciétie mundum creatú quod est primu fundametuz fidei. Dicit enim Daly Albéragelin prima par te sui libri in capitulo de natura Jouis:q tempore quo deus incepit creare mundum posuit Jouem in ascendete. Et Albumazar in itroductozio suo di cit. Et dicamus primúg motus circuli sit a virtute prime cause. Et qbusdaz interpositis dicit. Ecce qualiter pertraximus creatorem ex rebus apparéti bus z notis que ptingútur sensibus q sit sempiternus habens virtuté absqz effentia finis imobilis z incorruptibilis altissimus sit nomé eius benedictum z exaltatú exaltatide maxima. De diuisione astronomie expedio me breui ter z pono diuisioné quá ponit Albumazar in itroductorio suo magno. Due sunt species astronomie:quarum vna est sciétia totius.f.de circulis z motib eorum.Secunda est ars iudiciorum astronomie. Hanc diuisionem ponit etiá Ptolemeus in principio quadripartiti sui. Et Daly in commentoidem. Pris ma species est tradita perfecte z cóplete quátum ad principia z cóclufiones a Ptolemeo in almagesti. Vel posset prima species subdiuidi. Sciétia de mo nibus est duplex:quedam est in inſtrumentalis:quedam tabularis. Illa inſtru mentalis est duplex:quonia quedam sunt inſtrumenta ad obseruandum siue inuestigandum motus:sicut sunt regule Ptolemei armille a confimilia.Alia sunt inſtruméta pro opationibus quottidianis exercédis sicut astrolabium Sphaera solida z similia.Secunda species.f.ars iudicioz astrologie hztttuoz partes pncipales.Quaz puma est de interrogatióibus.Scda de natiuitati bus. Tertia de reuolutioibus annoz:z hec est duplex.f.de revolutionib an norum mundiz de reuolutõib annoz natiuitatum. Quarta de electionib?. De iſitis quattuor ptibus Daly Abéragel fecit vnū librum completu. Proles meus aute:z q quadripartito omisit duas partes.f.de interrogationibus thos rarum electionibus sed Daly qui commétauit ipfum:excufat ipsum dicens: satis prit haberi ifte due ptes ex his q Ptolemeus dicit ibide.preter istas sut qdá alie ptes iudiciop.f.deiunctioib magnis:de imaginibus:de figillis:de qbus pau vel nihil habemus.Aduertétes aut plurimi antiquop z et moder nop pste pres essent difficiles ad intelligendú debétibus prio adiscere illa sciam fecerunt libros introductorios in qbus posuerunt principia z expo suerunt terminos qbus vtunt magistri iudicioz. Inter aut alios introducto rios liber Alchabicij est magis approbatus apud modernos. Ideo dimissis alijs de ipso ad presens intédimus. Dis uisis ad litteram descendamus. stulata a domino. In principio iſtius libri ſepte possit queri. pri: mo que sit intentio libri. Secundo que sit vtilitas. Tertio quis sit titulus libu. Quarto cui parti philosophie supponatur. Quito quando debet legi.Sexto de subiccto libri. Septimo de diuisio ne libri. Intentio libri est patefacere scientiam vniuscuiusq; rei cuius scien tia necessaria est incipienti adiscere scientiam iudiciorum astrorum. Vtilitas eius patet:quonia omnis qui vult scientia iudicior incipere doctrina huius libu sufficiens erit ei:quá si bene intellexerit poterit omnes libros iudicioru per se intelligere. Titulus huius libri talis est. Incipiunt ysagoge Alchabicij ad scientiam iudiciorum astrorum. Et debet legi ante omnem libri iudiciozú astrorum. De qua parte sit patet ex precedétibus. Est enim de parte scientie iudiciorum astrorú. Subiectum eius est illud quod est subiectú in tota astro nomia iudiciali.f.corpus celeste inquantum per motum z lumé causat genes rationem a corruptionem in rebus existentibus sub orbe lune. Diuisio libri patebit in processu. Diuiditur autem iste liber prima diuisioe in duas partes scilicet in partem proemialem:z executiuam. Pars executiua incipit ibi. In nomine Domini zodiacus z cetera. Pars executiua diuidit in quinqs partes secundum q diuidit auctor in littera quas vocat quinqs differétias fm q pa tebit statim. pars proemialis potest diuidi in qttuor partes quoniaz in pri ma inuocat diuina auxilium pro completione operis sui: z cum hoc captat beniuolétiam regis sui ad cuius instátiam forte hunc librum composuit. In secunda parte ostendit causam sui libri siue motus qui mouit eum ad facien dum hunc librum. In tertia parte excusat se de rationibus. In quarta parte ponit diuisioné sui libri. Scda ps icipit ibi:cū vidisset. Tertia ps incipit ibi: non introduxi. Quarta pars incipitibi. Et diuisi eum in quinqs differentias. Primo breuiter sicut homo fidelis petit a deo plixitatem vite:vt possit opus suum complere. Et petit durabilitatem honoza gladij regis z custodia opes rum suorum z ampliationem sui imperij. cum vidissem. In ista parte ponit causas que mouit ipsum ad faciendum hunc librú. Et dicit q cum vidisset multos antiquorum fecisse libros introductorios ad sciétiam iudiciop astro rum z quosdam ex eis nó posuisse omnia necessaria ad introductóes a quos dam nimis prolixe tradidisse ea que tradiderunt: inter cófusa dicta eorum perisse.i.obscurata esse illa que necessaria sunt ad introductionem.Et etiam quosdam non pressisse in dictis eorum ordine discipline preponendo illa que debent preponi:z posponendo illa que debent posponi: sed fecisse prius. Incepit edere hunc librum z collegit in eo ex dictis antiquop ea que erat ne cessaria ad introductionem:z inutilia omisit. et no introduxi hic excusat se de rónibus z dicit q non po suit in hoc libro rónes q essent
translation: perfected by them. Some physicians, however, whose inexperience abounds, and whose reason is abbreviated in the knowledge of certain things necessary to them for this purpose, contend in their mastery that they should prioritize acquisition and postpone knowledge. Finally, they contradicted the science of judgments and said that there is no strength in the motions of the planets in this world. Against them, Albumasar cites the saying of Hippocrates, saying: "And Hippocrates already said in the book of air, when he made mention of the diversity of air and elements or natures, that the things we have said about the diversity of air are in the science of the stars. And that the science of the stars is no small part of the science of medicine." The ninth sect is that of the common people. And these are divided into two sects: one is of those who do not know the dignity of judgments and other sciences, nor the dignity of foreknowledge of things. The most worthy among them is he who has more substance. Of these, it was said before. Against them, Albumasar says that substance and fortune are adapted to the wise and the foolish, to the strong and the weak. And man is not praiseworthy for what is adapted to him from this, because this is not adapted to him by his knowledge, nor by his foolishness, nor by his strength or weakness, but he is praised for wisdom and knowledge, because the dignity of man above other animals is only through wisdom and knowledge and through the knowledge of things that have been and that are to come. And with some interpolations, he says that the most worthy thing in man is the knowledge of future things. The tenth sect is also of the common people. And these rejected the science of this mastery because they saw the multitude of errors of those who boast about it, because the common people receive things by the knowledge of appearance. And when they saw the multitude of errors of those who boast about this science in those things that are asked from the science of the judgments of the stars, thinking it false, they rejected it. And they refer the masters of this science to foolishness and said that it is a vain science. Albumasar says that these are not to be blamed in contradicting the masteries of this science, because most of those who boast about it are foolish, inexperienced, and fallible men. And with some interpolations, he says that these practice under the name of this mastery various seductions by which they seduce weak men. He means to say that some practice geomancy or sortilege under the name of this science. These seek nothing but profit. And he says that when these are asked about something, they extend in their answer to the thing that the questioner desires, and it suits them, wanting to make him happy for the sake of their greed. Let this suffice concerning the ten sects that Albumasar presents. And an eleventh sect could be added. And these are those who say that this science is against faith. To whom I say that they have not read the books of the ancient wise men, which if they had read, they would know that this science is not against faith, but rather for faith. For the doctors of this science posit a created world, which is the first foundation of faith. For Daly Albéragel says in the first part of his book, in the chapter on the nature of Jupiter, that at the time when God began to create the world, he placed Jupiter in the ascendant. And Albumasar says in his introduction: "And let us say first that the motion of the circle is from the power of the first cause." And with some interpolations, he says: "Behold how we have drawn the creator from apparent and known things that are perceived by the senses, that he is eternal, having power without the essence of an end, immobile and incorruptible. May his name be blessed and exalted with the greatest exaltation." I will briefly explain the division of astronomy and present the division that Albumasar presents in his great introduction. There are two kinds of astronomy: one is the science of the whole, that is, of circles and their motions. The second is the art of astrological judgments. Ptolemy also presents this division at the beginning of his Quadripartitum. And Daly in his commentary. The first kind is transmitted perfectly and completely as to principles and conclusions by Ptolemy in the Almagest. Or the first kind could be subdivided. The science of motions is twofold: some is instrumental, some tabular. That instrumental is twofold: because some are instruments for observing or investigating motions, such as Ptolemy's rules, armillary spheres, and similar things. Others are instruments for performing daily operations, such as the astrolabe, solid sphere, and similar things. The second kind, that is, the art of astrological judgments, has four principal parts. The first of which is concerning interrogations. The second concerning nativities. The third concerning the revolutions of years: and this is twofold, that is, concerning the revolutions of the years of the world and concerning the revolutions of the years of nativities. The fourth concerning elections. Daly Abéragel made a complete book on these four parts. Ptolemy, however, in the Quadripartitum, omitted two parts, that is, concerning interrogations and elections. But Daly, who commented on him, excuses him, saying: "These two parts can be sufficiently obtained from what Ptolemy says there." Besides these, there are some other parts of judgments, that is, concerning great conjunctions, concerning images, concerning seals, of which we have little or nothing. However, many ancient and modern authors, perceiving that these things were difficult to understand for those who first had to learn those sciences, made introductory books in which they set forth the principles and explained the terms that the masters of judgments use. Among other introductions, Alchabitius's book is the most approved among modern authors. Therefore, leaving others aside, we intend to deal with it for the present. Let us descend to the letter. requested by the lord. At the beginning of this book, seven things can be asked. First, what is the intention of the book. Second, what is its utility. Third, what is the title of the book. Fourth, to which part of philosophy it is subordinated. Fifth, when it should be read. Sixth, concerning the subject of the book. Seventh, concerning the division of the book. The intention of the book is to make clear the science of every thing whose science is necessary for the beginner to learn the science of the judgments of the stars. Its utility is clear: because everyone who wants to begin the science of judgments, the teaching of this book will be sufficient for him; which if he understands well, he will be able to understand all books of judgments by himself. The title of this book is as follows: "Here begin the Isagoges of Alchabitius to the science of the judgments of the stars." And it should be read before every book of the judgments of the stars. From what part it is, is clear from the preceding. For it is part of the science of the judgments of the stars. Its subject is that which is the subject in all judicial astronomy, that is, the celestial body insofar as through motion and light it causes generation and corruption in things existing under the orb of the moon. The division of the book will become clear in the process. This book is divided by the first division into two parts, namely, into a proemial part and an executive part. The executive part begins there: "In the name of the Lord, the zodiac, etc." The executive part is divided into five parts according to how the author divides it in the text, which he calls five differences, as will immediately become clear. The proemial part can be divided into four parts, because in the first, he invokes divine aid for the completion of his work; and with this, he seeks the goodwill of his king, at whose instance he perhaps composed this book. In the second part, he shows the cause of his book or the motive that moved him to make this book. In the third part, he excuses himself for the reasons. In the fourth part, he presents the division of his book. The second part begins there: "When he saw." The third part begins there: "I have not introduced." The fourth part begins there: "And I divided it into five differences." First, briefly, as a faithful man, he asks God for a long life, so that he can complete his work. And he asks for the durability of the honors of the king's sword and the preservation of his works and the expansion of his empire. "When I saw." In this part, he presents the causes that moved him to make this book. And he says that when he saw many of the ancients make introductory books to the science of the judgments of the stars, and some of them did not include all things necessary for introductions, and some transmitted what they transmitted too prolixly, so that they were lost among their confused sayings, that is, those things necessary for the introduction were obscured. And also some did not press in their sayings the order of discipline, placing first what should be placed first, and placing last what should be placed last, but did it first. He began to publish this book and collected in it from the sayings of the ancients those things that were necessary for the introduction, and omitted the useless. And "I have not introduced" here he excuses himself for the reasons and says that he did not include in this book the reasons that would be

title: Ptolemy's Confirmation of Judgments
