Primo dicit circulus fignorum diuidit in duodecim pres eğles que vocant ſigna z no mínať nominib duodecim imaginú que funt in ipfo circulo fignopique fút ries taurus geminize. Et quodlibet fignú diuidik in triginta pres eğles que vocant gradus.Et quilibet gradus diuidit in fexaginta a vocant minuta.Et qolibet minutú in ſeragita z vocant.fecuda z qolibet.fecúdú in ſexagtia z vο cant tertia. 7 quodlibet tertiu in fexaginta z vocant örta.z Ac vfqs in ifinituz. Circa istam pté pit moueri duo dubia. Primú est de numero fignoy.Se cundum de ordine. De numero fignoy disputat Albumazar dicés. Quidam ex bis qui diuersa a nobis ſenſere cótradixerunt dictis antiquor in numes, ro fignop. Et dixerüt cur puſtatis & figna funt duodecim nec plus nec min?. Quibus refpondet Albumazar dicés: Aracis philofophus patefecit. 48. imagines que füt in circulo.i.in octaua Sphaera 7 earu nomia:z auctorizane runt eius ſniam oés ſapiétes antiq z prordauerút cum eo fup hoc. Et duo, decim ex his imaginib funt in circulo fignoy. Pac de ca dixerut & funt duo decim. Aliá cám aſſignat Albumazar dicens: philofophi iuenerunt quod oés res que fiunt a deftruunt in boc múdo funt cópoſite ex elemétis zomni indiuiduo q efficit ex quattuor elemétis ſunt tria ee.finitiú medium z finis. Quattuorigiť eleméta multiplicata per tria faciunt duodecim:igit figna füt Duodecim. Lertia ca eſt qe per motů folis fit generatio 7 corruptio in rebin ferioubt eius opus plus appet in boc mudo of opus aliori planetari.Et ipſe rotať in bis duodecim imaginib z funt ei locus, Et Fm decurfuz eius in bis duodecim imaginib? variant tépoza anni. Jdeo philoſophi pofuerunt duodecim imagines digniores fignificatióe. Et attribuerüt eis fignificatióez vniuerfalé. Leteris vero triginta ſex imaginib attribuerut fignificationemp priá. Secuda dubitatio é de ordie z de pncipio. Aligs eni poffet dicere öre iceperűt ab ariete cum circulus nõ béat pncipiú nes fine. Ad hoc dicit pro lemeus in.156.ppofitide pme ptis ädriptiti:gió est pncipiű ab ariete qe eſt fi gnú masculínu:z agés eft digni pallo z egnoctialis trafit per ipm: 2 abinde Lab eqnoctiali icipit maior mutatio toti firmaméti. Sed aligs diceret per isita rónem videt o initiú debeat eé a libraiqu est fignú masculínuz egnoctis lis tráfit per ipm z maior mot firmaméti eſt ibi.qre ergo inceperut ab aries te potius qž a libra. Pot folui per dictu-ptolemelin.i40.ppoſitiée pme ptis vbi dicit q pncipiu in firmaméto eſt ille loc in quo coplexio tpis est humidis or ficut pima etas aialiŭ eſt in qua cõplexio eft humidior. poffet aliter dici qu ab ariete incipit generatio:a libra vero corruptio. De ordine diceret alis quis öre pofuerut taurum poft arieté:z deinde gemini:poftea cácrum.Dicit Albumazar q öttuor funt õlitates fimplices elemétoruz.f.caliditas ficcitas bumiditas 7 frigiditas. Et due ex iftis funt ficut agentia.f.caliditas z frigidi, tas z alie due funt ficut patiétia.f.ficcitas z humiditas.Agentia auté funt di gniora patiétibideo incieperut ab agentibus. Et agentium vnus eft pncipis um generationis.f.caliditas: aliud pncipiú corruptionis ſcilicet frigiditas. Res auté prius generať o corrúpiťió pofuerunt arietem in pncipio quod est fignum calidum z ficcum de natura ignis qui eft pmo calidus.Et poſuess runt cancrum in fine quod eſt ſignú frigidum z humidú de natura aque que eft pmo frigida. Et pofuerunt taurum poft arieté:quod est fignú frigidum z ficcum de natura terre qua eſt pmo ficca:qa ficcitas terre cóueniés est calozi ignis. Et pofuerunt fignú geminori ante cácrum qe eſt ſignum calidú z bus midum de na aeris qui est pmo humidus:z bumiditas aeris eft pueniés bu miditati aque. translation: It is necessary to defend what I have said: that these positions are in Ptolemy's book, without quadripartition. For there Ptolemy confirms judgments by many reasons. And he says that he made a book on the confirmation of judgments where he placed sufficient reasons for this. d iiij. Here he sets out the division of his book. And he says, 'And I divided it into these differences, as is clear from reading the letters.' The names of the lords. The zodiac. Indeed, the treatise begins when finished. And it can be divided into five parts, which are five differences according to how the author divides it in the letter. The first difference is about the essential and accidental being of the circle of signs. The second is about the natures of the seven planets. The third is what happens to the seven planets. The fourth is about explaining the terms or names that the masters of judgments use. The fifth is about the projection of parts. The second difference begins there: 'and with the help of God.' The third there: 'For the being of the planets.' The fourth there: 'The beginning of these.' The fifth there: 'and when I have noted.' The first part is divided into two, because in the first part he explains the essential being of the circle of signs. Secondly, about the accidental being. The second there. 'For the circle is formed at every hour.' The first part can be divided into four parts. In the first, he determines the division of the circle of signs and those things that follow that division. In the second part, he determines the dignities of the planets in the signs. In the third, about the figures of the signs. In the fourth, what the signs have from the limbs of man and what they have from regions and plants. The second part begins there: 'which the planets have.' The third there: 'these figures of the signs follow.' The fourth there: 'and one which.' The first part can be divided into five. In the first part, he sets out the division of the circle of signs. In the second part, he compares them to each other. In the third, he compares them to the four seasons of the year, and to the four ages, and to the four humors. In the fourth, he sets out the order of the planets and explains what he means when he says 'a planet is in a sign.' In the fifth, he adds something about the head and tail of the dragon. The second begins there: 'and six of these signs are northern.' The third there: 'and he calls them the fourth.' The fourth there: 'but to all planets.' The fifth there: 'it also signifies by the head and tail.' First, he says the circle of signs is divided into twelve equal parts which are called signs and are named by the names of the twelve images that are in the circle of signs, which are Aries, Taurus, Gemini, etc. And each sign is divided into thirty equal parts which are called degrees. And each degree is divided into sixty, which are called minutes. And each minute into sixty, which are called seconds. And each second into sixty, which are called thirds. And each third into sixty, which are called fourths. And so on to infinity. Around this part, two doubts can be raised. The first is about the number of signs. The second is about the order. Albumazar disputes about the number of signs, saying: 'Some of those who thought differently from us contradicted the sayings of the ancients regarding the number of signs. And they said, 'Why do you suppose that there are twelve signs, neither more nor less?'' To these, Albumazar responds, saying: 'The philosopher Aratus revealed 48 images that are in the circle, i.e., in the eighth sphere, and their names. And all the ancient sages authorized his opinion and agreed with him on this. And twelve of these images are in the circle of signs. Therefore, they said there are twelve.' Albumazar assigns another reason, saying: 'Philosophers found that all things that are made and destroyed in this world are composed of elements, and every individual that is made from the four elements has three beings: beginning, middle, and end. Therefore, the four elements multiplied by three make twelve; therefore, there are twelve signs.' The third reason is that by the motion of the sun, generation and corruption occur in things. Its work is more desired in this world than the work of other planets. And it rotates in these twelve images, and they are its place. And according to its course in these twelve images, the seasons of the year vary. Therefore, philosophers placed twelve images as more worthy of signification. And they attributed universal signification to them. To the other thirty-six images, they attributed particular signification. The second doubt is about the order and the beginning. For someone might say, 'Why did they begin with Aries, since a circle has neither beginning nor end?' To this, Ptolemy says in the 156th proposition of the first part of the Quadripartite: 'The reason for beginning with Aries is that it is a masculine sign, and the agent is more worthy than the patient, and the equinoctial passes through it, and from there, with the equinoctial, the greatest change of the whole firmament begins.' But someone might say by this reason that the beginning should be from Libra, which is a masculine sign, the equinoctial passes through it, and the greatest motion of the firmament is there. Why then did they begin with Aries rather than Libra? It can be resolved by Ptolemy's statement in the 140th proposition of the first part, where he says that the beginning in the firmament is that place where the complexion of the time is more humid, just as the first age of animals is when the complexion is more humid. It could also be said that generation begins with Aries, but corruption with Libra. Regarding the order, someone might ask why they placed Taurus after Aries, and then Gemini, then Cancer. Albumazar says that there are four simple qualities of the elements, namely, heat, dryness, humidity, and coldness. And two of these are like agents, namely, heat and coldness, and the other two are like patients, namely, dryness and humidity. Agents are more worthy than patients, therefore they began with agents. And of the agents, one is the principle of generation, namely, heat; the other is the principle of corruption, namely, coldness. But a thing is generated before it is corrupted, therefore they placed Aries at the beginning, which is a hot and dry sign of the nature of fire, which is primarily hot. And they placed Cancer at the end, which is a cold and humid sign of the nature of water, which is primarily cold. And they placed Taurus after Aries, which is a cold and dry sign of the nature of earth, which is primarily dry, because the dryness of earth is suitable for the heat of fire. And they placed the sign of Gemini before Cancer, which is a hot and humid sign of the nature of air, which is primarily humid, and the humidity of air is suitable for the humidity of water. For the clarification of the aforesaid, it is to be understood that in the eighth sphere there are forty-eight images, in which there is agreement among all the ancients, as he said. And in them there are 1022 stars which are called fixed stars and all move with one motion, namely, with the motion of the eighth sphere, about one degree in a hundred years. And the ancients placed them in six orders, so that those that are larger they placed in the first order, and there are eleven. And those that are smaller than these they placed in the second order, and there are 45. And those that are smaller than these they placed in the third order, and there are 208. And those that are smaller than these they placed in the fourth order, and there are 474. And those that are smaller than these they placed in the fifth order, and there are 217. And those that are smaller than these they placed in the sixth order, and there are sixty-three. Of these 48 images, 12 are in the path of the sun, as has been said, and there are 346 stars in them. The names of these images are Aries, Taurus, etc. And there remain 36 images, of which 21 decline from the path of the sun to the north and 15 to the south. Those that decline to the north have 360 of the aforesaid stars. And the names of the images are these: The first is Ursa Minor. The second is Ursa Major. The third is Draco. The fourth is the burning or blazing dog. The fifth is the barking or howling dog.

author: Alchabitius
title: Northern and Southern Constellations
text: Sexta corona ſeptetrionalis Septima icuruat vel genuflexus.Octane vul tur cadés Nona gallina.Decima fedés ſup fedé Undecima deferés caput algol.i.diaboli.Duodecima tenés babenas.iz ferpétari.i4.ferpes.is.amifa tor.i6.aquila volás Alius liber bét vultur volás.17.delphin.is.equus pris mº. Alius liber bet caput eq.ig.equ? fecúdus.Ali liber bét equus alat2.20. mulier que no nouit viru:2i.eft triagulus. Que vo declinant ad meridié bit ex ftellis fupradictis.zi6.örum imaginus nomina funt bec. Prima caruz est marin leo qué qdá vrſum appellat.2.algebar.i.canis validus.3.flume.4.le pus.5.canis maior.6.canis mino2.7.archa noe.8.ferpes.9.vas.io.com.ii. Deferés leoné z ipſius medietas est humane figure & altera equi.iz.leopars. dus.iz.tburibula.i4.cozona meridionalis.is.pifcis meridianus. Etſex exbis fignis ita precat auctor figna adin uice. Et didi i tres ptes Primo copat ea itu ad pres müdi:vel istú ad decliatides Secúdo oštű ad afcéfio nes Lertio oštű ad qualitates. Prima in pncipio. Secúda ibi:z fex ex illis dis cunt directe afcedétia. Zertia ibi:z vocat medietas. Primo dicit g ſex ex pie dictis.i2.fignis funt feptétrionalia:qa declinat a linea equinoctiali verſus po luz ſeptétrionale.f.a puncipio arietis vios ad fine virginis. Et alia sex.f.a prin cipio libre vfqz ad finé piſciú funt meridionalia:q: declinantab eqnoctiali li nea verfus polum meridionale. Juxta quá prem eſt itelligédum gegnoctia lis eft circulus qué defcribit ſol raptu firmaméti.f. per motum diurnum qn eſt in pmo puncio arietis 7 eundez defcribit in primo puncto libre. Jdeo vos catur a Ptolemeo equator Diei z noctis:qe tunc tpis dies z nox sunt eiusdes longitudinis per vniuerfum maduz ubi fol ontur 7 occidit. Doc ideo dico q duo funt loca in mudo ſup que ſol no oritur nec occidit qui est in in arietis vel libre quop vn é fub polo arctico z alter fub polo átarctico.bic poffet moueri illa qitio. Utrú fub egnoctiali fit babitatio. Uidet e non. Na vbi eſt ppetua caliditas ibi no eſt babitatio:fed fub eqnoctiali est perpetua calidis tas.fol enim femp decurrit ſuper illa ptem z modicum declinat ab illa. Item auctor ſphaere dicit e guae funt 30ne in celo.quay vna eſt torrida zona til la fecundum en eſt ſub egnoctiali z ex vtraqs pre vios ad tropicos 7 due sub polis z due medie. Jbi vult quod tres earú funt inhabitabiles fcilicet torida 30na ppter nimiŭ calové:z due exiftétes ſub polis ppter nimiŭ frigue 2 alie due im eum funt habitabiles qe temperate. Breuiter ego credo cu Auicena gſub eqnoctiali fit babitatio z optima habitatio Et breuiter pono vnam ró nem que mouit me. Jbi eſt optima babitatio vbi eſt maxima temperies:fed fub eqnoctiali eft maxima temperies:ergo zć. Maior est nota. Minoré des claro fub eqnoctiali enim dies inox sunt eğles ficut dictum eft:z tatum pót infrigidari aer in nocte ficut calefit in die. Item declinatio folis maxime varia tur ibi Mutat enim declinatio in vno die per 24.minuta io forte vix potac cidere qo ſol tráfeat directe fupra capita eoru qi boc no accidit nifi fol intras ret in arietis vel libre recte in meridie. Jté ſol nunqž manet in eadé altitu dine fup ipfos:fed statim in iſtati si eét mot in iſtati variak altitudo. Ita eniz velociter ficut pmá mobile mouek accedit ad meridiem z recedit a meridic. Et vbi debet fieri magna calefactio opz qo radij infigant per aliqo tempust ficut videm in fpeculo ardeti fi mouerer ſpeculum nó icederet festucas vel alia ré que opponeret. Item planete frigidi refrenat ibi caliditate calidop:q2 oés femp funt ibi ppinqui quis qda dicat o planete no caufant frigiditatez fed eop frigiditas eft calor minor. In veritate boc no credo falua gra illorü. Uidi enim qo eadé că frigoris no facit tátu frigus in ptib multú feptétriona lib? quátú facit in minus feptétrionalibus. poffet etiá pbari róne. Si cétita nullus planeta influeret frigiditatem fed caret caliditate fequeret o prens tia, Saturni in eftate eftas fieret calido:q2 tale additu tali:facit ipz magis tale: fed hoc no videm quare zi. Et si diceret aligs & minor caliditas ſua téperat maioré caliditaté folis:ficut aqua tepida calida. Poc no valet:qz ibi fit mixtio materialis.in influétijs aút celestibus fit mixtio virtualis.Et est imagináduz in influentijs de maiori calore z minori fić de duob ignib calefacietib vna ollam quoi vnus eét fortior alio. Lertú est quod minor ignis no ifrigidabit ollam nec impediet fortioré igné in calefactive ſua. Rones in oppoſité funt folute: quima dicit op eſt ibi ppetua caliditas.Dico quod no eſtibi ppetua caliditas que impediat bitationem.Auctorem fphaere nego in propofito quantum ad hoc. Et fex ex illis dicitur directe afcedentia. poffet diuidi ſed no eſt vis. Dicit breuiter q8.6.figna ex fignis pdictis afcens dut recte.z.6.oblique. Recte afcedunt a pncipio cácri vios ad finé ſagittarij. Alia.6.afcedunt oblique. Ifta babent ex tractatu de ſphaera ió no est hic ſta dú. Poftea dicit o totuoſe ſeu oblique aſcendentia obediút recte aſcédétib Jurta qo notádů o si duonaſcant in abuſda tpibus in qbus oés alie ſignifis cationes funt pars qitum ad radice ſupiorem z ét inferioze:fed vans comm nafcat fub figno directe afcedente a alter fub figno oblique vel totuofe afce dente ille qui natus fuerit fub obliquo obediet alteri qui natus fuerit fub fis gno, directo dum illa figna funt eiufdé longitudinis a pncipio cancri.Uerbi gratia:ille qui natus fuerit fub geminis obediet ei qui natus fuerit fub cans cro:z ille qui fub tauro ei qui fub leone.z ille qui fub ariete ei qui fub virgine z fic de alijs. Poftea dicit quod quelibet duo figna que sunt eiufdem longi tudinis a principio arietis dicuntur concordantia:ficut pifces z aries aqua rius z taurus capricomus z geminiz fic de alijs. Et poteft applicari ficut pri us. Et hoc est ideo quia aſcenſiones illoum fignorum funt equales.Et boe eſt quod dicit auetor tractatus de fphaera: quelibz duo figna equaliter dista tia ab alterutro punctorum equinoctialium adequantur in fuis afcenfionis bus. Poftea dicit quod medietas circuli que eft a principio leonis vſqz in fis nem capricomi vocať medieras maxima ppter afcenfiones. Et eft medietas folis:qrleo eft domus folis z afcenfiones incipiunt ibi notabiliter augmen tari. Et fol bet in tota illa medietate dignitatez ficut planete in terminis fuis. Et alia medietas eft lune.f.a pncipio aquarij.ufos ad fine cancri:z illa vocat medietas minima co o afcenfiones notabiliter icipiüt ibi nominari. Et lunge bétibi dignitatem qua fol babet in maxima.dedesupend
translation: The sixth is the northern crown. The seventh is the kneeling or genuflecting one. The eighth is the falling vulture. The ninth is the hen. The tenth is the seated one upon a seat. The eleventh is Algol, that is, the devil's head. The twelfth is the one holding the reins. The thirteenth is the serpent bearer. The fourteenth is the serpent. The fifteenth is the hunter. The sixteenth is the flying eagle. Another book has the flying vulture. The seventeenth is the dolphin. The eighteenth is the first horse. Another book has the horse's head. The nineteenth is the second horse. Another book has the winged horse. The twentieth is the woman who has not known a man. The twenty-first is the triangle. Those that decline to the south from the aforementioned stars are 216, whose imaginary names are these: The first of these is the sea lion, which some call the bear. The second is Algebar, that is, the strong dog. The third is the river. The fourth is the hare. The fifth is Canis Major. The sixth is Canis Minor. The seventh is Noah's Ark. The eighth is the serpent. The ninth is the vase. The tenth is the comb. The eleventh is the one bearing the lion, and its middle part is of human figure and the other of a horse. The twelfth is the leopard. The thirteenth is the thuribles. The fourteenth is the southern crown. The fifteenth is the southern fish. And six of these signs are thus prayed by the author to the signs in turn. And I divided them into three parts. First, I compare them to the parts of the world, or this to the declinations. Second, I show them to the ascensions. Third, I show them to the qualities. The first is at the beginning. The second is there: and six of them are said to ascend directly. The third is there: and it is called the middle. First, it says that six of the aforementioned 12 signs are northern, because they decline from the equinoctial line towards the northern pole, that is, from the beginning of Aries to the end of Virgo. And the other six, that is, from the beginning of Libra to the end of Pisces, are southern, because they decline from the equinoctial line towards the southern pole. According to this part, it is to be understood that the equinoctial is the circle that the sun describes by the rapture of the firmament, that is, by diurnal motion when it is in the first point of Aries, and it describes the same in the first point of Libra. Therefore, it is called by Ptolemy the equator of day and night, because at that time day and night are of the same length throughout the world where the sun rises and sets. I say this because there are two places in the world above which the sun neither rises nor sets, which is in Aries or Libra, one of which is under the arctic pole and the other under the antarctic pole. Here that question could be moved: whether there is habitation under the equator. It seems not. For where there is perpetual heat, there is no habitation; but under the equator there is perpetual heat. For the sun always runs over that part and declines little from it. Also, the author of the sphere says that there are five zones in the sky, one of which is the torrid zone, that is, according to him, under the equator and from both sides up to the tropics, and two under the poles and two temperate. There he wants three of them to be uninhabitable, namely the torrid zone because of excessive heat, and the two existing under the poles because of excessive cold, and the other two are habitable because they are temperate. Briefly, I believe with Avicenna that there is habitation under the equator, and the best habitation. And briefly I put one reason that moved me. There is the best habitation where there is the greatest temperateness; but under the equator there is the greatest temperateness; therefore, etc. The major is known. I declare the minor: for under the equator day and night are equal, as has been said, and the air can be cooled as much at night as it is heated during the day. Also, the sun's declination varies most there. For the declination changes in one day by 24 minutes, so it can hardly happen that the sun passes directly over their heads, for this does not happen unless the sun enters Aries or Libra directly at noon. Also, the sun never remains at the same altitude above them; but immediately, if there was motion, the altitude would vary in an instant. For as quickly as the prime mover moves, it approaches the meridian and recedes from the meridian. And where great heating is to occur, it is necessary that the rays penetrate for some time, as we see in a burning mirror, if the mirror were moved, it would not ignite straws or anything else that was opposed. Also, cold planets restrain the heat of the hot ones there, because all are always near there, although some say that planets do not cause cold, but their coldness is less heat. In truth, I do not believe this, with all due respect to them. For I have seen that the same cause of cold does not produce as much cold in very northern parts as it does in less northern parts. It could also be proven by reason. If it were so that no planet influenced cold, but lacked heat, it would follow that the presence of Saturn in summer would make summer hot, because such an addition to such makes it more such; but we do not see this, why, etc. And if someone were to say that its lesser heat tempers the greater heat of the sun, like warm water with hot. This is not valid, because there is a material mixture. In celestial influences, however, there is a virtual mixture. And it is to be imagined in influences of greater and lesser heat as of two fires heating a pot, one of which is stronger than the other. It is certain that the lesser fire will not cool the pot nor hinder the stronger fire in its heating. The reasons in opposition are solved: for what says that there is perpetual heat there. I say that there is not perpetual heat there that impedes habitation. I deny the author of the sphere in this matter. And six of them are said to ascend directly. It could be divided, but there is no force. It briefly says that 6 signs from the aforesaid signs ascend directly, and 6 obliquely. They ascend directly from the beginning of Cancer to the end of Sagittarius. The other 6 ascend obliquely. This is from the treatise on the sphere, so it is not to be stood here. Afterwards, it says that the tortuous or obliquely ascending signs obey the directly ascending ones. According to this, it is to be noted that if two are born at certain times in which all other significations are equal as to the superior and also inferior root, but one is born under a directly ascending sign and the other under an obliquely or tortuously ascending sign, the one who was born under the oblique will obey the other who was born under the direct sign, as long as those signs are of the same longitude from the beginning of Cancer. For example: he who was born under Gemini will obey him who was born under Cancer; and he who was under Taurus, him who was under Leo; and he who was under Aries, him who was under Virgo, and so on for the others. Afterwards, it says that any two signs that are of the same longitude from the beginning of Aries are said to be concordant, like Pisces and Aries, Aquarius and Taurus, Capricorn and Gemini, and so on for the others. And it can be applied as before. And this is because the ascensions of those signs are equal. And this is what the author of the treatise on the sphere says: any two signs equally distant from either of the equinoctial points are equal in their ascensions. Afterwards, it says that the half of the circle that is from the beginning of Leo to the end of Capricorn is called the maximum half because of the ascensions. And it is the sun's half: because Leo is the house of the sun and the ascensions begin to increase notably there. And the sun has dignity in that whole half, like the planets in their terms. And the other half is the moon's, that is, from the beginning of Aquarius to the end of Cancer: and that is called the minimum half because the ascensions begin to be notably named there. And the moon has dignity there which the sun has in the maximum. From above.

author: Alchabitius
title: The Houses of the Planets
text: Et uocatur medietas.ie Dic comparat figna ad inuice quantum ad qualitates in ges nerali. Et breuiter dicit & medietas que eft a principio arietis vsqzad finem virginis est medietas calida:quia facit nobis estates. Et alia medietas eft fri gida:quia facit nobis byemé. iben goo bot Pic'cóparat ifta Et uocatur illa quarta pars.gaad.te pora anni:z ad.4.etates: ad.4.humores fiue complexiones. Et dicit o ar ta pars q est a principio arietis vios ad principiú cancri eſt calidar bumida ficut ver z ficut etas puerilis z ficut complexio fanguinea. Et alia quarta po q eſt a pncipio cácri vios ad incipia libre est calida z ficca ficut estas: z ficut fecunda etas z ficut complexio colerica. Et alia quarta po que eſt a incapio libre vios ad principium capricorni eſt frigida z ficca ficut autumnusz ficut tertia etas que est principium diminutionis z ficut complexio melancolics. Et quarta pars que eſt a principio capricorni vſqz ad finem piſcium eft frigi da z bumida ficut byems:7 ficut etas vltima:zcomplexio flegmatica.lt Pic ordiné.7.plane Omnibus aut planetis.tarumiz quo accipiť in qn Dicit planeta eſt in figno z patet in littera. Sed nota hic triplicem acceptioez figni ficut ponit auctor ſphere: quia ibi inuenies no pono bic. Abent autem planete.prius determias *uit auctor de diui fione circuli fignorum: de quibufdam que cófequütur ipſam di Buifionem. Dic vult determinare de dignitatibus planetarum infi gnis. Et diuidik in duas. Quonia primo determinat de dignitati bus effentialibus. Secundo de accidétalibus. fa ps est ibi:er pratib quoqz planetay accidéralibus in fine huius differétie. Prima pars diuidit in du as:qm in prima facit qo dictum eft.f.determinat de dignitatibus planetarű in fignis. In ba parte determinat de fortitudinibus quas babent in ipfis dis gnitatibus.ka ibi:z quia iam auxiliate deo. Prima pars diuiditur in gnas partes Fm quinqz dignitates: qm primo determinat de dignitate planeta rum que vocat domus. Secúdo de dignitate que vocat exaltatio. Ler tio de dignitate que vocať triplicitas. Qnarto de dignitate q vocaf termi nus. Quinto de dignitate qvocat facies. Pa ibisbe fut exaltatiões.ftia ibi: triplicitates fic diftiguim. arta ibi:bit quoqz in vnoqefigno.guta ibi:facis es aut fignor. Puma pars pót diuidiin duas ptes:qmin prima pte ondit int domus planetar. In ſcoa pte qu qlibet eox pterſoléz lunabz duas Dom:quarú vna eſt eimagis pncipalis:z illa vocat gaudiúzió ondit q fint il le domus in qb gaudet.ka ibi: figna aut in qb. Prima diuidit in duas.pu mo ponit determinaduz de dignitatibus planetar z ponit diuifioné in litte ra.Scdo pleqkibi:domus be fut.Dicit o planete brit in fignis pdictis quaf dam dignitates feu ptátes per naturam & quafdam per accidens. Que funt per naturam funt domus exaltatio triplicitas terminus z facies. De bis aut dignitatibus quas habent per accidens vult poftea dicere. ind ==Start of OCR for page 1== Domus be funtissimuseu Dicit qaries z ſcorpio funt domus mars ni z virgo domus mercurij:cacer domus lune:leo dom? ſolis ſagittari z pi ſces domiouis:capricornoz ağri dom saturni. Postea dicit q.7.fignum a domo cuiuslibet dicit ei detrimétű:verbi gra:ficut leo est dom ſolis fic ağs rius qu eft fignú oppofitú leoni eſt detrimétú ſolis:7 eodem mo itelligas de alijs. Deinde dicit & duo ſigna que funt dom? vni planete dir acordatia in cingulo.i.in circulo fignorum fiue zodiaco qui est latus in medio zin ligatu ra ſtrict.i.iuxta polos. Ad q itelligédú opz imaginari circulos tráfeūtes p incipia fignox z per polos 30diaciiz totú ſpaciú cóprehéfum iter quoflibet duos circulos est.vnú fignú ficut exéplificatů fuit in lective pcedéte. Jſta có, cordátia videť mibi parú facere ad hoc y duo figna debeat eé dom vnius planete qe qlibet duo figna illo mó cócordát. Jó pót aliter exponi fiue gloſa ri verbú auctoris q õlibet duo figna que funt domus vni planete dir con cordatia in 30diaco qstú ad diſtátiá a domib? luminariú:z illo mó eſt verus: verbi gra:gemini z virgo funt dom mercurij zeliter diſtat a leone z a can cro que funt dom? luminariűz taur z libra que funt dom veneris eğer dis ſtat a leone 2 cácro. Et bác cócordatiá iuenies in oib-ptoleme affignat cáz qre leo est dom ſolis z cacer domlune z fikr de alijs in.173.ppone 7.6.20 pónib ſequentib? pme pris de domib? luminariú ſolis z lune dicit duo figna ſeptétrionalia que funt ppinqora zenith capiti nroniqžalia z bac róne faci unt calozé que funt cacer z leo pofuerit ambo pdomib duoy luminarium magnis z nobilib.leone qe masculus domú ſolis z cácrú qe femia domú lue Pali dicit in cométo exponés verba pdicta q duo figna marie ſeptētriõalia funt căcer z gemini:ſed gemini eſt ſignú calidú z bumidū.ió nó cócordat cus aliquo eox. In hoc & humectat no acordat cuz ſole qui calefacit z deficcat: nec cu luna qe feminea eft z nocturna.fignú aut geminor eft masculinu di urnű.Sed leo cócordat cú ſoleiqi eſt figni calidú z ficcuió cóuenit vt leo fit dom? folis z qe luna ſegtur ſolé ió cóuenit vt dom lune fit circa domů folis Lúc diceret aliqs äre ergo virgo nó eft domº lúe cũ fit circa leoné qui est do mus folis.Dicit baly q fignú virginis no puenit lune:qe virgo eſt fignú iсси Inna vero humida. Et pp boc pofuerút căcrú qui eſt humid domu lune.de domib aliorú planetarú affignat duas cás:qrú vna accipit ab ordine plane tarú z fignoy. Alia ab aſpectib?. Breuiter vt cócludá itétioné fua paucis vers bis vult dicere o capricornus z ağrius funt dom? ſaturni qe bec duo figna maxime diftat a domib luminariu z ſaturnus eſt ſuperior oium planetarú. Et fagittarius z pisces funt domus Jouis:qa hec duo figna funt iuxta dom? faturni:ficut Jupiter eſt ſubſaturno. Scorpio z aries funt domus martis:qu funt iuxra domus Jouis ficut mars ſub Joue.Libra z taurus funt domove neris pp eadem cam. Et virgo z gemini domus mercurij. Et hinceperit 02- dinem ab inferiozi iuenies idé:verbi gra:virgo z gemini funt iuxta domos lu minariú z ſunt domus mercurij:qe mercuri fegtur post luna in ordine:z fic De alijs. translation: And it is called the midpoint. That is, it compares the signs to each other as to their qualities in general. And it briefly says that the midpoint from the beginning of Aries to the end of Virgo is the hot midpoint, because it makes summer for us. And the other midpoint is cold, because it makes winter for us. It compares these to the four seasons, and to the four ages, and to the four humors or complexions. And it says that the fourth part, which is from the beginning of Aries to the beginning of Cancer, is hot and humid, like spring and like childhood and like the sanguine complexion. And the other fourth part, which is from the beginning of Cancer to the beginning of Libra, is hot and dry, like summer and like the second age and like the choleric complexion. And the other fourth part, which is from the beginning of Libra to the beginning of Capricorn, is cold and dry, like autumn and like the third age, which is the beginning of diminution, and like the melancholic complexion. And the fourth part, which is from the beginning of Capricorn to the end of Pisces, is cold and humid, like winter and like the last age and the phlegmatic complexion. It arranges the seven planets. It is taken from when a planet is in a sign, and it is clear in the letter. But note here the threefold acceptance of the sign as the author of the sphere states, because you will find it there, I do not put it here. The planets have. The author first determined the division of the circle of signs, and some things that follow that division. Here he wants to determine the dignities of the planets in the signs. And it is divided into two. Because first he determines the essential dignities. Second, the accidental ones. This part is there: and also concerning the accidental planets at the end of this difference. The first part is divided into two: because in the first he does what has been said, that is, he determines the dignities of the planets in the signs. In this part he determines the strengths they have in these dignities. As there: and because now, with God's help. The first part is divided into five parts according to the five dignities: because first he determines the dignity of the planets which is called the house. Second, the dignity which is called exaltation. Third, the dignity which is called triplicity. Fourth, the dignity which is called term. Fifth, the dignity which is called face. As there: these are the exaltations. These are the triplicities, we distinguish them thus. Fourth there: also in each sign. Fifth there: the faces of the signs. The first part can be divided into two parts: because in the first part it shows the houses of the planets. In the second part, each of them, except the Sun and Moon, has two houses, one of which is more principal, and that is called joy, and it shows which are those houses in which they rejoice. As there: the signs in which. The first is divided into two. First it sets out to determine the dignities of the planets and sets out the division in the letter. Second, it follows there: these are the houses. It says that the planets have in the aforesaid signs certain dignities or powers by nature and certain by accident. Those that are by nature are house, exaltation, triplicity, term, and face. But concerning these dignities which they have by accident, he will speak later. These are the houses. It says that Aries and Scorpio are the houses of Mars, and Virgo is the house of Mercury: Cancer is the house of the Moon: Leo is the house of the Sun: Sagittarius and Pisces are the houses of Jupiter: Capricorn and Aquarius are the houses of Saturn. Afterwards it says that the seventh sign from the house of any planet is called its detriment: for example, just as Leo is the house of the Sun, so Aquarius, which is the sign opposite to Leo, is the detriment of the Sun: and understand the same for the others. Then it says that the two signs which are the house of one planet are in agreement in the girdle, that is, in the circle of signs or the zodiac, which is wide in the middle and narrow at the poles. To understand this, it is necessary to imagine circles passing through the beginnings of the signs and through the poles of the zodiac, and the entire space comprehended between any two circles is one sign, as was exemplified in the preceding lecture. This agreement seems to me to do little to explain why two signs should be the house of one planet, because any two signs agree in that way. Therefore, the author's words can be explained or glossed differently, that any two signs which are the house of one planet agree in the zodiac as to their distance from the houses of the luminaries: and in that way it is true: for example, Gemini and Virgo are the houses of Mercury, and they are equally distant from Leo and Cancer, which are the houses of the luminaries, and Taurus and Libra, which are the houses of Venus, are equally distant from Leo and Cancer. And you will find this agreement in all. Ptolemy assigns the reason why Leo is the house of the Sun and Cancer is the house of the Moon, and similarly for the others in the 173rd proposition and the following 7th and 6th propositions of the first part concerning the houses of the luminaries, the Sun and Moon. He says that the two northern signs which are closer to our zenith, and for this reason cause heat, which are Cancer and Leo, were placed as the houses of the two great and noble luminaries. Leo, because it is masculine, is the house of the Sun, and Cancer, because it is feminine, is the house of the Moon. Others say in the commentary, explaining the aforesaid words, that the two northern signs are Cancer and Gemini: but Gemini is a hot and humid sign. Therefore it does not agree with any of them. In that it moistens, it does not agree with the Sun, which heats and dries: nor with the Moon, which is feminine and nocturnal. But the sign of Gemini is masculine and diurnal. But Leo agrees with the Sun, which is a hot and dry sign. Therefore it is fitting that Leo should be the house of the Sun, and because the Moon follows the Sun, therefore it is fitting that the house of the Moon should be near the house of the Sun. Then someone might say, why then is Virgo not the house of the Moon, since it is near Leo, which is the house of the Sun? Balinus says that the sign of Virgo is not suitable for the Moon: because Virgo is a dry sign, but the Moon is humid. And for this reason they placed Cancer, which is humid, as the house of the Moon. For the houses of the other planets, he assigns two reasons: one is taken from the order of the planets and signs. The other from the aspects. Briefly, to conclude his intention in a few words, he wants to say that Capricorn and Aquarius are the houses of Saturn, because these two signs are most distant from the houses of the luminaries, and Saturn is superior to all planets. And Sagittarius and Pisces are the houses of Jupiter: because these two signs are next to the houses of Saturn: just as Jupiter is below Saturn. Scorpio and Aries are the houses of Mars: because they are next to the houses of Jupiter, just as Mars is below Jupiter. Libra and Taurus are the houses of Venus for the same reason. And Virgo and Gemini are the houses of Mercury. And if you start the order from the inferior, you will find the same: for example, Virgo and Gemini are next to the houses of the luminaries and are the houses of Mercury: because Mercury follows the Moon in order: and so for the others. The other reason he assigns is taken from the aspects, and he wants to say that Saturn is greater in fortune: therefore his houses should be distant from the houses of the luminaries by an opposing aspect.

translation: which is an aspect of perfect enmity. In this way, Capricorn and Aquarius are distant. For Capricorn is in opposition to the Moon's domicile, namely Cancer, and Aquarius is in opposition to the Sun's domicile, namely Leo. And Jupiter is the greater fortune; its domicile should be distant from the luminaries' domiciles by a trine aspect, that is, an aspect of perfect friendship. In this way, Sagittarius and Pisces are distant: for Sagittarius aspects Leo with a trine aspect, and Pisces aspects Cancer. And Mars is the lesser fortune; its domicile should be distant from the luminaries' domiciles by a square aspect, which is an aspect of medium enmity. In this way, Scorpio and Aries are distant: for Scorpio aspects Leo with a square aspect, and Aries aspects Cancer. Venus is the lesser fortune; its domicile should be distant from the luminaries' domiciles by a sextile aspect, which is an aspect of medium friendship. In this way, Libra and Taurus are distant: for Libra aspects Leo with a sextile aspect, and Taurus aspects Cancer. And the two remaining signs are left for Mercury, namely Virgo and Gemini, which are near the domiciles of the luminaries, because Mercury can never be more than one sign distant from the Sun; indeed, according to the equations of the Alfonsine Tables, it cannot be more than 28 degrees and 7 minutes distant from the Sun. The dignities in which each of the five planets has domiciles show which is the principal domicile of each of them. It says that Saturn rejoices in Aquarius, Jupiter in Sagittarius, Mars in Scorpio, Venus in Taurus, Mercury in Virgo. These are the exaltations. It says the Sun is exalted in the 19th degree of Aries, etc. The text is clear. Ptolemy assigns the exaltations of the planets in the 19th proposition of the first part of the Quadripartitum and in the 6th following. He wants to say that the 19th degree of Aries is the exaltation of the Sun because when the Sun enters Aries, the days begin to increase over the nights, and its heat begins to appear more there. And Libra is its fall, because there the nights begin to increase over the days, and the heat begins to diminish there. And because Saturn is opposite to the Sun, because where heat increases, cold diminishes, and vice versa, therefore Libra is the exaltation of Saturn, and Aries is its fall. And when the Moon conjoins the Sun in Aries, its light first begins to appear in Taurus; therefore Taurus is the exaltation of the Moon, and Scorpio is its fall, because it is the opposite sign. And because Jupiter causes northern winds, and Cancer is a northern sign, therefore Cancer is the exaltation of Jupiter, and the opposite sign, namely Capricorn, is its fall. And because Mars is naturally scorching, and especially when it is in Capricorn, it burns much more, as it inclines to the southern part, therefore Capricorn is its exaltation, and it is the sign opposite to Jupiter's exaltation, and Cancer is its fall. And because Venus naturally humidifies, and especially when it is in Pisces, Pisces is the exaltation of Venus, and the opposite sign, namely Virgo, is its fall. And because Mercury dries by itself, and in this is contrary to Venus, therefore Virgo is its exaltation, which is the sign opposite to Venus's exaltation, and Pisces is its fall. Albumazar assigns almost the same causes, although he uses different words, and he received them from Ptolemy; therefore, let these suffice concerning the causes of domiciles and exaltations. First, the author determined concerning the domiciles and exaltations of the planets. Here he determines concerning the triplicities. And because aspects accompany triplicities, he therefore determines subsequently concerning the aspects of the signs. And according to this, this part can be divided into two parts. In the first, he determines concerning the triplicities. In the second, concerning the aspects, there, "secondly, the signs are said to aspect each other." The first part can be divided into two. In the first, he does what has been said. In the second, he shows which signs are movable, which fixed, and which common. The second part begins there: "four of these signs." The first part is divided into four parts according to the four triplicities. The second part begins there: "triplicity." The third part begins there: "triplicity." The fourth part begins there: "fourth triplicity." The first part is divided into two. In the first, he determines concerning the triplicities in general. In the second, in particular. The first part begins at the beginning. The second part begins there: "Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius." He says first that all three signs that are of the same nature make one triplicity. Afterwards, he says that Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius make the first triplicity, because these three signs are of the same nature, namely fiery, masculine, diurnal, choleric, hot and dry, bitter in taste, and these three signs are oriental. And this triplicity, like any of the others, has three lords who are called the lords of the triplicity. The first lord of this triplicity in nativities or questions or any other matters that occur during the day is the Sun. The second is Jupiter. The third is Saturn. In matters that occur at night, the first lord of this triplicity is Jupiter. The second is the Sun. The third is Saturn. It should be noted here what Albumazar says in his introduction, that the first sign of this triplicity, namely Aries, produces temperate heat. The second, namely Leo, produces scorching heat. The third, namely Sagittarius, produces corrupting heat. When any of these signs falls in the ascendant of a conjunction or opposition of the Sun and Moon in winter, the cold is tempered. In summer, there is excessive heat, especially if the lord of that sign is present in that sign or in any of the angles aspecting the ascendant. Afterwards, he says that Taurus, Virgo, and Capricorn make the second triplicity because they are of the same nature, for all three are feminine, nocturnal, earthy, cold and dry, melancholic, sour in taste, southern. The lords of this triplicity during the day are: first Venus, then the Moon, then Mars. And at night: the Moon first, then Venus, then Mars. Albumazar says that the first sign of this triplicity produces temperate cold, strengthening animals and crops. The second produces harmful cold. The third produces corrupting cold. When any of these signs falls in the ascendant of a conjunction or opposition of the Sun and Moon in winter, and especially Capricorn, there is great cold, especially if Saturn is in that triplicity. Then he says that Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius make the third triplicity because they are of the same nature: for they are masculine, diurnal, sanguine, hot and humid, airy, western, sweet in taste. Their lords during the day are: first Saturn, second Mercury, third Jupiter. At night: first Mercury, second Saturn, third Jupiter. And Albumazar says that the first sign of this triplicity produces temperate, strengthening, and sweet winds. The second produces harmful winds. The third produces winds that dissipate animals and crops. When any of these is the ascendant at the hour of the conjunction of the Sun and Moon, winds move in that lunation. And similarly understand this concerning the opposition. Afterwards, he says that Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces make the fourth triplicity because all three are feminine, nocturnal, northern, watery, cold and humid, salty in taste. Their lords during the day are: first Venus, second Mars, third the Moon. At night: first Mars, second Venus, third the Moon. Albumazar says that the first sign of this triplicity

author: Alchabitius
title: Rainfall Prediction from Conjunctions and Oppositions
text: ==Start of OCR for page 4== facit humiditate temperata. Secüdű abundatez. Zertiü deftruétez.Quádo aliqo fignor iſtoy fuerit in afcendéte cõiunctióis vel oppoſitóis ſolis z lune plumédi eſt de pluuia illo mése. Ab ifta.n.triplicitate z a venere z a luna ma xime incipiút pluuie. Dicit Abraba auénezre y si in cõiúctióe vel oppoſitõe pcedit introitu folis in arieté:fiue fuerit cõiunctio fiue oppofitio fuerit all qo iftoy fignop afcendés:z cú boc luminaria fuerint in angulis.i.in afcendé te:yel medio celi vel in occidete: autin angulo medie noctis erit nimia plus uia in illo anno. Sed fi luminaria fuerint in cafu ab angulis nó deſcédet plu via in illo mēle z in maioripte illi anni. Et ego exptus fum pluries o qn afcé dens piunctióis eft fignú aquatici:z luna ante oppoſitidez intrat illo ſignuz in eodé die qn luna intrat gradu q fuit in afcédéte incipit pluuia z durat ad minus tantú quítum luna morat in illo figno. Et fuo mo intelligas de vétos fis fignis z frigidis. Doc imienip experiétiam:fed poftea hoc inueni in libro Abiabá auenezre. z multum gauifus fui:qi magis fui certus deveritate:fed Abraba dic q 5 maxime eſt vey ſi ſignú afcendés fuerit aliqs anguloy figu re illi terre. Hoc intelligo de figura reuolutióis illi ani in terra illa. Illa.n.di versificat 63 diuerfas regiões. Et si no fuerit aligs #glou figur illi terre erat nubes fin pluuia.Lae triplicitatú fere fút eedé cu caufis domopsió ptráfeo. Quattuor quoqz ex bis ſignis bicoftedir füt figna mobiliar fira z media fiue coia. Et aſſignat cas z ponit exépla lra eſt pla na. Notandu eſt bic & Prolemeus dicitin.22.propofitide cetiloquij. No ua veſtiméta facere vel exercere luna in leone exite timédú eſt. Et dicit og facere intédit -ptolemeus ſcina dere z fuere. Per exercere intédit induere z vti ex hoc poffumus accipe rea gulam o oés res quas volumus bře fixas debem incipe luna exiftente in figno fixo:ficut edificare domos z cetera. Et res quas volumus cito muta re debemus incipere luna exiſtente in ſigno mobili ficut inter z cetera. тит Icútur aut figna se afpicere. au ctor determinauit de dignitate planetar avocat triplicitas. translation: makes for temperate humidity. Secondly, abundant. Thirdly, destructive. When any of these signs are in the ascendant of a conjunction or opposition of the Sun and Moon, rain is to be expected in that month. For from this triplicity, and from Venus and the Moon, rains especially begin. Abraham Avenezra says that if, in the conjunction or opposition preceding the Sun's entry into Aries, whether it be a conjunction or opposition, any of these signs is ascending, and with this the luminaries are in the angles (i.e., in the ascendant, or midheaven, or in the occident, or in the angle of midnight), there will be excessive rain in that year. But if the luminaries are cadent from the angles, rain will not fall in that month and for the greater part of that year. And I have experienced many times that when the ascendant of the conjunction is a watery sign, and the Moon enters that sign before the opposition, on the same day that the Moon enters the degree that was in the ascendant, rain begins and lasts at least as long as the Moon remains in that sign. And in a similar way, you should understand this for windy and cold signs. I found this by experience, but afterwards I found this in the book of Abraham Avenezra, and I rejoiced greatly because I was more certain of the truth. But Abraham says that it is especially true if the ascending sign is one of the angles of the figure of that land. I understand this concerning the figure of the revolution of that year in that land. For it varies according to different regions. And if it is not one of the angles of the figure of that land, there will be clouds without rain. These triplicities are almost the same as the causes of the houses, which I pass over. Four of these signs are also called bicorporeal, and there are movable, fixed, and common signs. And it assigns them and gives examples. The letter is plain. It should be noted here that Ptolemy says in the 22nd proposition of the Centiloquium: 'To make or use new clothes when the Moon is in Leo is to be feared.' And Paulus says in his commentary on the same proposition that all fixed signs are prohibited for this, and especially Leo, which, besides being fixed, is a sign of great victories and signifies the victory of all kings who exercise and have bad dominion over them. And he says that to 'make' Ptolemy intends to cut and sew. By 'use' he intends to wear and utilize. From this we can derive a rule that all things we wish to be fixed we should begin when the Moon is in a fixed sign, such as building houses and so on. And things we wish to change quickly we should begin when the Moon is in a movable sign, such as traveling and so on. Now the signs aspect each other. The author has determined concerning the dignity of the planets, which he calls triplicities. And because triplicity is taken from a trine aspect, therefore in this incidental part he determines concerning aspects, and it can be divided into two parts: in the first part he determines concerning aspects. In this part, concerning the projections of rays. The second part begins there: 'When a planet is in any sign.' The first part can be divided into four parts according to the four aspects. In the first, he determines concerning the sextile aspect. In the second part, concerning the square aspect. In the third, concerning the trine. In the fourth, concerning the opposition. The second part begins there: 'and it aspects the square before it.' The third part there: 'it also aspects the trine before it.' The fourth part there: 'it also aspects the seventh.' He says first that the signs aspect each other in this way: Every sign aspects the third sign after it and the third sign before it, just as Aries aspects the sign of Gemini, which is the third before it, and the sign of Aquarius, which is the third after it. And this aspect is called sextile because it holds the sixth part of the circle, i.e., 60 degrees, which is the quantity of two signs, and this aspect is called an aspect of medium friendship, and it can be the reason why any two signs distant in this way agree in one quality and with this in sex. For example, Aries and Gemini agree in heat and masculinity, and similarly Aries and Aquarius. Taurus and Cancer agree in coldness and femininity, and similarly Taurus and Pisces, and so you should understand for all. Afterwards he says that every sign aspects the fourth before it and the fourth after it, and this aspect is called tetragon or square because it holds the fourth part of the circle, i.e., 90 degrees, which is the quantity of three signs. And this aspect is called an aspect of medium enmity, because any two signs distant in this way sometimes disagree in one quality, sometimes in both; they also disagree in sex, for if one of them is masculine, the other will be feminine. And this is the reason why in acute diseases, for the most part, a crisis occurs when the Moon comes to the fourth aspect of the place where it was at the beginning of the disease, especially if there is a disagreement in both qualities; and commonly then a salutary crisis occurs. For example, if the Moon was at the beginning of the illness in any of the signs of the fiery triplicity, especially in Aries, when it comes to the fourth sign, which disagrees in both qualities and in sex, commonly a crisis occurs for health, just as if it was in Aries, the fourth aspect will be in Cancer; if in Leo, the fourth aspect will be in Scorpio; if in Sagittarius, the fourth aspect will be in Pisces. In other signs, however, there is no disagreement except in one quality and in sex. For example: Taurus and Leo: Taurus is a cold and dry feminine sign. Leo is a hot and dry masculine sign. And you will find this disagreement in others in their own way. And because of this, crises in these signs sometimes occur for health and sometimes for death, according to the fortunate or unfortunate aspect to the Moon. Then he says that every sign aspects the fifth before it and the fifth after it, and this aspect is called trine because it holds the third part of the circle, i.e., 120 degrees. And this aspect is called an aspect of perfect friendship, because any three signs distant in this way form one triplicity and agree in natures, as was said in the immediately preceding chapter. Afterwards he says: every sign also aspects the seventh from itself, i.e., the opposite sign; and this is an aspect of perfect enmity by reason of opposition. And every planet that is in any degree of any sign aspects a planet existing in another sign in a similar degree with the same aspect with which those two signs aspect each other. And the author speaks of 30 degrees of the zodiac; but according to Ptolemy's doctrine, aspects are taken in the equinoctial; therefore the letter can be glossed in this way. And this is to be understood of the degrees of the equinoctial. For example: if any planet is in Aries and another in Gemini, and the ascensions of the direct circle of the one in Aries have been subtracted from the ascensions of the direct circle of the one in Gemini, if 60 degrees remain, they aspect each other with a sextile aspect. In this way, understand for other aspects. In this, the author, 'when a planet is,' briefly explains himself by saying: If any planet is in any sign, it projects its rays in a similar degree of the sign that aspects it with some aspect. For example: if any planet is in the first degree of Aries and in the second minute, it projects its sextile rays to the first degree of Gemini in its second minute. The author was too brief in this chapter, therefore it is necessary to dwell a little on the explanation of aspects and the projection of rays. It must first be understood that aspects are considered in three ways. In one way, according to equal degrees, and according to this mode the author in the text spoke of aspects and their projection.

author: Alchabitius
title: Three Ways of Considering Aspects

translation: There are three ways of considering aspects and the projection of rays. One way is to consider aspects and the projection of rays according to the degrees of the zodiacal circle, that is, by computing the degrees of the zodiac that are between one planet and another. This is the method that the author presents in his book, and Abraham ibn Ezra also holds this method. He says that it has been proven almost infinitely that aspects should be taken in the zodiac and not in the equinoctial, and this method is very easy. It has been sufficiently exemplified in the letter. The second way is to consider aspects in the equinoctial ascensions of the direct circle, according to which Ptolemy instructs to take them, as was said above in the letter. If, therefore, you wish to equate aspects according to this method, seek the ascensions of the planet in the direct circle, which begin from Capricorn, and keep them. Then, in the same way, seek the ascensions of the degree of the other planet in the same direct circle, and keep them. Then subtract the ascensions of the one that is closer to Capricorn according to the order of the signs from the ascensions of the one that is farther away. If 60 degrees remain, they aspect each other with a sextile aspect. If 90 degrees remain, they aspect each other with a quartile aspect. And if 120 degrees remain, they aspect each other with a trine aspect. For an opposition aspect, however, it is not necessary to seek ascensions, because two planets that are opposite in the zodiac are also opposite in the equinoctial. This method is easy, therefore it is not necessary to dwell much on it here. The third method of aspects and the projection of rays is very difficult, especially for those not practiced in calculations. It must first be understood concerning this method that the projection of rays is taken according to the ascensions of the direct circle if the planet whose rays we wish to project is in the midheaven or in the angle of the earth. And if it is in the degree of the ascendant or in the degree of the descendant, it is taken according to the ascensions of the region or horizon, using tables made for that region. If, however, the planet is not in any of these four places, but in intermediate places, then the radiations are taken mixed. Therefore, when you wish to project rays to a planet, consider if the planet whose rays you wish to project is in the midheaven or in the angle of the earth. Then seek the ascensions of that degree in the direct circle. To these, add 60 degrees if you want a left sextile radiation, that which precedes according to the order of the signs from the place of the planet. And reduce the degrees obtained after the addition to equal degrees in the direct circle according to the doctrine of the canons of the primum mobile. The zodiacal degree that results in that place is the sextile ray. And just as I said 60 degrees for the sextile radiation, so understand 90 for the quartile radiation and 120 for the trine radiation. If, however, you want right radiations, that is, those that precede from the place of the planet towards the west, subtract from the ascensions of the planet's degree 60 degrees for the sextile radiation, or 90 for the quartile radiation, or 120 for the trine radiation. And reduce the remaining number to equal degrees as before, and the zodiacal degree that results in that place is the ray. For example, in a left radiation, I assume that Saturn is in the angle of the midheaven at the end of the sixth degree of Virgo. The ascensions of that degree in the direct circle are 247 degrees and 48 minutes. I want to have a sextile radiation, so I add 60 degrees, which gives 307 degrees and 48 minutes. I reduce these to equal degrees in the direct circle, and they result in 10 degrees, 14 minutes, and 14 seconds of Scorpio. There are the left sextile rays of Saturn in this case. For example, in a right sextile radiation, I subtract 60 degrees from the aforementioned ascensions, and 187 degrees and 48 minutes remain. I reduce these to equal degrees in the direct circle, and they result in 7 degrees, 9 minutes, and 15 seconds of Cancer. There, therefore, are the right sextile rays of Saturn. If, however, the planet whose rays you wish to project is in the degree of the ascendant, seek the ascensions of the planet's degree from the table of ascensions of the oblique circle serving your region. And if you want a left radiation, add to the ascensions of the planet's degree 60 degrees for a sextile radiation, or 90 for a quartile radiation, or 120 for a trine radiation. For example, in a left sextile radiation, I assume that Jupiter is in the degree of the ascendant at the end of the 10th degree of Sagittarius. The ascensions of that degree in the oblique horizon are 275 degrees and 27 minutes. I want to have a left sextile radiation, so I add 60 degrees to them, and 335 degrees and 27 minutes result. I reduce these to equal degrees in the oblique circle, and they result in 12 degrees, 44 minutes, and 21 seconds of Aquarius. There, therefore, are the sextile rays of Jupiter. For example, in a right sextile radiation, I subtract 60 degrees from the ascensions of Jupiter's degree, and 215 degrees and 27 minutes remain. I reduce these to equal degrees, and they result in 25 degrees, 54 minutes, and 50 seconds of Libra. There are the sextile rays of Jupiter. If, however, the planet is in the degree of the descendant, you will work with the opposite degree. That is, you will take the ascensions of the nadir of the planet's degree in the oblique horizon and proceed in the same way as before. But after you have reduced to equal degrees, the place of the ray is the place opposite to the place you find. And to make it clearer, I will give an example. I assume that Jupiter is in the degree of the descendant at the end of the 10th degree of Sagittarius. I seek the ascensions of the 10th degree of Gemini and find 41 degrees and 9 minutes. I add 60 degrees for a sextile radiation, and 101 degrees and 9 minutes result. I reduce these to equal degrees in the oblique horizon, and they result in 2 degrees, 30 minutes, and 45 seconds of Leo. Therefore, the rays are in the opposite of this, that is, 2 degrees, 30 minutes, and 45 seconds of Aquarius. If, however, the planet whose rays you wish to project is not in any of the aforementioned places, then it is necessary to take the radiations in a mixed way, according to the proportion of the distance of the planet's degree between one angle and another. The method is this: if the planet whose rays you wish to project is between the midheaven and the ascendant degree, first take the ascensions of the midheaven degree. Then subtract from the ascensions of the planet's degree in the direct circle, and what remains is the distance from the midheaven, which you keep. Then, to the ascensions of the planet's degree in the direct circle, add 60 degrees for a sextile radiation, or 90 for a quartile radiation, or 120 for a trine radiation. And reduce what results to equal degrees in the direct circle, and what results, keep and call it the radiation of the direct circle. Then seek the ascensions of the planet's degree in the oblique horizon using the table of your region. To these, add 60 degrees for a sextile radiation, or 90 for a quartile radiation, or 120 for a trine radiation. And reduce what results to equal degrees using the tables of your region, and what results, keep and call it the radiation of the region. Then see the difference between the radiation of the direct circle and the radiation of the region. Multiply this difference by the distance of the planet's degree from the midheaven.

author: Alchabitius
title: Calculating Planetary Rays

Dis itaqz fer uatis qre afcéfiones gradus planete in circulo obliquo p tabula factá ad la titudiné tue regióis qb adde.60.p radiate ſextilize.7 98 puenit reduc ad gradus equales in circulo obliquo z uocetur radiatio circuli obliqui Deins de quere afcéfiones gradus planete in circulo directo:gbus adde.60.gras dus pro radiatiõe ſextili z ceterasz o puenerit reduc ad gradus eğles in cir culo directo:z qo puenerit ferua:z voceť radiatio circuli directi.
