In nullo alio differt op nisi vbi pcepi addere afcefidib?.60.grad: ze.dés fubtrabere.60.zć Si aút gradus placte cuius radios vis pijcere: fuerit inter gradú occidétis z gradu.io.domopaberis cú gradu oppofito planete recte peandé viá: ficut iam dixi inter afcendens z angulű terre.quá do enim grad planete eſt inter gradum occidétis 7 mediú celi eius oppos e iij
translation: Divide the product by half the arc of the planet's diurnal degree. The proportional part will be obtained, which you add to the radiation of the direct circle. If the radiation of the direct circle is less than the radiation of the region, subtract it from itself if it is greater. What results will be the place of the rays. You will find half the arc of the planet's diurnal degree by subtracting the ascensions of the planet's degree in the oblique circle from the ascensions of its nadir and halving that. It could also be found in another way, the proportional part of the two radiations, namely, by taking a sixth part of the day and multiplying it by the hours of the planet's distance from the midheaven. But this method is more tedious than the first, because according to this method, it is necessary to divide the distance of the planet's degree from the meridian by the parts of the hours of the planet's degree to find the hours of distance from the meridian. I advise the first method. I give an example in this: let the degree of the midheaven be 20 degrees of Leo completed, and Saturn be in the sixth degree of Virgo completed. I seek the distance of Saturn from the midheaven by the ascensions of the direct circle. And I find the distance to be 15 degrees, 22 minutes. I will keep this. Then I subtract the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the oblique circle from the ascensions of the opposite degree. And there remain 201 degrees, 20 minutes, which is the entire diurnal arc of Saturn, which I will divide in half. And 100 degrees, 40 minutes result, which is half the diurnal arc of Saturn; I will keep this. Then I seek the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the direct circle, and I find 247 degrees, 48 minutes, to which I add 60 degrees, because I want the sextile sinister radiation. And 307 degrees, 48 minutes will result, which I reduce to equal degrees in the direct circle, and I find 10 degrees, 14 minutes, 14 seconds of Scorpio. And this is the radiation of the direct circle. Then I seek the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the oblique circle in the region whose latitude is 48 degrees. And I find 147 degrees, 11 minutes, to which I add 60 degrees, and 207 degrees, 11 minutes result, which I reduce to equal degrees in the oblique circle, and I find 19 degrees, 52 minutes, 14 seconds of Libra. I see that the radiation of the direct circle is greater than the radiation of the region. I subtract the smaller from the larger, and I find in the difference 20 degrees, 22 minutes, which I reduce to the same kind; and 1222 result. I also reduce the distance of Saturn from the midheaven to the same kind. The distance was 15 degrees, 22 minutes, reduced to the same kind, 920 minutes. I will multiply the difference by the distance, and 26684 seconds result. Then I reduce half the diurnal arc of Saturn to the same kind. Half the diurnal arc was 100 degrees, 40 minutes, reduced to the same kind, 6040 minutes, by which I divide the number that resulted from the multiplication. And three degrees, 6 minutes, 32 seconds will result, which I subtract from the radiation of the direct circle, because that was greater, and there remain seven degrees, 7 minutes, 42 seconds of Scorpio. There are the sinister sextile rays of Saturn in the proposed case. If, however, I wanted the dexter radiation, where I instructed to add 60 degrees, I would subtract 60, and proceed as before. If, however, the degree of the planet whose rays you wish to project is between the ascending degree and the angle of the earth, see its longitude from the ascending degree, which you will know in this way: Subtract the ascensions of the ascending degree in the oblique circle from the ascensions of the planet's degree in the same oblique circle. And if you want the sinister radiation, what remains will be the distance of the planet from the ascending degree. Then seek half the nocturnal arc of the planet's degree, which you will know in this way: Subtract the ascensions of the planet's nadir degree from the ascensions of the planet's degree, and the entire nocturnal arc will remain, which you halve. Having observed these things, seek the ascensions of the planet's degree in the oblique circle by a table made for the latitude of your region, to which add 60 for the sextile radiation. And what results, reduce to equal degrees in the oblique circle, and let it be called the radiation of the oblique circle. Then seek the ascensions of the planet's degree in the direct circle, to which add 60 degrees for the sextile radiation and the others, and what results, reduce to equal degrees in the direct circle, and keep what results, and let it be called the radiation of the direct circle. Then see the difference between the radiation of the region and the direct circle, by subtracting the smaller from the larger; multiply this difference by the distance of the planet from the ascending degree. And divide the product by half the nocturnal arc of the planet's degree, and the proportional part will emerge, which you add to the radiation of the region if it is less than the radiation of the direct circle, or subtract from the radiation of the region if it is greater than the radiation of the direct circle. And what results after addition or subtraction will be the place of the rays. I give an example in this: let the ascending degree be 18 Scorpio, Jupiter be in 10 Sagittarius. I seek the ascensions of the ascending degree in the horizon of 48 degrees, and I find 245 degrees, 55 minutes. Then I seek the ascensions of Jupiter's degree in the same oblique horizon, and I find 275 degrees, 27 minutes. I subtract the ascensions of the ascending degree from the ascensions of Jupiter's degree, and there remain 29 degrees, 32 minutes, which is the distance of Jupiter from the ascendant. Then I subtracted the ascensions of Jupiter's opposite degree from the ascensions of Jupiter's degree, and I halved the remainder, and I found 117 degrees, 9 minutes, which is half the nocturnal arc of Jupiter's degree. Then I added 60 degrees to Jupiter's ascensions, and 335 degrees, 27 minutes resulted, which I reduced to equal degrees in the oblique circle, and I found 12 degrees, 45 minutes of Aquarius. And this is the radiation of the region; so I kept it. Then I sought the ascensions of Jupiter's degree in the direct circle, and I found 338 degrees, 21 minutes, to which I added 60 degrees, and 398 degrees, 21 minutes resulted. I subtracted one revolution, i.e., 360 degrees, and 38 degrees, 21 minutes remained, which I reduced to equal degrees in the direct circle, and I found 50 degrees, 57 minutes of Aquarius; and this is the radiation of the direct circle. Then I subtracted the smaller radiation from the larger, and I found in the difference 6 degrees, 48 minutes, which difference I multiplied by the distance of Jupiter from the ascendant, and I divided by half the nocturnal arc, and I found in the proportional part 1 degree, 42 minutes, 47 seconds, which I subtracted from the radiation of the region, because that was greater. And after subtraction, there remained 11 degrees, 2 minutes, 13 seconds of Aquarius; and there is the place of Jupiter's rays. If, however, you wished to project the rays to the right, then you should see the distance of the planet's degree from the angle of the earth by the ascensions of the direct circle, which you will find by subtracting the ascensions of the planet's degree in the direct circle from the ascensions of the angle of the earth in the direct circle. And then you should add the proportional part of the two radiations to the radiation of the direct circle if it were less than the radiation of the region, or subtract if it were greater. In no other way does the operation differ, except where I instructed to add 60 degrees to the ascensions, etc., and to subtract 60, etc. If, however, the degree of the planet whose rays you wish to project is between the occidental degree and the degree of the 10th house, you will proceed with the opposite degree of the planet directly by the same way, as I have already said between the ascendant and the angle of the earth. For when the planet's degree is between the occidental degree and the midheaven, its opposite is between the ascendant and the angle of the earth.

author: Alchabitius
title: Calculating Planetary Rays Beyond the Angle
text: fitus gradus est inter afcendens zangulum terre. Et tantű diſtat gradus op pofitus planete ab aſcendéte quátum gradus planete a gradu occidétis:id eadem eſt operatio:nec plus nec minus. Sed poftqz cópleueris opationes locus radion est locus oppoſit illi loci qué inuçifti ficut tibi exéplificaui:qn planeta éin gradu occidétis. Si aut cötingat ficut fepe cotingit o grado planete e fit ante angulu: locvbi cadūt radij fui fit vitra anguli:ficut vbi gra loc planete fit iter medium celi ? afcendés z radij cadát vitra afcendens.f. inter afcendens zangulum terre. Jſte caſus öſi frequéter contingit in radias tione trina:z multotiens in quarta:z aliquádo in ſextili. Modus est ifte fi los cus planete cuius radios uis proijcere fit inter medium celi z afcendés z ra dij fui debeat cadere vitra afcedens:uide diſtatiaz planete a medio celi per modú pius dictú:z vide medietaté arcus diurni gradus planete p modum puus dictűzz ferua vtriqz.1.diftantiam a medietatem arcus diurni. Deinde gras aſcéfiones gradus planete in circulo directo. Quere ét afcefiones gra dus afcendétis in eodem circulo directo. Deinde fubtrahe afcenfiones gra dus planete ab afcenfionibus gradus afcendentis. Et qo remaſerit ferua:z vocetur pars radiationis circuli directi. Deinde qre afcenfiones grad plas nete in circulo obliquo.z quere afcenfiões gradus afcendétis in circulo obs liquo:z fubtrabe aſcenſiones planete ab afcenfionibus grad afcendentis:7 quod remáferit ferua a vocetur pars radiatiõis regionis. Deinde fubtrabe partem radiationis minorem a maiozi z remanet differétia qž multiplica p Diſtátiá planete a medio celi z pductú diuide p medietaté arcus diurni gra dus planete 7 pueniet po pportiõalis qž adde pti radiatõis circuli directi fi fuerit minor pte radiatióis regiõis.uel fubtrabe si fuerit maior:z o puenes rit erit po radiationis eäta:qps fi fuerit minor.60.gradibus radij ſextiles cadunt vitra anguluz afcendétis fi fuerit maior cadut ante angulü. Et fi bec ps fuerit minor.90 gradibus radij quarti cadut vltra angulu.fi maior cadut ante angulu. Et fibec po fuerit minori 20.gradi.ficut est quafi frequéter cae dunt radij trini ultra angulum.fi fuerit mai02.120.cadut ante angulu.Si igit cadant radij vltra angulum fubtrabe ptem radiatiõis equatá a.60.gra.pio radiatióe ſextili uel a.90.pro radiatione quarta:vela.i 20 p20 radiatione tri na:z q8 remanferit adde fup afcenfiones gradus afcendétis in circulo oblis quo a quod prouenerit reduc ad gradus equales in circulo obliquo 7 gras dus ſigni qui exierit erit locus radiop. Si ſciueris iftam operationé ſcies dis rigere planetá exiftétem in vna quadra ad locum exiftentem in alia quadra. Þúc modú nó vidi expoſitú nec pofitú in aliq libro. Ut planius pateat qu Dixi pona in hoc exemplu. Pono o gradus medij celi fit. io.gra.cancriz fa-turnus fit in quarto gradu virginis:7 gradus aſcédésin onzõte obliquo in regione cuius latitudo est. 48. gradus fit.7.gra8.59.minuta libre. Quefiui afcenfiones grad medij celi in circulo directo z inueni.i 90.gra.53.minuta. Deinde quefiui afcenfiones gradus ſaturni in circulo recto z inueni. 245. gradus 55.minuta Subtrari afcenfiones medij celi ab afcenfionibus gra. faturni z remáferűt.55.gradus.2.minuta: eſt diſtatia saturni a medio celt. Deinde fubtraxi afcenfiones gradus ſaturniin circulo obliquo ab afcenfio nibus gradus oppoſiti in eodé circulo obliquo a remaſerunt.203.gra.3.mis nuta:z hic fuit arcus diurnus gradus ſaturni qué mediaui z inueni.i o i.gra dus.3i.miuta.30.Fa que est medietas arcus diurni gradus ſaturni.Subtra xi aſcenſiones gradus ſaturni in circulo directo que fuerit.245.gra.55.mis nuta ab afcenſionibua gradus aſcendentis in eodem circulo directo q fues rút.277.gra.i 9. minuta z remaſerut.31.gra.24.minuta q fuit pars radiatios nis circuli directi. Deinde ſubtraxiaſcenſiones gradus ſaturni in circulo ob liquo q fuerit.i44 gra.277.miuta ab aſenſioib gra.aſcédétis in eode cir culo obliquo que fuerut.190.gra.53.minuta: remiſerunt.46.gra.26.mis nuta que fuerut pars radiationis regionis fiue circuli obliqui:vidi qö pars radiationis circuli obliqui fuit maior parte radiatióis circuli directi. Subs traxi ergo minoré de maiori z remiſerűt.i 5.gradus.2.minuta que fuit diffe rentia duarú partium quá multiplicauip diſſtanția ſaturni a medio celi zdi, uifip medietaté arcus diurni gra.ſaturni. 7 exiuerit.8.gra 8.minuta.59.fa z bec fuit pars proportionalis differentie quá addidi ſupra parte radiatio nis circuli directi z prouenerunt.39.gra.32.minuta.59.ka. Iſti ſunt gradus aſcenſionum qui fuerunt in figura a ſaturno vſqz ad gradum aſcendentem: z quia fuerűt pauciores q3.60.ſciui o radij ſextiles ſaturni in hac figura ces ciderunt.vltra gradum aſcendétem. Subtraxi ergo hos gra.a.60.pioradia tione ſextili z remaſerűt.20.gradus.27.minuta. 1.Fm:quos addidi ſuper af cenſiones gra.aſcendétis in circulo obliquo qui fuerunt. i 90.gra.53.minus ta:z puenerunt.2 ii.gradus.20.minuta quos reduriad gradus equales in circulo obliquo z inueni.22.gra.54 minuta. 52. Fa libre:ibi fuerit radij ſexti les ſaturni in propofito exemplo km hunc modum debet fieri in alijs angus lis. Et bec de tadiationibus ſufficiant. A dent etiam quinqz planete in uno quoqz ſigno terminos.pau terminauit de tribus dignitatibus eéntialibus planetay. In iſta parte auctor determinat de quarta dignitate eſſentiali qua babent planete in ſignis. Et diuidit in duas ptes:qm in ima parte ponit termios planetay. In ſecúda parte ſoluit quadam cótrauerſia q eft inter aliquos de triplicitati bus z terminis. Scraps incipit ibi. Quidá pponit. Prima ps diuidi pot in duas ptes:ita opmo incipit tractare de termis planetap Iraliter. Scdo pos nit tabula de ipfis terminis.Secuda incipit ibiz pp diuerſitaté eorudez. Dic pmoq quos planete.f.Saturnus Iupiter Mars Uen z Mercuri bit ter mios diſpoſitos p diuerfos gra. Et pmus terminé Iouis a pncipio ariet v o ad fine ſexti gra.eiufdé. Et a pncipio ſeptimi gra.eiufdé ariets vis ad fine 12.eft termin Ueneris. Et a pncipio.i3.gra.vſqz ad fine.20.eft termin Mer curij. Et a pncipio.21.vfqs ad fine.25.est terminus Martis. Et a pucipio.26. viq ad fine Arietis est termin Saturni. Poftea dic:z quiſti grad füt diuerfi: ita o non uadút equaliter nec fm ordiné planetay graue effer eos tenere in memoria:ideo poſuit eos eo in tabula ini videtur in littera. e iij
translation: The degree is between the ascendant and the angle of the earth. And the degree opposite the planet is as far from the ascendant as the degree of the planet is from the degree of the occident: that is the same operation: neither more nor less. But after you have completed the operations, the place of the rays is the place opposite to that place which you found, as I exemplified to you: when the planet is in the degree of the occident. But if it happens, as it often happens, that the degree of the planet is before the angle: the place where its rays fall is beyond the angle: as where the degree of the planet is between the midheaven and the ascendant and the rays fall beyond the ascendant, that is, between the ascendant and the angle of the earth. This case occurs almost frequently in trine radiation: and often in quartile: and sometimes in sextile. The method is this: if the place of the planet whose rays you wish to project is between the midheaven and the ascendant and its rays should fall beyond the ascendant: see the distance of the planet from the midheaven by the method previously described: and see half the diurnal arc of the planet's degree by the method previously described and keep both, that is, the distance and half the diurnal arc. Then find the ascensions of the planet's degree in the direct circle. Also find the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the same direct circle. Then subtract the ascensions of the planet's degree from the ascensions of the ascendant's degree. And what remains, keep: and let it be called the part of the radiation of the direct circle. Then find the ascensions of the planet's degree in the oblique circle. And find the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the oblique circle: and subtract the ascensions of the planet from the ascensions of the ascendant's degree: and what remains, keep and let it be called the part of the radiation of the region. Then subtract the smaller part of the radiation from the larger and the difference remains, which multiply by the distance of the planet from the midheaven and divide the product by half the diurnal arc of the planet's degree and a proportional part will result, which add to the part of the radiation of the direct circle if it is smaller than the part of the radiation of the region. Or subtract if it is larger: and what results will be the equated part of the radiation: which if it is less than 60 degrees, the sextile rays fall beyond the angle; if it is greater, they fall before the angle. And if this part is less than 90 degrees, the quartile rays fall beyond the angle; if greater, they fall before the angle. And if this part is less than 120 degrees, as is almost frequently the case, the trine rays fall beyond the angle; if it is greater than 120, they fall before the angle. If therefore the rays fall beyond the angle, subtract the equated part of the radiation from 60 degrees for sextile radiation, or from 90 for quartile radiation: or from 120 for trine radiation: and what remains, add to the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the oblique circle and what results, reduce to equal degrees in the oblique circle and the degree of the sign that comes out will be the place of the rays. If you know this operation, you will know how to direct a planet existing in one quadrant to a place existing in another quadrant. I have not seen this method explained or placed in any book. So that what I have said may be clearer, I will put it in this example. I assume that the degree of the midheaven is 10 degrees Cancer and Saturn is in the fourth degree of Virgo: and the ascendant's degree in the oblique horizon in a region whose latitude is 48 degrees is 7 degrees 59 minutes Libra. I sought the ascensions of the midheaven's degree in the direct circle and found 190 degrees 53 minutes. Then I sought the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the direct circle and found 245 degrees 55 minutes. I subtracted the ascensions of the midheaven from the ascensions of Saturn's degree and 55 degrees 2 minutes remained: this is the distance of Saturn from the midheaven. Then I subtracted the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the oblique circle from the ascensions of the opposite degree in the same oblique circle and 203 degrees 3 minutes remained: and this was the diurnal arc of Saturn's degree which I bisected and found 101 degrees 31 minutes 30 seconds, which is half the diurnal arc of Saturn's degree. I subtracted the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the direct circle which were 245 degrees 55 minutes from the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the same direct circle which were 277 degrees 19 minutes and 31 degrees 24 minutes remained which was the part of the radiation of the direct circle. Then I subtracted the ascensions of Saturn's degree in the oblique circle which were 144 degrees 277 minutes from the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the same oblique circle which were 190 degrees 53 minutes: and 46 degrees 26 minutes remained which was the part of the radiation of the region or oblique circle: I saw that the part of the radiation of the oblique circle was greater than the part of the radiation of the direct circle. I therefore subtracted the smaller from the larger and 15 degrees 2 minutes remained which was the difference of the two parts which I multiplied by the distance of Saturn from the midheaven and divided by half the diurnal arc of Saturn's degree. And 8 degrees 8 minutes 59 seconds resulted and this was the proportional part of the difference which I added to the part of the radiation of the direct circle and 39 degrees 32 minutes 59 seconds resulted. These are the degrees of ascensions which were in the figure from Saturn to the ascendant's degree: and because they were less than 60, I knew that the sextile rays of Saturn in this figure fell beyond the ascendant's degree. I therefore subtracted these degrees from 60 for sextile radiation and 20 degrees 27 minutes 1 second remained: which I added to the ascensions of the ascendant's degree in the oblique circle which were 190 degrees 53 minutes: and 211 degrees 20 minutes resulted which I reduced to equal degrees in the oblique circle and found 22 degrees 54 minutes 52 seconds Libra: there were the sextile rays of Saturn in the proposed example. This method should be used for other angles. And let this suffice concerning radiations. Also, the five planets have terms in each sign. He has finished with the three essential dignities of the planets. In this part, the author determines the fourth essential dignity which the planets have in the signs. And he divides it into two parts: because in the first part he places the terms of the planets. In the second part he resolves a certain controversy which exists among some concerning triplicities and terms. The second part begins there. Some propose. The first part can be divided into two parts: so that first he begins to treat of the terms of the planets generally. Secondly, he places a table of these terms. The second part begins there because of the diversity of the same. First, he says that the planets, that is, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury have terms arranged by different degrees. And the first term of Jupiter is from the beginning of Aries up to the end of the sixth degree of the same. And from the beginning of the seventh degree of the same Aries up to the end of the 12th is the term of Venus. And from the beginning of the 13th degree up to the end of the 20th is the term of Mercury. And from the beginning of the 21st up to the end of the 25th is the term of Mars. And from the beginning of the 26th up to the end of Aries is the term of Saturn. Afterwards he says: and these degrees are diverse: so that they do not proceed equally nor according to the order of the planets, it would be difficult to keep them in memory: therefore he placed them in a table as appears in the text. e iij

author: Alchabitius
title: Triplicities and Terms
text: Quidá preponunt triplicitates termi ſoluit dá contrauerfia que eſt iter ſapientes de triplicitatib z terminis.Et dicit qda pferút triplicitates terminis. Et qda pferút terminos triplicitatib Soluit iſta ptrauerſia z dicit o dni triplicitatů ſunt fortiores qitum ad nuse tritioné puerozi:qz in natiuitatib ſignificat nutritioné z ab eis accipiť ſignis icatio vtrű puer ſit vitalis aut nó. Dni aút terminoy funt fortiores in Directi one:qe du dirigit gradus aſcédés alicui natiuitatis dirigit ad terminos for tunaru? infortunaru3.7 Dris termini illi vocat diuifor vel diſpoſitoz ozdin durat directio in illo termino:z ſi fuerit ibi aliqs planeta vel radij ei erit ptis ceps. De hoc diffuſi dicet in ſequétiboño pcedéte. Notádú est hic de ter miniso diſcordia fuit iter ſapiétes antiquos de terminis.Et fuerút tres opi niones famoſe de terminis. quarū vna fuit indoy.alia egytiozu.tertia caldeo ru.-ptoleme in.218.ppofitione pme ptis ädripartiti magis approbat termi nos egyriou z dicit ſe inueniffe vnú libaú de terminis antiqui qui p uetusta te erat pro maiori pre fact. Et ille cocordabat cú terminis egyptioru:z in il lo libro ſumma graduű terminorū cuiuslibet planete erat tai quát numer annorú ſuoz maior quos dant in natiuitatib. Uerbi gra:anni maiores ſass turni ſunt.57.ani. Iouis.79.Martis.66.Ueneris.82. Mercurij.76.2 oés iſti collecti faciút.360. Si volueris hoc experiri.cõputa gradus oium termi nop alicui planete:verbi gra Iouis:z inuenies numeru annoy ſuorum ſcie licet.79.2 ſic de alijs. Daly in cóméto. 206.ppofitionis pmeptis ödripartiti dicit. Illi de egypto fuerunt ſapiétes magici qui fuerunt ab antiquo tpe.naz iſti fuerút ſtudij z exercicij magniin ſciétijs z ſapientijs oibus de qbus hõre iuuare pot. Et ſcim hoc per chronicas ſapiétum antiquor km o loqueban tur de ipfis a per ea que de ſuis operib? remaſerút a multis milioannouz vis ad hodierna dié. In ego dico illud & ego vidi de vna experiétia qž inue ni in loco qui dict eft oculus ſolis:q2 in modico tpe miracula magna vidi. bec funt verba Paly.de experiétia aût tacet. In cétiloquio in cóméto illius p poſitióis vultus hui ſeculi.dicit vná experiétia de ſigillo ſcorpionis facto in lapide km o pót ibi videri:z qui voluerit videre ibide videat. A cies autem ſignorú. Dic determinat de guta dignitate z vle tima eéntiali qž bit planete in ſignis q vocat facies. Et pot diuis di in tres ptes:qm in ima pre ponit diuiſioné z ordinatione facie rum. In secunda pte docet iuenire facies per numeros. In tertia pte ponit de eis tabula. Scda pars icipit. Lú babueris grad in alquo figno. Zertia eſt in fine vbi ponit tabula.Dicity qolibet ſignú diuidiť in tres pres eğles vocant tres facies 7 õlibet facies est ex.io.gradib. Et icipiüt iſte fa cies a pncipio arietis z predut 13 ordine ſignora planetap.ita opma facies eſt martis.f.pmi.io.gradus.fcoa facies arietis.f.io.gradus ſequêtes ſunt ſos lis qui ſuccedit marti in ordie ſphaerap.ſtia eſt veneris ſuccedit ſoli. Et p ma facics tauri eſt mercurij que ſuccedit veneri. Secüda lune que ſuccedit mercurio. Tertia ſaturni. Et pma geminoy Iouis.z ſic ſecudum húc ordiné vios ad fine ſignon. Poftea docet luenire per numeros cui planete fit aliq facies. Et dicit cu babueris gradú alicui ſigni z volueris ſcire cuius planete facies fit in illo gradu cóputa numerú ſignozi a pncipio arietis vis ad in: cipiú figni ppofiti:z multiplica illú numerum per tres:z cú numero pducto adde facies cópletas figni ppofiti:verbi graſitranfiuerint.io.gradus com pleti adde vnitaté.Si.20.completi adde duo. Deinde a toto numero ſubtras be.7.quoties poteris. qo remáſerit computa a marte im ordiné planetay. z vbi numerus finit fuerit ſequés planeta eſt dñs faciei queſite:verbi gratia pono qu babeam.is.gradus leonis volo ſcire cuius facies fit.is.gradus le onis ſcio e a principio arietis funt.4.figna completa.multiplicabo ergo 4.per.3.7 erunt.1 2. Et ſcio y de leone tranfiuit vna facies completa:ió adda vnum z erunt.iz facies cóplete a pncipio arietis.ſubtraba inde ſepté z rema nét.6.dabo.pmam vnitatem marti.fecundam foli.tertia veneri.quartá mercurio.gutamlune.fextam ſaturno. Erit ergo facies ſequés que non fuit com pleta Iouis. Poftea ponit tabulam cuius opus facile est. Queres eni nomé illins igni cui dominú faciei queris z inuenies in directo quis fit dominus pme faciei z go ſecüde z quis tertie. I quia auxiliante deo ofte terminauit de dis gnitatibus planetari in ſignis. In iſta pte determinat de fortitu, dinibus quas babét in iſſtis dignitatib. Et pot diuidi in duas par testqm in pma pte oftendit quot fortitudines beat planeta in älis bet dignitate. In ka pre cóparat iſtas dignitates ad res ſéfibiles.fa est ibi:vn gda de hac re. Prima pars pot diuidi in duas:qm pmo continuat ſe ad dice da.fo pleqtur ibina dis dom. Dicit qe adiuuate deo cópleuit de dignitatis boplanetay in ſignis.mo vult ondere quot fortitudines beat glibet in ipfis di gnitatib. Poftea dicit q dis dom het groß fortitudies.dis exaltatióis hét attuor.dis triplicitatis bet tres.dng termini duas.dis faciei vna. Poftea re plicat illa ptrouerfia de terminis. Et ponit cóparationé in dignitatib plane tarū dicés q qda de dignitatib planetarű fecerut talé cóparationé o plane ta exiſtés in ſua domo eſt filis viro exiſtéti in domo atqz driatide fua. Et plane ta in exaltatide ſua eſt filis viro i regno atqs gloria ſua z patet in littera. [Ex iſta littera pót notari fi aliqo planeta patiať magnú ifortuniú in domo vel ex altatióe ſua boc est multú deteſtabile.ficut multú cét deteſtabile ſirex vinces ret in ppio regno ſuo z in fortiori loco regni ſui. Jó qi luiaria eclypsank in ppijs domibus vel exaltationib? fuis hoc est malum ſignum.dropiet S Equuntur bas figureſignoruz. Pius auctor determinauit de dignitatibº planetari in ſignis z de fortitudinibas bit in iſtis dignitatib. In iſta pre determiat de figuris ſiue formis ipfor ſignor hoc eſt oftendit nobis que ſigna bit formas bumanasiz que bit formas alioy animalium.7 manet ins dluiſa.Dicit qo quedam ſigna dicunt rōnalia.filla quorumimagines funt fi gurate ad imagies homini.Et illa ſunt gemini virgo aquari libza z medies
translation: Some prefer triplicities to terms, which resolves the controversy among the wise regarding triplicities and terms. And it says that some prefer triplicities to terms, and some prefer terms to triplicities. This controversy is resolved by saying that the lords of triplicities are stronger as regards the nourishment of children, because in nativities they signify nourishment, and from them is taken the signification of whether the child will be vital or not. The lords of the terms, however, are stronger in direction, because when the ascending degree of any nativity is directed, it is directed to the terms of fortunes and misfortunes. And the lord of that term is called the divider or disposer of the order as long as the direction endures in that term; and if there is any planet or its rays there, it will be a participant. More will be said about this in the following, not preceding. It should be noted here concerning the terms that there was disagreement among the ancient wise men about the terms. And there were three famous opinions about the terms, one of which was of the Indians, another of the Egyptians, and the third of the Chaldeans. Ptolemy, in the 218th proposition of the first part of the Quadripartite, more approves the terms of the Egyptians and says that he found an old book of terms which, due to its age, was for the most part made. And that agreed with the terms of the Egyptians; and in that book, the sum of the degrees of the terms of each planet was as great as the number of its greater years which they give in nativities. For example: the greater years of Saturn are 57 years, of Jupiter 79, of Mars 66, of Venus 82, of Mercury 76. And all these collected make 360. If you wish to experience this, compute the degrees of all the terms of any planet, for example, Jupiter, and you will find the number of its years, namely 79. And so for the others. Daly, in the commentary on the 206th proposition of the first part of the Quadripartite, says: Those of Egypt were wise magicians who were from ancient times, for these were of great study and exercise in all sciences and wisdoms by which man could be helped. And we know this through the chronicles of the ancient wise men, according to what they spoke of them, and through what remains of their works for many thousands of years up to the present day. I therefore say what I saw from an experience that I found in a place called the eye of the sun, because in a short time I saw great miracles. These are the words of Paly. But he is silent about the experience. In the Centiloquium, in the commentary on that proposition, 'the face of this age,' he mentions an experience of a scorpion seal made in stone, as can be seen there; and whoever wishes to see it, let him see it there. And the faces of the signs. Here he determines concerning the dignity and the ultimate essential that a planet has in the signs, which is called faces. And it can be divided into three parts: because in the first part he places the division and ordering of the faces. In the second part he teaches how to find the faces by numbers. In the third part he places a table for them. The second part begins: 'When you have a degree in any sign.' The third is at the end where he places the table. He says that every sign is divided into three equal parts called three faces, and each face is of 10 degrees. And these faces begin from the beginning of Aries and proceed in the order of the signs of the planets, so that the first face is of Mars, that is, the first 10 degrees. The second face of Aries, that is, the next 10 degrees, are of the Sun, who succeeds Mars in the order of the spheres. The third is of Venus, who succeeds the Sun. And the first face of Taurus is of Mercury, who succeeds Venus. The second of the Moon, who succeeds Mercury. The third of Saturn. And the first of Gemini of Jupiter. And so according to this order up to the end of the signs. Afterwards he teaches how to find by numbers to which planet any face belongs. And he says, when you have a degree of any sign and wish to know whose planet's face it is in that degree, compute the number of signs from the beginning of Aries up to the beginning of the proposed sign; and multiply that number by three; and to the product add the complete faces of the proposed sign. For example, if 10 complete degrees have passed, add one. If 20 complete, add two. Then from the total number subtract 7 as many times as you can. What remains, count from Mars in the order of the planets. And where the number ends, the following planet is the lord of the sought face. For example, I have 15 degrees of Leo. I want to know whose face the 15th degree of Leo is. I know that from the beginning of Aries there are 4 complete signs. I will therefore multiply 4 by 3, and it will be 12. And I know that one complete face has passed in Leo; therefore I will add one, and it will be 13 complete faces from the beginning of Aries. I will subtract 7 from that, and 6 will remain. I will give the first unit to Mars, the second to the Sun, the third to Venus, the fourth to Mercury, the fifth to the Moon, the sixth to Saturn. Therefore, the following face, which was not complete, will be of Jupiter. Afterwards he places a table whose use is easy. For you will seek the name of that sign whose face's lord you seek, and you will find directly who is the lord of the first face, and who of the second, and who of the third. And because, with God's help, he has now finished concerning the dignities of the planets in the signs. In this part he determines concerning the strengths they have in these dignities. And it can be divided into two parts: because in the first part he shows how many strengths a planet has in each dignity. In the second part he compares these dignities to sensible things. There is a certain saying about this matter. The first part can be divided into two: because first he continues to speak. He says that, with God's help, he has completed concerning the dignities of the planets in the signs. Now he wants to show how many strengths each has in these dignities. Afterwards he says that the lord of the house has great strengths. The lord of exaltation has four. The lord of triplicity has three. The lord of the term has two. The lord of the face has one. Afterwards he repeats that controversy about the terms. And he places a comparison in the dignities of the planets, saying that some have made such a comparison of the dignities of the planets that a planet existing in its house is like a man existing in his own house and dominion. And a planet in its exaltation is like a man in his kingdom and glory, and it is clear in the text. From this text it can be noted that if any planet suffers great misfortune in its house or exaltation, this is very detestable. Just as it would be very detestable if a king were conquered in his own kingdom and in the strongest place of his kingdom. Therefore, if luminaries are eclipsed in their own houses or exaltations, this is a bad sign. He will speak of this. The figures of the signs follow these. The previous author determined concerning the dignities of the planets in the signs and concerning the strengths they have in these dignities. In this part he determines concerning the figures or forms of these signs, that is, he shows us which signs have human forms and which have forms of other animals. And it remains undivided. He says that some signs are called rational, those whose images are figured to the images of men. And these are Gemini, Virgo, Aquarius, Libra, and half of Sagittarius.

author: Alchabitius
title: Sign Classifications and Terrestrial Divisions
text: tas pma ſagittarij. z iſta dicunt habentia pulchras voces.Et virtus comm eſt maior cum fuerint in oriéte. Et queda ex ſignis dicunt habétia balas.f.ge mini virgo z piſcis. Queda ex eis dicunt quaprupedia.f.ſagittarius leo ari es taurus z capricorn. Et eop quedas ſunt domeſtica.filla que funt figuras ta ſecúdum figuras aialium domeſticorum. Et hec ſunt aries taurus z capai cornus.Et eoruni virt magis apparet cum fuerint in meridie. Uirtus autē virginis capricorni z aquarij magis apparet cum fuerint in ſeptétrione. Et quedam ex ſignis vident vitioſa z turpia z totuofa.f.aries taur cancer ſcoz pius z piſcis.z virtus eorum magis apparet in occidéte. Quedaz vero funt iterilia.f.gemini leo virgo.Et quedam dicuntur habentia paucos filios.f.ari es taurus libra ſagittarius capricornus 7 aquarius.Et quedas ex his ſignis ſunt multum luxurioſa.f.aries z taurus leo z capricomus.Et quedam dicus tur multum filiorum ſcilicet cancer ſcorpius z piſcis. Et quedam ex ſignis Dicuntur habentia dimidiam vocem:illa ſcilicet que habent vocem anima lium balantium rugientiam z mugientium:vt aries taurus leo capricornus z vltima pars ſagittarij. Quedam ex ſignis dicuntur carentia omnino voce Cilla que figurantur ſecundum imagines animalium carentium voce:vt cá cer ſcorpio z piſcis. Daly abenragel breuiter expedit ſe deformis ſignorum dicens.Signorum alia ſunt in forma bominum. Alia in forma beſtiaró.Alia in forma beſtinglorum Alia in forma reptilium. Que funt in forma humana ſunt gemini libra aquari virgo. In forma beſtiap aries taurus capricorn z ſagittari. In forma beſtinglo.i.aialium rapatium leo. In forma reptilium cancer ſcorpio z piſcis. I unum quodque ſignorum ba bet propriam ſignificatione. tor determinauit de figuris ſignori. In iſta pre oftédit nobis quid babet ſigna ex membris hominum:z ex regionibus z ex planetis. Et pot dis uidi in duas ptes:quoniam in pma parte facit quod dictum est. In ſecunda Determinat de doloribus planetarum in ſignis, Secunda incipit ibi. Et fig dem planete ſignificauerint dolorem. Prima pot diuidi in.i2.partes ſecúduz 9.12.ſunt ſigna.Claru est qo dicit in littera excepto o nomia regionú nó ſunt nobis nota. Notandi est hic qs Ptolemens diuidit tota terram habitabis lem in õttuor pres. Et ſecúdú diuifionem Ptolemeiimaginari pót vna linea tranſiens ab oriéte in occidéte per mediű babitationis ita qö iſta linea dinis dat partem meridionalem a ſeptétrionali. Et dicit baly o iſta linea diſtatab egnoctiali per.36.gradus.z ſecundú banc diuiſioné diſtat ab vltima habita tione in ſeptentrione per.30.gradus vel circa:ita quod tota latitudo contine ar.66.gradus. Et tata eſt diſtatia egnoctialis vsqz ad circulú arcticú:imo eſt 66.gradus.26.minuta:7.30.ka. Ideo dicit baly vel circa. Et dicit quod infine iſtius linee verſus occidentez eſt paſſagiű herculis. Et hoc est vnde tranſitur in hiſpaniam z est mare ibi ſtricta quod exiſtens in vno littore poteſt videre exiſtété in alio, St ſunt ibi tres iſule qrú ynā vidét trăſeutes in hiſpaniam per terra occidétis. Et ſtat in ea idolű tenés in manu claues ad demoſtrandum gvitra locum illú nó fit babitatio. Et in alibet duarum aliarum inſularú ſtat idolum ſcom eŭdé modú. Et in pre oriétali in fine linee pdicte ftant idola eos dem modo tenentia claues 7 demonſtrantia quod non fit vltra illum locú ba bitatio verſus orienté.Dicit Daly & Percules poſuit iſta idola in ſignunı g ipſe acquiſiuerat totú mundum.Longitudo linee pdicte ſcoum dicit Paly eſt iso.graduu. Dis viſis im ſententia -Ptolemei imaginet iſta linea diuidi p medium ita qo remaneat.90.gradus verſus oiétem:r alij.90.verſus occide tem. Et imaginet tranſire yna linea per polos mundi permediú diuiſionis lis nee ſupradicte ita videlicet y tranfeat per medium terre babitabilis ſcom la titudinem. Prima auté linea dicebat tranſire per medium terre habitabilis Em longitudiné. Pater itaqs o due linee ſupradicte que diuidunt tota terraz babitabilem in attuor ptes cõiungunt in loco interfectionis linearuz qui eſt punctus in medio terre babitabilis, bac diuifive intellecta facile est videre Iniam. Ptolemei in pritide ſignop z terraru. Dicititas Ptoleme obec tria figna aries leo z ſagittarius que faciût pmam triplicitatem ptinét ad ärtam prem terre babitabilis que eſt iter ſeptétrioné z occidés.z illá örtá gubernat Iupiter eo q est ſeptétrionalis:z mars hét pticipatione cum eo:q2 ptinet ad ptem occidétalé. Et tria ſigna triplicitatis ſecúde.ſtaurus virgo capricomus ptinét ad ptem que est inter meridiem z owens:7 gubernat ipſam venus:q2 eſt meridionalis.z hét pticipationem cum ea ſaturn? qm ad ptem oriétalez ptinet. Et tria ſigna triplicitatis tertie.f.gemini libra z aqrius ptinet ad ptem que est inter oues z ſeptétrioné:z gubernat ipſam ſaturnus eo q pertinet ad oriens z habet pricipationé cuz co Iupiter eo q pertinet ad ſeptentrionem. Et tria ſigna triplicitatis quarte.f.cacer ſcorpio z piſcis pertinet ad partem que est inter meridié z occidés.z gubernat ipſam mars:qe pertinet ad occis dens.z habet pticipationem cum eo ven. Ex predictis poterit glibet conies cturare ad qö ſignum ptineat qlibet terra. Sciet enim per longitudinez zla *titudiné regionis de qua ärta ſit. Uerbi gratia:ois terra cui latitudo eſt ma io2.36.gradibus.zlógitudo ei ab occidete min.go.gradib fuerit est de är ta ſeptétrionali occidentali z ſic de alijs ſuo modo.Lognita igitur quarta in qua est terra tua ſcis quod vnum ſignum ex tribus ſignis triplicitatis illing arte reſpicit illa terra3.7 poteris ſcire quod ex illis tribus reſpiciat ea per mo res 7 cóſuetudines hoium habitátiű in ea. Uerbi gra:ſcis o terra chriſtiano rű eſt in pre ſeptétrionali occidentali ſcio quod aries leo z ſagittarius dnatur ſup ipſam. Et ſi velim ſcire ſupra o pté eius diak qolibet iſtop ſignozu reſpi cio ad mores r códitiões boim cum qbus ſignis magnis cócordat.Conſtat aute gromani ſunt famoſiores a nobiliores iſtius ärte video conuenit vt at tribuať eis fignú nobilius inter bec tria.z hoc eſt leo domus ſolis. Lofkat ét almani ſunt homines iradūdi z furioſi de natura martis:ideo cóuenit vt attribuat eis aries Domus martis. Conſtat etiaz y hiſpani ſunt boni milites exercitij magni in armis:ideo cóuenit vt attribuak eis ſagittari qo eſt ſigni militiarű z belloy. Et hanc ptitione ponit Ptoleme in littera in ſecunda par te quadripartiti.Dicit enim ſic z prouincie cu ariete acordates ſunt terra bú
translation: the last part of Sagittarius. And these are said to have beautiful voices. And their virtue is greater when they are in the East. And some of the signs are said to have voices, namely Gemini, Virgo, and Pisces. Some of them are said to be quadrupedal, namely Sagittarius, Leo, Aries, Taurus, and Capricorn. And some of these are domestic, meaning those that are figured according to the shapes of domestic animals. And these are Aries, Taurus, and Capricorn. And their virtue appears more when they are in the South. But the virtue of Virgo, Capricorn, and Aquarius appears more when they are in the North. And some of the signs are seen as vicious, ugly, and tortuous, namely Aries, Taurus, Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. And their virtue appears more in the West. Some, however, are sterile, namely Gemini, Leo, Virgo. And some are said to have few children, namely Aries, Taurus, Libra, Sagittarius, Capricorn, and Aquarius. And some of these signs are very luxurious, namely Aries, Taurus, Leo, and Capricorn. And some are said to have many children, namely Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. And some of the signs are said to have a half-voice, namely those that have the voice of roaring and bellowing animals, such as Aries, Taurus, Leo, Capricorn, and the last part of Sagittarius. Some of the signs are said to be entirely voiceless, namely those that are figured according to the images of voiceless animals, such as Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. Daly Abenragel briefly explains the forms of the signs, saying: Some signs are in the form of humans. Others in the form of beasts. Others in the form of wild beasts. Others in the form of reptiles. Those in human form are Gemini, Libra, Aquarius, Virgo. In the form of beasts are Aries, Taurus, Capricorn, and Sagittarius. In the form of wild beasts, i.e., predatory animals, is Leo. In the form of reptiles are Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. Each of the signs has its own signification. Ptolemy determined the figures of the signs. In this, he shows us what the signs have from the limbs of humans, and from regions, and from planets. And it can be divided into two parts: because in the first part he does what has been said. In the second, he determines the pains of the planets in the signs. The second begins there: And the figure of the planet will signify pain. The first can be divided into 12 parts according to the 12 signs. It is clear what he says in the text, except that the names of the regions are not known to us. It is to be noted here that Ptolemy divides the entire habitable earth into four parts. And according to Ptolemy's division, one can imagine a line passing from East to West through the middle of the habitation, so that this line divides the southern part from the northern. And Daly says that this line is distant from the equator by 36 degrees. And according to this division, it is distant from the last habitation in the North by 30 degrees or so, so that the total width contains 66 degrees. And such is the distance from the equator to the arctic circle: indeed it is 66 degrees, 26 minutes, and 30 seconds. Therefore Daly says "or so." And he says that at the end of this line towards the West is the passage of Hercules. And this is where one passes into Spain, and there is a narrow sea there, so that one standing on one shore can see one standing on the other. There are three islands there, one of which is seen by those passing into Spain through the western land. And on it stands an idol holding keys in its hand to show that beyond that place there is no habitation. And on each of the other two islands stands an idol in the same manner. And in the eastern part, at the end of the aforesaid line, stand idols in the same manner, holding keys and showing that beyond that place there is no habitation towards the East. Daly says that Hercules placed these idols as a sign that he had acquired the whole world. The length of the aforesaid line, according to Daly, is 180 degrees. Having seen these things, in Ptolemy's opinion, imagine this line divided in the middle so that 90 degrees remain towards the East, and another 90 towards the West. And imagine a line passing through the poles of the world through the middle of the division of the aforesaid line, so that it passes through the middle of the habitable earth according to latitude. The first line, however, was said to pass through the middle of the habitable earth according to longitude. It is clear, therefore, that the two aforesaid lines, which divide the entire habitable earth into four parts, join at the intersection of the lines, which is a point in the middle of the habitable earth. With this division understood, it is easy to see Ptolemy's intention in the division of signs and lands. Thus Ptolemy says that these three signs, Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius, which form the first triplicity, belong to the fourth part of the habitable earth, which is between the North and the West. And Jupiter governs that fourth part because he is northern; and Mars has participation with him because he belongs to the western part. And the three signs of the second triplicity, namely Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn, belong to the part that is between the South and the East; and Venus governs it because she is southern; and Saturn has participation with her because he belongs to the eastern part. And the three signs of the third triplicity, namely Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius, belong to the part that is between the East and the North; and Saturn governs it because he belongs to the East and Jupiter has participation with him because he belongs to the North. And the three signs of the fourth triplicity, namely Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, belong to the part that is between the South and the West; and Mars governs it because he belongs to the West; and Venus has participation with him. From the foregoing, anyone can conjecture to which sign any land belongs. For he will know by the longitude and latitude of the region to which quarter it belongs. For example: any land whose latitude is greater than 36 degrees and whose longitude from the West is less than 90 degrees is of the north-western quarter, and so on for the others in their own way. Therefore, having known the quarter in which your land is, you know that one sign out of the three signs of the triplicity of that quarter regards that land. And you will be able to know which of those three regards it by the customs and habits of the people living in it. For example: you know that the land of Christians is in the north-western part, so you know that Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius rule over it. And if I want to know over which part of it each of these signs rules, I look at the customs and conditions of the people with which great signs they agree. It is clear that the Romans are the most famous and noblest of this quarter, so it is fitting that the noblest sign among these three be attributed to them, and this is Leo, the house of the Sun. It is also clear that the Germans are irascible and furious men of the nature of Mars, so it is fitting that Aries, the house of Mars, be attributed to them. It is also clear that the Spanish are good soldiers, greatly exercised in arms, so it is fitting that Sagittarius, which is the sign of military affairs and wars, be attributed to them. And Ptolemy places this division in the text in the second part of the Quadripartitum. For he says thus: and the provinces agreeing with Aries are the land of the...

author: Alchabitius
title: Regions and Climates
text: tanie z terra de ſalacia.Credo & deberet dici de ſcalauia z germaia. Et q cos cordat cu leone fut:italia:gallia:apulia:z cecilia. Et Daly dicit ibi: bi fütter mini rome. Et q cócordát cú ſagittario funt terre de turcia calciata z biſpa nia. De pritióe alior ſignop cu alijs terris pay curo. Quia nec noia eay пес editões boiuz füt mibi nota nec faceret mibi aliquá vtilitate & fcire. Sigf aut cognofcat eas a noluerit in eis mozari applicet im modú dem in tribofi gnis pdict. Dicit Abraba avenezreq pmű clyma eſt capricomi z aqrij:z gu bernator eius est faturnus. Scom clyma eſt ſagittarij z piſcis z gubernator ei'eſt Jupiter. Zertiú clyma eſt ſcorpij z arietis z gubernator eiveſt Mars. Quartű est leonis a gubeanator eis eſt ſol. Quintú clyma eſt tauri z libre z ei gubernator é uenus. Sextú eft geminoré z uirgis 2 ei gubernator mers curi. Septimű est cácri z gubernatillo luna. Abraba auenezre ponit có ditiones naſcentiú fub qlibet ſigno zé ſnia sua ista. Qui nat fuerit fub ima facie ariets erit ruffus z fim: véter ei erit puz strict carne extenuat?:bébit o fupra pedé finiſty fignú z in cubito man ſiniſtre:z amici eierűt plures 2 ipe odiet malú. Qui vo nat fuerit fub fa facie ierit ei nigredo plchritudi fa ciei cõiūcta:corp'eins tépatű pceps in irá aio occultás volūtaté mala: estos alti cordis:inimici ei plures erut. Qui nat fuerit fub tertia facie ei? erit ruf fus color ei? z croce é erit ſolitari: Qui nat fuerit fub pma facie tauri erit breuis ſtature:fui oculi magni:labia ſpiſſa:z bébit fignú in collo:z é corde fe ftin:amici ei plures: manerieb delitian delectabit ipe. Nat aut fub facie ei pa:bébit facié rotűdá: vétré latű:ockos pulchros: 7 eisaia volūtaría reſqs cito ppédet:z circa ſpatulas pilofus exiftet.z bébit fignú in lúbis. Et q nat? fuerit fub tertia facie pulchri coloris exiſtet pulchregs facieisz bēbit fignú in oculo finiftro:virqz laborioſus erit.nec in mulierib?fortunat exiſtet. Qui aut nat fuerit fub fine bui ſigni erit carés teſticulis naturals a fine vel bermos frodit. Qui nat fuerit fub pma facie gemiop erit pulchri corpis z decétis: z oculop z pilozihebit fignú in capite vel in oculis. Uisus ei acut minime é iracudus eritqz laborator z cu mulierib minime fortunat exiftet. Et q nat fuerit sub scoa facie erit breuis ſtature a niger z ſup eius cubitú fignú nigres dinis:eiuſog iba pulchra z dulcia. Natus fub tertia facie nimiű bebit oculos paruos:eritqz vir fufpicator 7 lenis:7loquek dba irratōabilia:critos médar. Qui natus fuerit fub pma facie cancri erit coupe z palis decens: fupcilia ei? erût ſtricta nareſs longe a ſpatule late:z babebit fignú in cubito dextro:aut eius brachio:z aia eius bona:amici eius plures: eritqz ſciens z ingeniofus. Qui natus fuerit sub scoa faciezerit color eius rubeus breuifs ſtature:carés barba:z babebit ſignú in oculis. Qui fub tertia facie nat fuerit erit craffus breuifqz ſtature:z habebit multitudinem pilov in ſupcilijs. Qui nai fuerit sub prima facie leonis erit pulcher corporeiz facie: 7 color vultus eius erit rubeus albo cómixtus:oculi eius cómixti:pectus rectum:z crura fua patie tur morbi in parte fuperiori: erit cognitus inter gentes:z fimplex imixtus qcofortio regum. Qui natus fuerit ſubſecunda facie erit pulcher: pectus eius erit latum:teſticuli z crura gracilia:eritqz bonoratus inter familiam ei ac alti cordis. Natus autem fub tertia facie erit aliquantulum breuis ſtass ture eritos color ei alb rofeo pmixtus eius vor vehemens amas malies res:2 babebit plenitudiné amicoy z morbos plures fuftinebit. Qui nat fue rit fub pma facie virginis erit decentis ftatuere:erit corpus ei erectű z puls chú:z erit ſciés z act?:eius capilli cripfi:z erit diligés iusticiaz:eius vor vehe meno:ſterilis exitter:aia eius bona:facies pulcbraterites fcriba z bene doc tus. Qui aut natus fuerit fub fecúda facie erit pulcbre apparétie:ocli ei par ni:nafus eius pulcher:eritqz difciplinabilis z fimplex preuoluntarius corde Diliget laudari. Nat fub tertia facie erit pulcher afpectu preptibilis difciplis ne:veri fenfus ac fimplex z ſapiens. Qui nat fuerit fub pma facie Libre:erit pulcher facie:r in ei capite vinera ierút:erico laborator z fimplex ac difcipli nabilis. Nat fub fecida facie erit pulchri vultus z inerit ei ipedimétú in ocu lis:eftqz corde preuolitarius. Natus fub tertia facie erit pulcher z erit cogni tus z bonorat inter gentes ipfi. Et qui fuerit natus fub fine figni huius erit nulliſexus aut vtriufqz. Qui fuerit nat fub pma facie fcorpionis parú pul cher exiftetiz babebit fignŭ in capite:z pect eius erit latu. Et habebit figna in pede finiftro vel in manu dextra esteß bó caftigationis bonas difcretione plen? ac in loquela festinus. Natus fub fecúda facie caput ei erit magnum: exiſtetqz modice pulchre figure.z habebit fignum in genu z dorfo:z erit bó difcipline verba fua multiplicas. Qui natus fuerit fub tertia facie erit breuis ftature oculi eius cótoti appetés comedere amás mulieres. Qui aut natus fuerit fub fine buius figni aut erit nullius aut vtriufqz ſexus. Qui natus fue rit fub pma facie ſagittarij erit pulchre ſpeciei:decor afpectu recte ftatures diligés bonű:regib ac magnatibus ſe cómifcés. Nat ſubſecuda facie corp babebit codecca facies in eius crocea fupcilia cópreſſa z bébit fignum in pe ctore. Nat ſub tertia facie erit longus:pulcher facie:oculi eius vt murilegit pect latú:z babebit fignú in finiftro crure. Qui fub pma facie capricomi nas to fuerit erit corpus ſun códecés:pectus latú:z bebit fignú nigrum in cubito erit aftutus difciplinabilis fimplex z puoluntari. Natus fub fecúda erit pul cher:bébit nares longas:oculi ei pulchri:voluntas ci mala z iracidus:eft bó fciétie. Natus fub tertia facie erit pulcher corpore:ei in facies citrina: 7 in eins brachio finiftro erit nota vel in ipfius genu:feſting in iraz:defpiciens malú vel pugnás pro malo:diligés mulieres:bó difcipline diligész fodales Quiaût natus fuerit in fine buius ſigni erit filius adulterin z oino buic ieft figno minime in mulierű natiuitatib valere. Qui aut natus fuerit fub pma facie aquarij erit corpore z facie formofus:zhébit in pectore aut pede finis Aro fignú:eritqs bó difciplinabilis z sodales diligés. Natus fub fecida facie erit longus:facies eius erit rubea:z fignum bēbit in dorso a sub eius cubito eritas oibus diebus fuis in dolore. Natus fub tertia facie corpore pulcher: Ratura breuis:facie roseus: bébit fignum fab cubitis:z erit muliey amator. Et qui fub fine buius ſigni natus fuerit diuerfificatus erit in eins figura ates in oibus fuis operibus.Qui autem fub pma facie pifciu originé traxerit:co pus eius erit album:z facies fimiliter:pectus cius latú:barba pulchra:fros preclara:oculi eius magis nigri qz albi. Et fone aliquod membrum fibi defi aet:diliget doumire;eritos gulosus z ebiofus;z babebit fignum fub cubito
translation: and the land of Salacia. I believe it should also be said of Scalavia and Germania. And those that concord with Leo are: Italy, Gaul, Apulia, and Sicily. And Daly says there: here are the ministers of Rome. And those that concord with Sagittarius are the lands of Turkey, Calatia, and Hispania. I do not care about the attribution of other signs to other lands, because neither their names nor the editions of men are known to me, nor would it be of any use to me to know them. If anyone knows them and does not wish to dwell on them, let him apply them in the manner described in the aforementioned three signs. Abraham Avenezra says that the first clime is of Capricorn and Aquarius, and its governor is Saturn. The second clime is of Sagittarius and Pisces, and its governor is Jupiter. The third clime is of Scorpio and Aries, and its governor is Mars. The fourth is of Leo, and its governor is the Sun. The fifth clime is of Taurus and Libra, and its governor is Venus. The sixth is of Gemini and Virgo, and its governor is Mercury. The seventh is of Cancer, and its governor is the Moon. Abraham Avenezra sets forth the conditions of those born under each sign, and his opinion is this: He who is born under the first face of Aries will be reddish and thin; his belly will be somewhat constricted, his flesh attenuated; he will have a mark above his left foot and on the elbow of his left hand; and his friends will be many, and he himself will hate evil. But he who is born under the second face will have blackness combined with the beauty of his face; his body will be temperate, quick to anger, concealing evil will in his mind; of high heart; his enemies will be many. He who is born under the third face will have a reddish and saffron color; he will be solitary. He who is born under the first face of Taurus will be short of stature; his eyes large; lips thick; and he will have a mark on his neck; and he is quick of heart; his friends many; he will delight in various manners. But he who is born under the second face will have a round face; a broad belly; beautiful eyes; and his mind will be willing and quick to act; and he will be hairy around the shoulders; and he will have a mark on his loins. And he who is born under the third face will be of beautiful color and beautiful face; and he will have a mark in his left eye; and he will be a laborious man; nor will he be fortunate in women. But he who is born at the end of this sign will lack natural testicles, or be an hermaphrodite. He who is born under the first face of Gemini will be of beautiful and decent body; and of eyes and hair; he will have a mark on his head or in his eyes. His sight will be keen; he will be least irascible; and he will be a worker and least fortunate with women. And he who is born under the second face will be short of stature and black, and a mark of blackness above his elbow; and his words beautiful and sweet. He who is born under the third face will have very small eyes; and he will be a suspicious and gentle man; and he will speak irrational words; and he will be a liar. He who is born under the first face of Cancer will be of decent body and hair; his eyebrows will be narrow, his nostrils long, and his shoulders broad; and he will have a mark on his right elbow or arm; and his soul good; his friends many; and he will be learned and ingenious. He who is born under the second face will have a reddish color, short stature; lacking a beard; and he will have a mark in his eyes. He who is born under the third face will be fat and short of stature; and he will have a multitude of hairs on his eyebrows. He who is born under the first face of Leo will be beautiful in body and face; and the color of his face will be reddish mixed with white; his eyes mixed; his chest straight; and his legs will suffer from disease in the upper part; he will be known among people; and simple, not mixed with the company of kings. He who is born under the second face will be beautiful; his chest will be broad; his testicles and legs slender; and he will be honored among his family and of high heart. But he who is born under the third face will be somewhat short of stature; and his color will be white mixed with rosy; his voice vehement; loving women; and he will have many friends and suffer many diseases. He who is born under the first face of Virgo will be of decent stature; his body will be erect and beautiful; and he will be learned and active; his hair curly; and he will love justice; his voice vehement; he will be sterile; his soul good; his face beautiful; and he will be a scribe and well-taught. But he who is born under the second face will be of beautiful appearance; his eyes small; his nose beautiful; and he will be disciplined and simple, of a willing heart. He will love to be praised. He who is born under the third face will be beautiful in appearance, susceptible to discipline; of true senses and simple and wise. He who is born under the first face of Libra will be beautiful of face; and ulcers will be on his head; and he will be a worker and simple and disciplined. He who is born under the second face will be of beautiful countenance and will have an impediment in his eyes; and he is of a willing heart. He who is born under the third face will be beautiful and will be known and honored among people. And he who is born at the end of this sign will be of no sex or of both sexes. He who is born under the first face of Scorpio will be little beautiful; and he will have a mark on his head; and his chest will be broad. And he will have a mark on his left foot or in his right hand; and he will be a man of good discipline, full of discretion, and quick in speech. He who is born under the second face, his head will be large; and he will be of moderately beautiful figure; and he will have a mark on his knee and back; and he will be a man of discipline, multiplying his words. He who is born under the third face will be short of stature; his eyes small; desirous of eating; loving women. But he who is born at the end of this sign will be of no sex or of both sexes. He who is born under the first face of Sagittarius will be of beautiful appearance; graceful in aspect; of straight stature; loving good; associating with kings and magnates. He who is born under the second face will have a decent body; his face saffron; his eyebrows compressed; and he will have a mark on his chest. He who is born under the third face will be tall; beautiful of face; his eyes like a cat's; his chest broad; and he will have a mark on his left leg. He who is born under the first face of Capricorn will have a decent body; a broad chest; and he will have a black mark on his elbow; he will be astute, disciplined, simple, and willing. He who is born under the second face will be beautiful; he will have long nostrils; his eyes beautiful; his will evil and irascible; he is a man of knowledge. He who is born under the third face will be beautiful of body; his face yellowish; and on his left arm there will be a mark or on his knee; quick to anger; despising evil or fighting for evil; loving women; a man of discipline, loving companions. But he who is born at the end of this sign will be an illegitimate child, and this sign is of little value in the nativities of women. But he who is born under the first face of Aquarius will be beautiful in body and face; and he will have a mark on his chest or left foot; and he will be a disciplined man and loving companions. He who is born under the second face will be tall; his face will be red; and he will have a mark on his back and under his elbow; and he will be in pain all his days. He who is born under the third face will be beautiful of body; short of stature; rosy of face; he will have a mark under his elbows; and he will be a lover of women. And he who is born at the end of this sign will be diversified in his figure and in all his works. But he who is born under the first face of Pisces, his body will be white; and his face likewise; his chest broad; his beard beautiful; his forehead clear; his eyes more black than white. And some member will be lacking to him; he will love to sleep; and he will be gluttonous and drunken; and he will have a mark under his elbow.

author: Alchabitius
title: Planets and Body Parts
text: vel in pede. Natus vero fub fecunda facie erit breuis ftature.Decorus afpes ctu:barba nigra:pilofus:z crit ambulans in duritia cum filijs boium.Na tus fubtertia facie piſcium erit aſpectu preclarus: oculi eiuspulchri eritqs morbosus. I fi quidem planete coffee Prius auctor oftens dit aliter figna reſpé ciút membra humani corporis. In iſta pte oftendit nobis qualiter planete refpiciunt membra copis humani in ipfis fignis. Et pót b pars diuidi in.i2.ptes fecúdu.i2.figna.vbi pres incipiunt patet. •Prima pars diuidi pót in duas:quonia smo pponit.Secudo pſequit ibi:in ariete. Dicit pmoq fi aliqs planeta fignificauerit aliqua infirmitaté fiue debi litatem in corpore humano habebit in quolibet figno membrumppium in quo erit illa infirmitas. Et quita est couenit tractare de dolozib planetaruz in fignis. Et incipit ab ariete prededo fecundú fucceffioné fignorú z planeta rum. Quod dicit auctor planú eft in littera. Ad euidétiá vnius ptis ponovnú exemplum. Ponoq in aliqua natiuitate ſatarnus fignificauerit nato debilis tatem alicui membri z fit faturn in ariete.Dico o illa debilitas vel infirmis tas erit in pectore. Et si saturn fit in tauro erit illa ifirmitas in vetre z fic intel ligas de ceteris fignis. Pono étq mars in natiuitate itelligas de reuolutio ne annop natitaris fignificauerit vulnerationé z fit mars in ariete erit vuln in capite. Si in tauro erit vulnus in collo:z fic de ceterio fignis z planetis in telligas Ptolemeus vult & mébra in gbus cadunt morbi ze.impediméta ac cipiunt a figura celi in bora natiuitatis vel revolutionis.Dicit enim in.74. ppofitione centiloquij cú fuerit mars in afcendente alicuius natiuitatis erit cicatrix in facie vel capite nati. Dicit enim Daly q caput eft afcendentis z Em boc collum eſt ſecunde domus.brachia tertie domus z fic km ordinem domorum fecundum q dictum fuit de ſignis. De hac materia diffufius log tur domino cócedéte in capitulo ſexte domus. Ont quoque iuno quoque na pte auctor determinat de qbufdam fpecialibus pprietatib? plas netarú que funt in diuerfis gradibus circuli fignomz z funt fer. funt enim ibi gdam gradus mafculini z gdam feminini.Et funt ibi qdam gradus lucidi z gdam tenebroh. Etia funt ibigdam gradus qui di cunt putei. Et funt ibi qdam gradus qui dicunt azemena. Et funt ibi gdam gradus qui dicunt augmétates fortuna. Et funtibi gdam gradus qui dicun tur cofortes. Et fecúdum hoc iſta pars pot diuidi in fex ptes:quonia in pma pte enumerat gradus masculinos z femininos. In fecunda parte enumerat gradus lucidos z tenebrosos z fumofos. In tertia parte enumerat gradus qui dicuntur putei. In quarta parte enumerat gradus qui dicuntur азета na. In quinta parte enumerat gradus qui dicuntur augmentantes fortunaz 7 gradus fublimitatis. In fexta parte enumerat gradus quidicuntur confor res. C-prima pars incipitin pncipio.Secunda incipit ibi:z in vnoquoqsho rum fignor. Zertia incipit ibizz in ſignis funt gradus.Quarta ibi:z funt in fi gnis quidam gradus. Quinta ibi.z in circulo.Sexta ibi iz os duo gradus. Prima pars potest diuidi in duas partes:quonia in prima parte incipit enu merare gradus macſulinos z femininos litteraliter. In fecúda parte ponit tabula de eis. As dicit patet in littera. Poftea dicit o funt qda grad in circu lo qui dicunt lucidi z gdam tenebrofi:z gda fumofit quidam qui vocant vas cui. Et icipit primo eos enumerare. Et deide ponit de eis tabula z patet.poft ea dicit og ſunt in circulo gdam gradus qui dicuntur putei.z enumerat illos omnes in littera.7 qdam libri babent tabulam de eis factam. Deinde dicit q infignis funt quidam gradus qui dicuntur azemena Et dicit qazemena eſt quedam debilitatio corporalis vt eſt cecitas vel ſurditas vel aliqd taliuz vel amiffio mébri. Jurta quod notanduz q dicit Daly Abenragel in pmo capitu lo domus fexte libri fui'de natiuitatib. Quando pars azemena semena fuerit in pti tione aſcendentis z luna iuncta cum ea vel dominus aſcédentis fignificati azemena erit in illo membro nati quod cadit in partitione illius figni. No tandum est etiam q ſi ſol in natiuitate alicuius fuerit in gradu azemena erit debilitatio in oculo dextro si luna erit in 'finiſtro. Poftea dicit o funt quidam gradus in circulo qui dicuntur augmentantes fortunam z enumerat eos in uttera. Juxta quam partem notandum est quod Paly dicit in comméto.229. propoſitionis prime partis quadripartitifcire te conuenit o gradus aug. mentantes foitunam funt gradus exaltationis ſolis z planetarum fortunas rum.ficut.ig.arietis.3.tauri.is.cancri.27.pifcium. Similiter funt illi in quib? adiunguntur termini fortunarum:ficut.iz.fagittarii 2:13.pifcium.2 sunt etiaz gradus terminorum fortunarum.Et gradus in quibus funt stelle fixe de na tura fortunarum. Et econtrario in quibus funt ſtelle de natura infotunaruz Et poftea dicit y omnes duo gradus qui funt eiufdem longitudinis a capiti bus fignowm mobilium funt eiufdem fortitudinis. Et dicuntur confortes ficut.20.gradus capricorni.z.io.gradus ſagittarij. Ifti eni duo gradus equa liter diftant a capite capricorni.7.20.libre.z.io.virginis equaliter diftant a ca pite libre.Et.20.cancrit.io.geminorum equaliter diftant a principio cancri. 7.20.gradus arietis.z.io.pifcium equaliter diftant a principio arietis. Pto, lemeus dicit.229.propofitione prime partis. Aftrologi ceteri multum locus ti fuerunt de gradibus a diuiferunt eos in gradus lucentes lubricos z fus mofos. Et funt ex eis azemana:z funt ex eis putei. Et locuti fuerunt de alijs multis ficut inuenies per multos libros astronomie.Et fi hoc inuenerunt ex perientia bonum eſt og operis per hoc.Et forfita fi eis accidit q aliquis pla neta vel ftella fuit in gradu vel eius radius 7 fecit fortunam ceciderunt q gradus hoc fecerit. Et fiquidem ita est conuenit vt te mutes de vno gradu in alium. Et bic finem imponamus noſtre rationi. Uidetur periſtam littera -Ptolemeus non multum approbat gradus augmentantes fortunam 26. Dicit enim fi ita eſt conuenit vt te mutes de vno gradu in alium.gradus eni Dicuntur lucidi vel tenebrosi propter ftellas fixas ibi exiftentes. Et vult Paly fecundum o preallegati fuit q gradus dicuntur augmentantes fortunam in quibus funt ftelle de natura fortunarum.Conftat autem & stelle fixe mus cant loca ſua reſpectu zodiaci none ſphaere. translation: or in the foot. A native born under the second face will be of short stature, beautiful in appearance, with a black beard, hairy, and will walk with difficulty among the sons of men. A native born under the third face of Pisces will be of splendid appearance, his eyes beautiful, and he will be sickly. Indeed, the author first shows how the planets relate to the members of the human body. In this part, he shows us how the planets relate to the members of the human body in those signs. And this part can be divided into 12 parts according to the 12 signs, where the parts begin. The first part can be divided into two, because first it proposes, second it follows there: in Aries. He says first that if any planet signifies some infirmity or debility in the human body, it will have in each sign a proper member in which that infirmity will be. And it is fitting to treat the pains of the planets in the signs. And it begins from Aries, proceeding according to the succession of signs and planets. What the author says is clear in the text. For the evidence of one part, I give one example. I assume that in some nativity, Saturn signifies a debility of some member to the native, and Saturn is in Aries. I say that that debility or infirmity will be in the chest. And if Saturn is in Taurus, that infirmity will be in the belly, and so you understand for the other signs. I also assume that Mars in a nativity (you understand this for the revolution of the native's years) signifies a wound, and Mars is in Aries, the wound will be in the head. If in Taurus, the wound will be in the neck, and so for the other signs and planets you understand. Ptolemy wants the members in which diseases and impediments fall to be taken from the figure of the sky at the hour of nativity or revolution. For he says in the 74th proposition of the Centiloquium: when Mars is in the ascendant of any nativity, there will be a scar on the face or head of the native. For Daly says that the head is of the ascendant, and the neck is of the second house, the arms of the third house, and so according to the order of the houses, as was said about the signs. He will speak more extensively on this matter, God willing, in the chapter of the sixth house. Also, the author determines in this part about certain special properties of the planets that are in different degrees of the circle of signs, and there are six of them. For there are some masculine degrees and some feminine. And there are some lucid degrees and some dark. Also, there are some degrees that are called wells. And there are some degrees that are called azemena. And there are some degrees that are called augmenting fortune. And there are some degrees that are called consortes. And according to this, this part can be divided into six parts: because in the first part it enumerates masculine and feminine degrees. In the second part it enumerates lucid, dark, and smoky degrees. In the third part it enumerates degrees that are called wells. In the fourth part it enumerates degrees that are called azemena. In the fifth part it enumerates degrees that are called augmenting fortune and degrees of exaltation. In the sixth part it enumerates degrees that are called consortes. The first part begins at the beginning. The second begins there: and in each of these signs. The third begins there: and in the signs there are degrees. The fourth there: and there are in the signs certain degrees. The fifth there: and in the circle. The sixth there: and all two degrees. The first part can be divided into two parts: because in the first part it begins to enumerate masculine and feminine degrees literally. In the second part it provides a table of them. As it says, it is clear in the text. Afterwards it says that there are certain degrees in the circle that are called lucid and some dark, and some smoky, and some that are called empty. And it begins by enumerating them first. And then it provides a table of them, and it is clear. After that it says that there are in the circle certain degrees that are called wells. And it enumerates all of them in the text. And some books have a table made of them. Then it says that in the signs there are certain degrees that are called azemena. And it says that azemena is a certain bodily debility, such as blindness or deafness or something similar, or the loss of a member. In this regard, it is to be noted that Daly Abenragel says in the first chapter of the sixth house of his book on nativities: When the azemena part is in the partition of the ascendant and the Moon is conjoined with it, or the lord of the ascendant signifies azemena, it will be in that member of the native that falls in the partition of that sign. It is also to be noted that if the Sun in someone's nativity is in an azemena degree, there will be a debility in the right eye; if the Moon is, in the left. Afterwards it says that there are certain degrees in the circle that are called augmenting fortune, and it enumerates them in the text. In this regard, it is to be noted that Paly says in the commentary on the 229th proposition of the first part of the Quadripartitum: you should know that the degrees augmenting fortune are the degrees of exaltation of the Sun and fortunate planets, such as the 19th of Aries, the 3rd of Taurus, the 15th of Cancer, the 27th of Pisces. Similarly, there are those in which the terms of the fortunes are joined, such as the 12th of Sagittarius and the 13th of Pisces. And there are also degrees of the terms of the fortunes. And degrees in which there are fixed stars of the nature of fortunes. And conversely, those in which there are stars of the nature of misfortunes. And afterwards it says that all two degrees that are of the same longitude from the heads of the movable signs are of the same strength. And they are called consortes, such as the 20th degree of Capricorn and the 10th degree of Sagittarius. For these two degrees are equally distant from the head of Capricorn. And the 20th of Libra and the 10th of Virgo are equally distant from the head of Libra. And the 20th of Cancer and the 10th of Gemini are equally distant from the beginning of Cancer. And the 20th degree of Aries and the 10th of Pisces are equally distant from the beginning of Aries. Ptolemy says in the 229th proposition of the first part: Other astrologers have spoken much about degrees and divided them into lucid, slippery, and smoky degrees. And some of them are azemena, and some of them are wells. And they have spoken of many other things, as you will find in many astronomical books. And if they found this by experience, it is good to work by it. And perhaps if it happened to them that some planet or star was in a degree or its ray, and it made fortune, they concluded that the degree did this. And if indeed it is so, it is fitting that you move from one degree to another. And here let us put an end to our reasoning. It seems from the text that Ptolemy does not much approve of the degrees augmenting fortune. For he says: if it is so, it is fitting that you move from one degree to another. For degrees are called lucid or dark because of the fixed stars existing there. And Paly wants, according to what was previously alleged, that degrees are called augmenting fortune in which there are stars of the nature of fortunes. It is clear, however, that fixed stars change their places with respect to the zodiac of the ninth sphere. From that text of Ptolemy, it is possible.

title: Judgments from the Ninth Sphere
