Dicit alfraganus quod corpиз ſaturni est.i.vicibus tantum quâtum terra. Apiter fortuna maſculus. bic ponie naturam Jouis z planum est quod dicit in littera. Jouis auté virtú opus est coplexionis téperate z loc ſui motus eft medianus inter fri giditaté ſaturi 7 calore martis 7 ppter hoc puenit vt calefaciat zhumectat. Dicit Paly in cóméto.multi crediderút cú audierút hoc verbuz Ptolemei quod opera ſtellarů ſecúdú ei opinioné ſequerent loca ſphaero rum ſcơm 2 vna exiftit ſupra alia ficut credidit biza auéquiton z alkindus 7 elij qui cum talibus ratiõibus nos'impediuerút. Ego vo dico gli opa ſtets lari funt ſecudu natura quomodo poffunt ſcri propoſitides ädruuiales qm radices barum ambay ſcientiay diverſe ſut vna.fab alia.Amplius ſi oſa cor pora celeftia no funt alicuius cóplexionis:calide:bumide:frigide:nes ficce ficut pbauit Ariftoteles quo pot Jouis complexio temperarico qo ſphaes ra fua exiſtat inter illa martis 7 faturni. Nam fi bene ad dcm inſpexeris pro lemei inuenies nó effe put ipfi crediderunt. Et poftqz docuit nos viam qua ſcirent virtutes ſtellarů ponit hoc vice alia in boc loco dicens quod qi pros banerimus moduz operis qo operať in aere nos circúdante inuenimus est medianus inter magna frigiditatem quá facit ſaturnus & magni calorez qué facit marsiz boc affirmat id qo dixit qo calefacit:z humectat.dicit Paly abenragel iupiter é planeta equalitatis:coitatis:bonitatis:meliorameti: in tellectus:fenfus:z pietatis:q temperatus eft equalis z fortuna paſpectum z corporalem cóiunctionem fignificat boniiatem meliorationeni legem fim plicitaté z caftitate dirigit z nó dánat:populat no destruit:abhorret fatur num z eius naturas. Quando deus incepit creare mundum erat in domo afcedéte lucet in ariete tauro z gemini:z minuit lux ſua in cacro leone z virs gine.obfcurat in libra ſcorpióe z ſagittario: minuit eius obſcuratio in caps como ağrio z pifcibus.Dic alfragan q corpus Jouis eft.9 5.tmqztű terra. Ars maſculinus. bic ponit natura martť z pas mars pprie deficcat z p vim nature fue comburit eo qb igneuz b3 coloremiz quia circa ſolem eſt z qm ſphaera in qua ſol conſt M tet in littera.dicit-Proleme ſtit pofita eft ſub eo.dicit Daly in comento Prolemeus vult dice req opus virtutis martis ideft q experimur inuenimus ficcitatis magne. Ad id quod dixit o virtus martis comburit eo qß igneum bz colorez. dico quod fſtelle funt fubftantie ſimplicis z non compofite ex naturis diuerforuz elementoru:apparitio fui coloris eft recta eo quod ibi nó est aligd quod mu tet colorem cóuenientem eidem ſubſtantie. Ptolemeus autem docet nos p boc dictueregulă a ſcimus vtuté ſtellay quá p nos intelligere nó poſſumus z poſſimus ea int
translation: In this question, I looked at the ascendant and its lord, and the Moon, for whom the question was asked. I looked and found the lord of the ascendant in the midheaven, which at first glance seemed to indicate prosperity. But because I found Saturn in the angular house of the East, near the angle, within two degrees, I doubted the life of that person, especially because the Part of Fortune was unfortunate there. Then I looked at the house of death and found Venus stronger there, that is, in the house of death, having more testimonies because of exaltation and triplicity. I looked at its place in the figure and how it related to the ascendant, to the lord of the ascendant, and to the Moon, and I found it aspecting the ascendant with a quartile aspect, and Saturn existing there with the same aspect, which made me doubt life even more. Then I looked at the Moon and found it oppressed under the Sun's rays; this was one sign of death. I also found the Moon separated from Venus, going to the conjunction of the lord of the ascendant, and it transferred the nature of the lord of the house of death to the lord of the ascendant. This was a more certain sign of death. From these, I concluded that the man for whom the question was asked was dead, and it was found to be so. And because Saturn, being in the ascendant, was the lord of the seventh house, signifying the wife of the person for whom the question was asked, and Venus, signifying death, aspected him, this signified that perhaps the deceased had committed fornication with the wife of the person for whom the question was asked. The second difference in the natures of the seven planets. This is the second difference of this book, in which the author determines the natures of the seven planets. And it can be divided into four parts: first, it determines the natures and properties of the seven planets. Second, it determines the order or principality of the planets in perceptions. Third, concerning the nature of the head and tail of the dragon. Fourth, it determines the order of the planets in the lordship of days and hours. The second part begins there: 'concerning the order of the planets'. The third there: 'the head of the dragon'. The fourth there: 'if there is any day'. The first part is divided into seven parts: first, it sets out the nature of Saturn. Second, Jupiter. Third, Mars. Fourth, the Sun. Fifth, Venus. Sixth, Mercury. Seventh, the Moon. Where the parts begin is clear in the text. First, it sets out the nature of Saturn and what it signifies from things and human customs and from sects and so on, all of which clearly appears in the text up to that part about the years of Fridaria. Concerning that part, it is to be understood when it says 'the years of Fridaria of Saturn are', it is to be understood as the year of governance in the life of the native. For the elucidation of this, it is to be known that in a diurnal nativity, Fridaria begins with the Sun and governs it for 10 years. Afterwards, Venus governs it according to the quantity of its years. Then Mercury: thus according to the order of the planets, going to the end of life. If, however, the nativity is nocturnal, the governance begins with the Moon and governs it according to the quantity of its years. After the Moon, Saturn governs it according to the quantity of its years, and so according to the order of the planets. There is a proper chapter on this in the fourth difference, and there it will speak more extensively about this, God willing. When it says in the text: 'the greatest', it is to be understood concerning the years that the planets give in the duration of things, namely in sects and in other great matters. When it says 'greater and middle and lesser', it is to be understood concerning the years that the planets give in the nativities of men for living: and as I say about Saturn, so you should understand about the other planets. Ptolemy says in the 82nd proposition of the first part that the greater and stronger work of Saturn is to cool and dry a little because of the strength of cold: and he sees this as being because it is very far from the heat of the Sun and the humid vapors of the earth. Albumazar refutes Ptolemy, and I will, God willing, present his reasons at the end of this part on the natures of the planets. Daly says in the commentary on the aforementioned proposition that Ptolemy means that Saturn performs this work because its work is very far from the work of the Sun, which is the work of heat, and from the Moon, whose work is to attract humors: because when he looked at the comparison of Saturn with the Sun and Moon, he found that it was contrary to both, and because of this, he said that it is fitting that it be cold and dry. And Ptolemy himself glossed this comparison in what follows. Ptolemy says that to know the virtue of this planet and others will be for the rectification of figures in respect to the Sun and Moon, because we see some of them changing the air in one manner of change and some of them in another manner in different arrangements of change, increasing and decreasing. Daly says in the commentary: 'If you remember what we told you by which I can prove the virtues of the stars, you do not need a gloss in this. And if you have forgotten this, listen. I tell you that the Sun and Moon, because their works are more apparent than the works of other stars, are more manifest to us: and the works of the remaining stars seem hidden: it is fitting that you look at the stars having commonality with the two luminaries through figures: that is, through aspects, whether they increase in their works or diminish from them: and so you will look at all of them: until you know the certainty of all, not once but many times. And if you find that they always follow one manner of operating, you will know for certain what kind of virtue they have.' Daly Abenragel says about the nature of Saturn: 'Saturn is an old, great, weary, despised planet of anxieties, sadness, and long illnesses. Its nature is cold and dry and it is assimilated to melancholy, which is governed by all humors, and none by it. When it is the almuten of a nativity, and it is in Cancer, the native will be of a very ugly face and a marvelous creature in its form and vision, so that all who see him and hear him spoken of will be terrified and amazed.' Alfraganus says that the body of Saturn is 91 times as large as the Earth. Jupiter is a masculine fortune. Here he sets out the nature of Jupiter, and what he says in the text is clear. 'The virtue of Jupiter, however, is of a temperate complexion, and its place of motion is median between the coldness of Saturn and the heat of Mars, and because of this, it happens that it heats and moistens.' Daly says in the commentary: 'Many believed, when they heard this word of Ptolemy, that the works of the stars, according to his opinion, followed the places of the spheres according to whether one existed above another, as believed by Hiza, Avenquiton, Alkindus, and others who hindered us with such reasons. But I say that the works of the stars are according to nature, how can quadrivial propositions be written, since the roots of these two sciences are diverse, one from the other. Furthermore, if all celestial bodies are not of any complexion: hot, humid, cold, nor dry, as Aristotle proved, how can Jupiter's complexion be temperate because its sphere exists between those of Mars and Saturn? For if you look closely at Ptolemy's statement, you will find that it is not as they believed. And after he taught us the way by which we might know the virtues of the stars, he puts this another way in this place, saying that since we have proven the mode of operation that operates in the air surrounding us, we have found it to be median between the great coldness that Saturn makes and the great heat that Mars makes, and this affirms what he said, that it heats and moistens.' Daly Abenragel says: 'Jupiter is the planet of equality, community, goodness, improvement, intellect, sense, and piety: it is temperate, equal, and a fortune. By aspect and bodily conjunction, it signifies goodness, improvement, law, simplicity, and chastity. It directs and does not condemn; it populates and does not destroy; it abhors Saturn and its natures. When God began to create the world, it was in the ascending house, shining in Aries, Taurus, and Gemini; and its light diminished in Cancer, Leo, and Virgo. It darkens in Libra, Scorpio, and Sagittarius; its darkening diminishes in Capricorn, Aquarius, and Pisces.' Alfraganus says that the body of Jupiter is 95 times as large as the Earth. Mars is masculine. Here he sets out the nature of Mars, and Mars properly dries and by the force of its nature burns because it has a fiery color, and because it is around the Sun, and because the sphere in which the Sun is placed is below it. Daly says in the commentary: 'Ptolemy means that the work of the virtue of Mars, that is, what we experience, we find to be of great dryness. To what he said, that the virtue of Mars burns because it has a fiery color, I say that the stars are simple substances and not composed of the natures of diverse elements: the appearance of their color is correct because there is nothing there that changes the color suitable to the same substance. Ptolemy, however, teaches us by this saying a rule by which we know the virtue of the stars, which we cannot understand by ourselves, and we can understand it.

author: Alchabitius
title: Mercury's Influence on Weather and Ptolemy's Theories
text: ==Start of OCR for page 1== rum fignificantium pluuias z per cum z per mutationem ſua de signo in fis gnum:z per eum eius ſtatum ac retrogradationem 7 directionem ipſius τρ oppoſitiones z cóiunctiones fuas ſcient motus ventorum a ei fortitudines ſſi erunt fortes vel debiles in oibus partibus illius anni:quoniam quando erit de vno ſigno z intrat aliud:vel quando eſt ſtationarius aut retrogradus fignificat ventos fortes humiditates a pluuiam z in aere accidentia de nubi bus turbationes z bis fimilia. Similiter qui est in oppoſitione lune exiſtente luna in aliquo fignorum aqueori vel aereop ſignificat quod pdirim?. In re volutione annoy mundi bét magná z veridicas fignificatione fup tenentes officicia regű. Alfraganus dicit & quátitas corporis mercurij eſt ynaps de. 32.milibus ptibus corporis terre. Una fortuna femina nocturnal Pic ponit naturam lune.ſnia littere patet. Dicit Ptolemeus ma ior virtus lune eſt humectare:pro eo q est multum circa terram zppter fumofitatem frigidam que per eam eleuat.z ppter hoc ad ſtatum talem mutant corpora manifeſta mutatione:q: facit mutariz pus trefcere corpora vt plurimum:z bét etia cum fole participationé modica m in caloze pro eo g recipit Inmen ab eo. Albumazar redarguit Ptolemeű in cauſa naturarum planetarum z funt verba fuaiſta: quoniam plures reges grecie fuerunt ſapientes post Alexandrum filia Philippi: vocabaturqz vnuf quiſqs eorum Ptolemeus:fueruntqz numero decem.z vna mulier: erantos regnantes in egypto:z fuerunt anni regni eorum.275.fuerunt vniuerfi fa pientes ex quibus fuit Ptolemeus vnus qui edidit librum almageſti ſup cau fam motus circuli z gequid in eo eft ex planetis. Et quidam eorum edidit lis brum de iudicijs astrozum referens eum ad Ptolemeum auctorem libri al mageſti.Dicituros o ille qui edidit librum iudiciorum ipfe edidit libzuz alma gefti:neſcituros veritas buius rei:fed ille qui ex eis edidit librum iudiciorum narrauit in libro ſuo naturas planetarum a eorum causas. Incepitos dicere fol calefacit in ſuauitate ac mora z y opus eius fit in hoc euidétius 7 forti us opere ceterozum planetarum ppter magnitudinem eius z quáto magis eleuat ad zenith capitum noftrorum auget nobis calor. Et putauit o natu ra lune fit humida ppter primitatem circuli ei a terra z ppter receptioné va porum.quad ea eleuant ex ea. Et putauit & natura ſaturni fit frigida z ficca ppter longitudinem circuli eius a calore ſolis z longitudinez ei ab humidis tate vaporum terre. Et putauit & mars natura effet calida z ficca eo q coloz ei fimilis eft igniiz queſt ppe folem z qe eſt ſub eo erigiturqs calor eius ad eum z calefacit eum. Et putauit & Jupiter fit equalis complexionis:eo o fic eius circulus inter circulos ſaturni z martis. Et ppter banc cauſaz est natu ra cius calida bumida equalis z temperata. Et putauit o veneris natura lit calida bumida equalis.calor autem eius exppingtate circuli ei a fole. Dumi ditas vero er vapore humido qui contingit eam ex terra. Putauitos natu ra mercurij fit in gbufdam boris ficca z in gbusdam bumida.Siccitas eipp appropingtatem ei a fole z gnon elongatur ab eo elongatione maxime. ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== Pumiditas vero eius ppter primitate circuli eius circulo lune. Hoc eft opu rauit Ptoleme in naturis planetarűz bec est ratio qua usus est ſuper hoc. Sed nos dicemus nunc o fit in eius dictis pungendus idest reprehenfione Dignus ná o putauit ex fole z calefactise rerum ab eoin ſuauitate z moza 15 inuenit ex fuo ope.quod autem dixit o natura lune fit humida ppter primi tatem circuli eius a terra z receptione vaporum qui erigunt ex terra ad cam boc repelliť a ſapientibus eo o ſpacium quod eſt inter faciem terre 2 pгopin quiorem locum in quo eſt luna fit.i28044 miliarionz fere ea menfura qua fit ynum miliarium.J000.cubitorum. Et hoc patet in lib20 in quo narrantur longitudines corpozum ſuperiorum ab inuicem. Et non plus eleuant vapo res a ſuperficie terre ſcom o narrauit Ptolemeus qz.i6. ſtadia ſcom gita diuni quadringentorum cubitorum eſt:nam.i6.ſtadia faciunt duo miliaria 7 Decimam ac tredecimam vni miliarij fere.longitudo vo lune qui magis p pe eſt ſupficiei terre est vt diximus.i28044 miliariori fere.vn ergo vel quð applicat vapor terre ad luna quoufos corrupat vel mutet nam ei. Per mo dum primilé reprobat ipm in naturis aliorum planetarum: fed ptranſeo ca breuitatis. Daly in cómétoppofitioni pallegate de natura lune ride.t ad ro nes Albumazaris, Dicit enini Daly exponés verba Ptolemei smo postc Docuit nos Ptolemeus & vtutes ſtellarum corpor fuoy ſequunt magnitu dinezz corpus lune paruŭ eſt ficut in almageſti narranit z eius opus ſequit opus ſolis pte vna.Dicit hic q est hoc pro eo & multum eſt ppe terra z pue mitad nit ad nos eius virtus antes muter z ppter hoc apparet eius opus magis ož opus aliarum ſtellar. Et videmus vifibiliter o attrabit humores terrez corpomz ea ſequétiu z pro eo bét lucé a ſole operaf in terrenis caloze то dicum accidentalé cum quo facit putrefcere z mutari humores existentes in corporibus ficut oftenfum est in phifica z in nä. Et manifestum est q lux ſas cit calore.z pot boc pbari per ſpecula que coburút z filia.z qe luna lucé bét a ſole cóuenit vt beat modicu calorem quem facit ficut per acidens. Et hoc est id quod vult dicere Ptolemeus.Et qe boc non intellexit Albumazar cre didit & erraret in dicto ſuo dicens quó pot fumofitas bumida ad luna afce dere cuz fumofitas afcendere nequeat ultra.i6.ſtadia z luna ſatis longi eſt Ego vero riſi de dictis talibus qualia funt bec:z dixi qualiter no itellexit boce p Prolemeus noluit dicere q fumofitas bumida afcedat uses ad lund:fed. Dixit o afcenfus fumofitatis humide qui eſt verſus luna docet nos q attras bit humores terre z aliop corpomm eam fequentiu ficut adamas trabit fer rum.Alfraganus dicit auctoritate -Ptolemei & magnitudo corporis lune eſt vna pars de.39.ptib terre:z dicit & maius corpoiuz corpop vniuerfi eft fol z poſt ſole.is.marime funt ſtelle:Dicit enig vnaques captenet coupe terre centies fepties. Lerti in magnitudine eſt Jupiter: z art ſaturn. Poſt ſatur nú funt ſtelle fixe ſcoi ordinis arum libet ptinet terram nonagenes. poſtip fas funt ſtelle tertij ordinis arum vnaques ptinet terraz ſeptuageſies ? bis. Post ipas funt ſtelle ärti ordinis örü qlibet ptinet terram.54. Poſt ipſas füt ſtelle anti ordinis örum libet ptinet terram.37. Poſt ipſas funt ſtelle fexti ordinis 7 minorillay.immo minor oium que vident:qrum probatio eft pof ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: of the planets signifying rains, and through it and through its change from sign to sign, and through its state, retrogradation, and direct motion, and its oppositions and conjunctions, they will know the movements of the winds and their strengths, whether they will be strong or weak in all parts of that year. For when it is in one sign and enters another, or when it is stationary or retrograde, it signifies strong winds, humidity, and rain, and in the air, disturbances from clouds and similar things. Similarly, that which is in opposition to the Moon, with the Moon existing in any of the watery or airy signs, signifies what is predicted. In the revolution of the years of the world, it has great and true significance over those holding offices of kings. Alfraganus says that the quantity of Mercury's body is one part of 32,000 parts of the Earth's body. A fortunate female nocturnal planet places the nature of the Moon. The meaning of the letter is clear. Ptolemy says the greater virtue of the Moon is to moisten, because it is very close to the Earth and because of the cold smokiness that is elevated by it. And because of this, manifest bodies change to such a state, for it causes bodies to change and putrefy for the most part. And it also has a small participation with the Sun in heat, because it receives light from it. Albumazar refutes Ptolemy on the cause of the natures of the planets, and his words are these: because many kings of Greece were wise after Alexander, son of Philip, and each of them was called Ptolemy; there were ten of them, and one woman; they reigned in Egypt; and the years of their reign were 275. All of them were wise, among whom was Ptolemy, who published the book Almagest on the cause of the motion of the circle and whatever is in it concerning the planets. And one of them published a book on the judgments of the stars, referring it to Ptolemy, the author of the book Almagest. It is said that he who published the book of judgments also published the book Almagest; the truth of this matter is unknown. But he who among them published the book of judgments narrated in his book the natures of the planets and their causes. He began to say that the Sun heats with sweetness and delay, and that its work in this is more evident and stronger than the work of the other planets because of its magnitude, and the more it rises to the zenith of our heads, the more its heat increases for us. And he thought that the nature of the Moon is humid because of the proximity of its circle to the Earth and because of the reception of vapors that rise from it. And he thought that the nature of Saturn is cold and dry because of the length of its circle from the heat of the Sun and its distance from the humidity of the Earth's vapors. And he thought that Mars' nature is hot and dry because its color is similar to fire, and it is near the Sun, and it is below it, and its heat rises to it and heats it. And he thought that Jupiter is of an equal complexion, because its circle is between the circles of Saturn and Mars. And for this reason, its nature is hot, humid, equal, and temperate. And he thought that Venus's nature is hot, humid, and equal. Its heat, however, is from the proximity of its circle to the Sun. Its humidity, however, is from the humid vapor that reaches it from the Earth. He thought that Mercury's nature is dry at certain times and humid at others. Its dryness is due to its proximity to the Sun and because it does not move far from it. Its humidity, however, is due to the proximity of its circle to the Moon's circle. This is what Ptolemy worked on regarding the natures of the planets, and this is the reasoning he used for it. But we will now say that there is something to be criticized in his words, that is, worthy of reprehension. For he thought that the Sun heats things with sweetness and delay, and he found this from his work. But what he said, that the nature of the Moon is humid because of the proximity of its circle to the Earth and the reception of vapors that rise from the Earth to it, this is refuted by the wise, because the space between the surface of the Earth and the nearest place where the Moon is, is 128,044 miles, approximately, by the measure that one mile is 1,000 cubits. And this is clear in the book in which the distances of the superior bodies from each other are narrated. And vapors do not rise more than 16 stadia from the Earth's surface, according to what Ptolemy narrated, which is 400 cubits; for 16 stadia make two miles and a tenth and a thirteenth of one mile, approximately. The length of the Moon, which is closest to the Earth's surface, is, as we said, approximately 128,044 miles. How then can the Earth's vapor reach the Moon so as to corrupt or change its nature? In a similar way, he refutes him on the natures of the other planets, but I pass over this for brevity. Daly, in his commentary on the aforementioned opposition regarding the nature of the Moon, refers to Albumazar's arguments. Daly, explaining Ptolemy's words, says: Ptolemy taught us that the virtues of the stars follow the magnitude of their bodies, and the Moon's body is small, as narrated in the Almagest, and its work follows the Sun's work in one part. He says here that this is because it is very close to the Earth, and its virtue reaches us quickly, and because of this, its work appears more than the work of other stars. And we see visibly that it attracts the humors of the Earth and the bodies that follow it, and because it has light from the Sun, it operates in earthly things with a moderate accidental heat, with which it causes the humors existing in bodies to putrefy and change, as shown in physics and in nature. And it is manifest that light causes heat, and this can be proven by mirrors that burn and similar things. And since the Moon has light from the Sun, it is fitting that it has a moderate heat, which it produces as if by accident. And this is what Ptolemy means to say. And because Albumazar did not understand this, he believed that he erred in his statement, saying, "How can humid smokiness ascend to the Moon when smokiness cannot ascend beyond 16 stadia, and the Moon is quite far?" I, however, laughed at such statements as these, and I said, "How did he not understand this?" For Ptolemy did not mean to say that humid smokiness ascends all the way to the Moon, but he said that the ascent of humid smokiness towards the Moon teaches us that it attracts the humors of the Earth and other bodies following it, just as a magnet attracts iron. Alfraganus says, on the authority of Ptolemy, that the magnitude of the Moon's body is one part of 39 parts of the Earth; and he says that the largest body of all bodies is the Sun, and after the Sun, the largest stars are 15. For he says that each of them contains the Earth 107 times. Third in magnitude is Jupiter, and fourth is Saturn. After Saturn are the fixed stars of the second order, each of which contains the Earth 90 times. After them are the stars of the third order, each of which contains the Earth 72 times. After them are the stars of the fourth order, each of which contains the Earth 54 times. After them are the stars of the fifth order, each of which contains the Earth 37 times. After them are the stars of the sixth order, and the smallest of them, indeed the smallest of all that are seen, the proof of which is placed.

Deinde ponit 02 diné planetarú in regimine corporis post natiuitatem quátum ad proceffuz in etatibus: z dicit o luna icipit natú regere ab ingreſſu eius in mundűiz re git ipſum quattuor annis qui funt anni nutritionis. Deinde mercuri regit ipſum decem annis. Deinde venus octo. Deinde sol nouem. Deinde mars is.post boc Jupiter.i2. Et ille planeta cui eſt.iz.bora ille est dis illius noctis: 7cum? est.25.bora.f.pma hora diei ſequétis illius est dies ſequens. 7 auctor ponit exemplum in littera.ideo non est neceſſe vt ponam.z lic completa est expoĥ tio fecunde differentie buius libri. I quia annuente deo.f differentia bui libri in qua auctor determinat de eſſe planetarum in ſemetipſis a de eſſe eorum adinuicem ſcilicet vnius ad alterum. Et diuidit in duas partes: quoniam in ima determinat de eſſe eorum in ſemet ipſis. In secunda parte de eſſe coumadinuicem.fess cunda eſt ibi. De eſſe auté ipſorum adinuicem.Et illa diuidit in duas:qm smo determinat de effe qnqs pla netarű reſpectu luminariú Scöa de effe eop iater ſe ſcoa ibi:eé aut prio de terminat de effe eoruz in ſeipſis. Et eſt ſnia littere talis. Planete aliquádo di cunt afcendétes z aliquádo deſcendentes 7 aliquando dicunt aucti lumine z magnitudinerz aliquádo diminuti lumine z magnitudine. Et quádoqs diss cunt aucti numeroz qnqs diminuti numero. Et quis dicunt aucti motu z qnqz diminuti in motu. Et omnes planete preter ſolem dicunt quiqs ſepten trionales'z qiz meridionales.Et dicunt afcendentes a deſcédétes in ſepte trione z meridie. Jſta ſunt que accidunt planetis in ſemetipſis.Et ifta expos nit auctor 02 per ordine in littera dicés o planeta quies aſcédit in circulo ſuo ec centrico ita o fit in ſuperiori parte eccentrici ſuino diftans ab auge per.90. gradus ante vel retro z tunc lumen eius apparet minus 7 etiam apparet mi no2 in quantitate z eius curſus eſt tunc tardus quantum ad motů centri epi cycli Dicitur tunc fortior. Quando autem est aliqua longitudinum medias rum eccentrici fui.f.distans abauge per 9o.gradus precile tunc est equalis in lumine z magnitudine a curſu.i.non dicitur magnus neqs paruus in lumi ne vel magnitudine z motus centri epicycli eſt equalis cum medio motuip hus.Cum vero fuerit extra bec loca ſcilicet distans ab auge plus.go.gradi bus ante vel retro tunc deſcendit z apparet maior lumine z magnitudine z mouetur centrum epicycli tunc velocius of medius motus ipfius.Et cú fus erit argumentum planete equatum minus.180.gradibus fcilicet quando ad ditur equatio argumenti ſuper medium motum tunc dicitur auctus nume ro. Et fi argumentum equatum fuerit plus.i80.gradibus.f.quando equatio argumenti ſubtrabitur a medio motu tunc dicitur numero diminutus.Sed in luna quando addiť equatio dicit aucta numero:quando ſubtrabitur dicif Diminuta numero. Et quando aliquis trium planetarum altiorum mouetur ==End of OCR for page 4==
translation: The Earth contains 18. The largest body after them is Mars. After Mars, Earth. After Earth, Venus. After Venus, the Moon. And lastly, Mercury. It is clear, therefore, according to Alfraganus, that the largest of all bodies is the Sun, and the smallest is Mercury, with the exception of generable and corruptible things. Regarding the distances of these bodies from the Earth, Alfraganus says that the closest distance of the Moon from the Earth is 33 times the Earth's semi-diameter and 2/20 of it, which will be 104,120 miles. And the longest distance of the Moon, which is the closest distance of Mercury, is 64 times the Earth's semi-diameter and 7/6 of it, which is 208,542 miles. And the longest distance of Mercury, which is the closest distance of Venus, is 167 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 542,750 miles. And the longest distance of Venus, which is the closest distance of the Sun, is 1120 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 3,640,000 miles. And the longest distance of the Sun, which is the closest distance of Mars, is 1220 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 3,965,000 miles. And the longest distance of Mars, which is the closest distance of Jupiter, is 8876 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 28,847,000 miles. And the longest distance of Jupiter, which is the closest distance of Saturn, is 14,405 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 46,862,500 miles. And the longest distance of Saturn, which is equal to the distance of the fixed stars, and the quantity of the semi-diameter of the circle of the signs, is 20,110 times the Earth's semi-diameter, which is 65,357,500 miles. And when this is doubled, the quantity of the entire diameter of the circle of the signs, i.e., the eighth sphere, will be 130,715,000 miles. And when this is multiplied by 3 and 1/7, the quantity of the circumference of the circle of the signs, or of any other larger circle described in the eighth sphere, will be 410,818,570 miles. And the measure of each degree of the greater circle will be 1,141,160 miles. Regarding the order of the planets: Here he sets the order of the planets in the conception of children, namely, the order in governing the fetus as long as it is in the mother's womb. He says that Saturn governs it from the hour of conception until the end of one month. In the second month, Jupiter governs it. In the third, Mars. In the fourth, the Sun. In the fifth, Venus. In the sixth, Mercury. In the seventh, the Moon. And because of this, as he says, the child in the seventh month seeks to exit, because the operations of all the planets are completed in him; and those who are born then can live for the same reason. Then the governance returns to Saturn, and he governs it in the eighth month; and then those who are born do not live because of the malice of Saturn. Then Jupiter governs it in the ninth month; and then they commonly are born and live because of the goodness of Jupiter. Then he sets the order of the planets in the governance of the body after birth, as to the progression in ages; and he says that the Moon begins to rule the native from its entry into the world, and rules it for four years, which are the years of nourishment. Then Mercury rules it for ten years. Then Venus for eight. Then the Sun for nine. Then Mars for 15. After this, Jupiter for 12. Then Saturn until the end of life. The Dragon's Head has the nature of the Tail. Here he sets the nature of the Head and Tail of the Dragon.

translation: If there is any day. Here he sets the order of the planets as to the lords of days and hours. He says that if a day belongs to a planet, the first hour of that day, according to which the first hour begins from the rising of the Sun, belongs to that planet. And the second hour belongs to the planet that succeeds in the order of the heavens, descending. And the third to the third planet in order, and so on in order until the end of the 12 hours of the artificial day. And that planet to which the 12th hour belongs is the lord of that night. And to whom the 25th hour, i.e., the first hour of the following day, belongs, that is the following day. And the author gives an example in the text, so it is not necessary for me to give it. And thus the exposition of the second difference of this book is completed. And because, God willing, the third difference of this book, in which the author determines the being of the planets in themselves and their being in relation to each other, namely, one to another. And he divides it into two parts: because in the first he determines their being in themselves. In the second part, their being in relation to each other. The second is there: "Regarding their being in relation to each other." And he divides that into two: because first he determines the being of the five planets with respect to the luminaries. Second, their being among themselves. The second is there: "But first he determines their being in themselves." And the meaning of the text is this: Planets are sometimes said to be ascending and sometimes descending, and sometimes they are said to be increased in light and magnitude, and sometimes diminished in light and magnitude. And sometimes they are said to be increased in number, and sometimes diminished in number. And sometimes they are said to be increased in motion, and sometimes diminished in motion. And all planets except the Sun are said to be five northern and five southern. And they are said to be ascending and descending in the north and south. These are the things that happen to the planets in themselves. And the author explains these in order in the text, saying that a planet that ascends in its eccentric circle, so that it is in the upper part of its eccentric, not distant from the apogee by 90 degrees before or after, then its light appears less, and it also appears smaller in quantity, and its course is then slow as to the motion of the center of the epicycle. It is then said to be stronger. But when it is at one of the middle longitudes of its eccentric, i.e., distant from the apogee by exactly 90 degrees, then it is equal in light and magnitude and course, i.e., it is not said to be large or small in light or magnitude, and the motion of the center of the epicycle is equal to its mean motion. But when it is outside these places, namely, distant from the apogee by more than 90 degrees before or after, then it descends and appears greater in light and magnitude, and the center of the epicycle then moves faster than its mean motion. And when the equated argument of the planet is less than 180 degrees, namely, when the equation of the argument is added to the mean motion, then it is said to be increased in number. And if the equated argument is more than 180 degrees, i.e., when the equation of the argument is subtracted from the mean motion, then it is said to be diminished in number. But in the Moon, when the equation is added, it is said to be increased in number; when it is subtracted, it is said to be diminished in number. And when any of the three superior planets moves

Et cum elongat fuerit plus.90.gra dibus ab interfectione eſt ſeptétrionalis deſcedens.i.minuens latitudinem quouſqs elongatus fuerit ab interfectione per.iso.gradus z tunc eft interfes ctione oppoſita pime z nulla habet latitudinem.i.eſt in eclyptica:z cuz tranfi uerit illam fit eius latitudo meridiana z eſt meridionalis deſcendés quouſq elongat fuerit ab interfectione pma per.270 gradus. Et cu tranfiuerit buc numerum eft meridionalis afcendens quousqß reuertif ad interfectionem primam ubi latitudo eius erit nulla. Dec eſt ſententia littere de eſſe planeta rum in ſemetipſis. E eſſe auté ipſoruz adinuicé tra ctemusic determinat de leum adiicen Prius determinauit de eſſe planetarum in ſeip pmo de eſſe quinqs planetarū reſpectu luminariu. Et diuidiť in quattuor pres ſcom quattuor el que habét planete erga ſolez 2 lunam. Pris mum est viſio faciei ad facié. Scom est ſecuritas. Lertiú est cobuſtio. Quara tum eſt orientalis z occidentalis. Primo ergo determinat de viſione faciei ad faciem.Scdo de ſecuritate. Lertio de cobustive. Quarto de oriétalitate z occidétalitate. Prima ps est in pn. Scda icipit ibi:z ex hoc ductoria. Lertia ibi:z ois plaeta. Quarta ibi:z ex quo altiores. Snia sme ptis est. Qn alige planeta fuerit occidétalis a ſole ſ. oriať poſt ſolis ortuzz fuerint inter ipm ſolem tot figna quot funt inter domű illius planetez leonem qui est dom ſolis talis planeta dicit eſſe in viſione ſolis facie ad faciem.Uerbi gra:capzi cornus est domus ſaturni z eſt ſextu ſignum a domo ſolis.f.leone.cum igit fuerit ſaturn? in ſexto ſigno a ſole cóputando numerű ſignori a ſole verſus ſaturnum ſcom ordinem ſignorum.Aliter enim non eet occidentalis ſi non cóputaret boc modo tunc dicit ipſum videre facie ad faciem. Etfignú ſagit tarii eft qntum a leone.cum igit Jupiter fuerit in qnto ſigno a ſole incipiédo a ſole verſus iouem Fm ordinem ſignorum dicit videre ipſum facie ad faciē z'fic de alijs. Et quando aliquis planeta fuerit orientalis a luna z fuerit inter ipſum z lunam ſcom ordinem ſignorum tot figna quot ſunt inter domu ipfi tis planete z domum lune.f.cancrum tunc dicit eſſe in eius viſione facie ad faciem.Uerbi gratia:cancer est fignum fextum ab alia domo ſaturni.f.ab ağ rio:ids cum fuerit luna a ſaturus in.6.igno eſt inter eos viſio facie ad facie Et ét cancer est fignú gntű a domo iouis.f.a pifcibió cu fuerit luna in.5. ſigno a loue im ordinem fignorum est inter eos viſio facie ad faciemiz fic de alijs ſuo modo poteris exemplificare, simpile opamp отти винай ==End of OCR for page 5== X hoc ductoria planete. bi betere nat de.2.effe. 5.planetarū erga luminaria.f.de ductoria quod idem est o fecus ritas. Et est s[i]nia talis qn aliqs planeta est in suo baim z in aliquo angulorū ascédetis.sin ascédéte vel in.io.vel.7.vel.4.domoz ali q8 luminarium sit in aliquo anguloy ab ipso planeta ita q sit planeta in die orientalis a sole z in nocte occidétalis a luna.boc est vt sit in die in.io.signo a sole tuc eniz est in angulo a sole z oriétalis.vel in nocte in. 4.signo a luna.tuc eni est in angulo a luna z occidétalis tunc dicit eé in sua ductoria. Angulus aut oppositionis non bét hic locu. Ró'est qe planeta existés in opposito solis vel lune nec est ouétalis nec occidétalis. Poftea dicit o ois planeta, a tépore quo abscondit sub radijs solis vis ad temps quo icipit apparere dicit cóbu stus.Et incipit coburi qn incipit itrare radiosiz cũ venerit ppe solé infra.i2. gradus vocat oppreffus: ci fuerit cú sole in eodé gradua fuerit inter cos min semidiametro solis z cu hoc latitudo planete fuerit minor semidiame tro solis dicit vnit vel incorporatus soli. Et cú trasierit bác vnioné dicit eé in coualescétia donec sit liberat a radijo solis. X quo apparent altiores tres. Hic determinat de oriétalitate z occidétalitate planetax.Dicit tres pre superiores qu exeunt de sub radijs solis z icipiût appares re de mane ante ontú solis quoufs ueniat ad oppositi solis.boc est q sint ppingoses circulo horizontis oriétalis qž occidétalis, qui sol est in li nea medie noctis tuc vocant orientales a dextri. Uocant oriétales eo q ont tur an solemiz vocant dextri eo q funtin dextra pre celi a sole. Pars enim oc cidentalis est dextra z oriétalis sinistra. Ponat enim qbomo vertat faciem suam verfus meridiem patet qy oziés est sibi a sinistris z occidés a dextris.Et qn aligs trium supiozum planetarú trasierit oppositioné solis dicit occidé talis sinister,dicit occidétalis nó g sit in pte occidétali celi a sole imo bui có trariú est veru.Sed di occidétalis pro táto quoccidit poft'solé.Et di sinifter quest in pte oriétali celi a sole que est sinistra km y dictú est. Uen voz mercu ri qlibet cox infra tps quo circuit epicyclű suū cóiungit soli bis.vna vice p Directione.f.in superiori pre epicycli. Alia vice per retrogradationé.f.in iscrior pte epicycli. Igik qn aligs cop sepat a cóiunctiõe solis in medio retrogradas tidis.f.in iseriori pre'epicycli recedit a sole verfus pre occidétalem celi.Et qu elóga fuerit a sole tiny pôt videri videt de mane an ontú solis.ió túc vocat onétalis dexter donec coburat a sole.i. Donec ingat soli in superiori pte epicy, cli per directioné. Et cú sepatus fuerit a sole per directioné videt de vespere post occafuz solis ió túc vocat occidétalis finister donec iterü coburat.Et est Dia iter veneré z mercuriú z tres supiores.ven eniz mercuri qñqz in recef su eop a sole siue in exitu de sub radijs siút oriétales z que in exitu comz de sab radije siút occidétales. Planete aut supiores sempy cum exeut de sub ras dijs solis siunt orientales. Et dicuntur augmentariinsotrudine donec sint slongati a sole per 30.gradus.7 z.30.usqz ad.60.dicuntur sotes. Et post 1
translation: When a planet moves with more than its mean motion, it is called augmented motion. When it moves with less than its mean motion, it is called diminished motion. The same applies to the Sun and Moon. When it moves exactly with its mean motion, it is called equal motion. When Venus or Mercury moves faster than the Sun, it is called augmented motion; when slower, it is called diminished motion. When any of the six planets crosses the node of intersection of its deferent with the ecliptic, which node is called the ascending node, it is northern and ascending, meaning it increases in latitude until it is 90 degrees from the intersection. When it is more than 90 degrees from the intersection, it is northern and descending, meaning it decreases in latitude until it is 180 degrees from the intersection, and then it is at the opposite intersection of the first and has no latitude, meaning it is in the ecliptic. When it crosses that, its latitude becomes southern, and it is southern and descending until it is 270 degrees from the first intersection. When it crosses this number, it is southern and ascending until it returns to the first intersection where its latitude will be zero. This is the meaning of the letter regarding the state of the planets in themselves. Their state relative to each other will be discussed and determined later. First, it determined the state of the planets in themselves, then the state of the five planets with respect to the luminaries. And it is divided into four parts according to the four states that the planets have with respect to the Sun and Moon. The first is face-to-face vision. The second is security. The third is combustion. The fourth is oriental and occidental. Therefore, it first determines face-to-face vision. Second, security. Third, combustion. Fourth, oriental and occidental. The first part is in the first. The second begins there: and from this guidance. The third there: and every planet. The fourth there: and from which the higher ones. The meaning of the first part is: When any planet is occidental to the Sun, that is, it rises after the Sun's rising, and there are as many signs between it and the Sun as there are between the house of that planet and Leo, which is the house of the Sun, such a planet is said to be in face-to-face vision with the Sun. For example: Capricorn is the house of Saturn and is the sixth sign from the house of the Sun, that is, Leo. Therefore, when Saturn is in the sixth sign from the Sun, counting the number of signs from the Sun towards Saturn according to the order of the signs (otherwise it would not be occidental if not counted in this way), then it is said to see it face-to-face. And the sign of Sagittarius is the fifth from Leo. Therefore, when Jupiter is in the fifth sign from the Sun, starting from the Sun towards Jupiter according to the order of the signs, it is said to see it face-to-face, and so on for others. And when any planet is oriental to the Moon, and there are as many signs between it and the Moon according to the order of the signs as there are between the house of that planet and the house of the Moon, that is, Cancer, then it is said to be in its face-to-face vision. For example: Cancer is the sixth sign from another house of Saturn, that is, Aquarius. Thus, when the Moon and Saturn are in the sixth sign, there is face-to-face vision between them. And also Cancer is the fifth sign from the house of Jupiter, that is, Pisces. Thus, when the Moon is in the fifth sign from Jupiter according to the order of the signs, there is face-to-face vision between them, and so on for others in their own way you can exemplify. From this, the guidance of the planet. Here it is determined concerning the second state of the five planets with respect to the luminaries, that is, concerning guidance, which is the same as security. And the meaning is this: when any planet is in its own term and in one of the angular houses (the ascendant, or the 10th, or the 7th, or the 4th house), and one of the luminaries is in one of the angular houses from that planet, so that the planet is oriental to the Sun by day and occidental to the Moon by night. This means that by day it is in the 10th sign from the Sun, for then it is in an angle from the Sun and oriental. Or by night it is in the 4th sign from the Moon, for then it is in an angle from the Moon and occidental. Then it is said to be in its guidance. The angle of opposition does not apply here. The reason is that a planet existing in opposition to the Sun or Moon is neither oriental nor occidental. Afterwards, it says that every planet, from the time it is hidden beneath the Sun's rays until the time it begins to appear, is called combust. And it begins to be combust when it begins to enter the rays. And when it comes near the Sun, within 12 degrees, it is called oppressed. When it is with the Sun in the same degree, and there is less than the Sun's semidiameter between them, and with this the planet's latitude is less than the Sun's semidiameter, it is said to be united or incorporated with the Sun. And when it has passed this union, it is said to be in convalescence until it is freed from the Sun's rays. From which the three higher ones appear. Here it determines the oriental and occidental nature of the planets. It says that the three superior planets, when they emerge from beneath the Sun's rays and begin to appear in the morning before the Sun's rising until they come to the opposition of the Sun, this means that they are closer to the eastern horizon circle than to the western. When the Sun is in the line of midnight, then they are called oriental and dexter. They are called oriental because they rise before the Sun, and they are called dexter because they are in the right part of the sky from the Sun. For the western part is right and the eastern is left. For if a man turns his face towards the south, it is clear that the east is to his left and the west to his right. And when any of the three superior planets has passed the opposition of the Sun, it is called occidental sinister. It is called occidental not because it is in the western part of the sky from the Sun, but the opposite is true. But it is called occidental because it sets after the Sun. And it is called sinister because it is in the eastern part of the sky from the Sun, which is left as stated. Venus and Mercury, each of them, within the time in which it circuits its epicycle, conjoins the Sun twice. Once by direct motion, that is, in the superior part of the epicycle. Another time by retrogradation, that is, in the inferior part of the epicycle. Therefore, when any of them separates from the conjunction of the Sun in the middle of retrogradation, that is, in the inferior part of the epicycle, it recedes from the Sun towards the western part of the sky. And when it has moved away from the Sun as much as it can be seen, it is seen in the morning before the Sun's rising. Therefore, then it is called oriental dexter until it is combust by the Sun, that is, until it joins the Sun in the superior part of the epicycle by direct motion. And when it has separated from the Sun by direct motion, it is seen in the evening after the Sun's setting. Therefore, then it is called occidental sinister until it is again combust. And there is a difference between Venus and Mercury and the three superior planets. For Venus and Mercury, sometimes in their recession from the Sun, or in their emergence from beneath the rays, they are oriental, and when they emerge from beneath the rays, they are occidental. But the superior planets, always when they emerge from beneath the Sun's rays, are oriental. And they are said to increase in magnitude until they are 30 degrees from the Sun. And from 30 to 60, they are called strong. And after 1

author: Alchabitius
title: Planetary States and Interactions
text: az elongati fuerint a solé ultra.60.gradus dicunt orientales entes ad debi litaté usqz ad principium retrogradationis. Et a principio retrogradatióis vsqz ad oppositione solis dicunt orientales retrogradi. Et ab oppositione usqz ad directionem dicuntur occidentales retrogradi. Et a directione usqz ad.30.gra.post directões dicunt occidétales sores. Et a.30.gra.post directio nem quousqz veniat ad.30.gra.prope solem dicitur occidétales euntes ad debilitatem. Et postea dicunt occidétales debiles quousqz comburant. Ue nus voz Mercurius quádo separant a sole per retrogradatióez siunt one tales z vocantur debiles quousqz veniát ad directidez:7 a directione donec sint elongati a sole tantů quátum solent elongari in pncipio retrogradatios nis dicunt orientales fortes. Deinde siunt debiles orientales z quousqz ops primant sub radijs solis:z postea dicunt vniti cum sole:deinde combusti eus tes ad apparitionem:z ab apparatione eox usqz ad elongationem eouma superiori parte epicycli p.60.gra.sunt occidétales fortes:z ab hoc loco usqz ad retrogradatióem sunt occidentales euntes ad debilitatem:z postea usqz coniunctionem sunt occidentales debiles. Si do no intelligeretira hoc mo esse falsa cú non sit poffibile eos elongari a sole usqz ad.60. gra.in 30diaco. Postea dicit quando aliquis planeta exit de sub radijs solis z nulli cõiungi tur corpore vel aspectu est in suo lumine proprio. Iste illorum erga se inuicez. Perius minauit de his q accidunt planetis in semetipsis:z his que acci dunt quos planetis erga luminaria. Hic determinat de his q ac dunt.7.planetis ad se inuicez. Et poffet diuidi in tot partes quot Sunt modi accedentium que accidūt planetis ad inuicé. Et pit reduci ad.i 6. modos sm sniam littere qui sut isti piunctio:vacuatio: cursus:seralitasitras latio luminis:reddit luminis:prohibitio:pulsatio nature:receptio:redditus nature:resrenatio sine Zietas:frustratio:abscisio luminis:veneratio:destru ctio:obseffio:dilectio z odium. Ista exponit auctor in littera p ordinem usqz ad sinem isti differétie. Snia pme pris est talis. Coiunctio est cú fuerint duo planete in duobus signis que se aspiciút aliquo aspectuz ille q fuerit leuioz inter eos.i.velocios in motu trasierit de suo signo pauciones gradus q pó derosus.i.tardioz motu de suo signo.ita o differétia ista sit.6.gra vel infra:z tunc dicit q leuior vadit ad cõiunctioné póderosiozis:verbi gra:pono q sol sit in ariete z trasierit de ipso ariete.6.gra.z Mars sit in Leone z trasierit de ipso.i2.gra.ista duo signa aspiciút se aspectu trino. Et sol qui est velocioz in motu qz mars trasivit pauciores grad de ariete iz mars de leone:z sut in differétia.6.gra. In pposito exemplo sol vadit ad piunctionem martis de aspectu trino. Et si sol eet in.i2.gradu arietis:7 mars in.i2.gradu leonis:tunc effet cóiunctio completa. Et si sol effet vltra duodecimű gradú arietis mars te existente in.i2. Leonis:tunc effet inter eos separatio. Et hec vocatur piun ctio longitudinis. Coniunctio autem latitudinis est cum fuerint duo plane, te in vno signo cõiuncti corporaliter z fuerint equalis latitudinis abeclypti ca ita quod latitudo vnius sit ascendens in septentrione z alterius descedés in septentrione vel vnius ascendens in meridie z alterius descendens in me ridie. Uel si fuerit cóiunctio ex aliquo aspectuum z latitudo vnius fuerit ses ptentrionalis ascendens:alteri meridiana ascendens. Uerbi gratia:pono quod luna cõiungatur saturno in aliquo signo:z in aliquo gradu z minuto illius ita quod saturnus sit iuxta genzabar suum uadens ad septentrionem in latitudinem:z sit eius latitudo.3.minutop 7 luna appropinquet ad cauda Draconis ueniés a septerétione minués latitudinésita quod ei latitudo simis liter sit.3.minutorum hec est cõiunctio veriffima quátum ad longitudinem z latitudiné. Et in hoc casu luna eclypsaret saturnum nifi diuerfitas aspectus lune eet tanta qo impediret. Licet auctor dicat quod latitudo vnius debeat esse ascédés z alterius descendés tamen uidet mihi o in cõiunttióe corpmī in omni casu quo cótingere poterit qo latitudines amboy sint eğles ad cans dem parté. Uolo dicere o ambe latitudines sint sepetriõales vel ambe me ridionales erit cõiunctio latitudinum veridica:z in aspectu oppositiõis silas titudines fuerint equales in diuerfis ptibus ita q vna sit meridionalis z alia septriõalis erit aspectus veridicus quátum ad latitudines. I cum seperatur unus plancta ab alio. Hic ponit vacuatez cursus. Dicit qn aliquis planeta sue rit coniuctus alteri corpore vel aspectu: post separatoez ab illo nó cõiungit alicui alteri corpe vel aspectu quádiu fuerit in illo si gno tunc dicit esse vacuus cursu Aerbi gra:pono q8 luna iungat Joui co pozair in sagittario:z post sepationez eius a Ioue no sit aliqs planeta in sagit tario cui possit coniūgi: nec radij alicuius quem possit aspicere in hoc casu luna é évacua cursu donec exeat de sagittario z lūgat alicui corpe yľ aspectu. I si fuerit planeta in aliquo si gno. Dicit quando aliquis planeta fuerit in aliquo signo vbi nul lus alter planeta sit nec radij alicuius ille planeta quádiu fuerit in signo illo sic se habente dicitur esse feralis: siue siluestris in si militudine hominis non habentis societatem cu aliquo. Et cum ferit separatus. Dicit quado planeta uelo cioris motus sepauerit se a planeta tardioris motor in separabo se ab eo iungatur alteri tunc traffert naturam primi ad s[e]com. Uerbi gratia:pono quod luna separetur a coniun ctione corporali iouis:z antes compleatur separatio coniungatur saturno aliquo aspectu:uel corporaliter luna traffert túc natura Jouis ad saturnuz. Fit etiam alio modo translatio scilicet quádo planeta leuis cõiungit pondes rohori se:rille ponderosus alteri adhuc ponderosiozi se tunc medius tranf fert naturam leuis ad ponderofiorem verbi gra: pono quod luna aspiciat toué aliquo aspectu ziupiter aspiciat saturnu. Iupiter in boc casu transsert naturam lune ad saturnum. Di aut non coniungitur duoplanes Dicit qu qi te fuerint boc mo b
translation: When they are elongated from the Sun beyond 60 degrees, they are called oriental, going to debility until the beginning of retrogradation. And from the beginning of retrogradation until the opposition of the Sun, they are called oriental retrograde. And from the opposition until direct motion, they are called occidental retrograde. And from direct motion until 30 degrees after direct motion, they are called occidental strong. And from 30 degrees after direct motion until they come within 30 degrees of the Sun, they are called occidental, going to debility. And afterwards, they are called occidental weak until they are combust. Venus and Mercury, when they separate from the Sun by retrogradation, become oriental and are called weak until they come to direct motion; and from direct motion until they are elongated from the Sun as much as they usually are at the beginning of retrogradation, they are called oriental strong. Then they become weak oriental until they are oppressed under the Sun's rays; and afterwards, they are called united with the Sun; then combust, going to appearance; and from their appearance until their elongation from the superior part of the epicycle by 60 degrees, they are occidental strong; and from this place until retrogradation, they are occidental, going to debility; and afterwards until conjunction, they are occidental weak. If you do not understand this, it is false, since it is not possible for them to be elongated from the Sun up to 60 degrees in the zodiac. Afterwards, it says that when a planet emerges from under the Sun's rays and is not conjoined to any other body or aspect, it is in its own light. This is about their interactions with each other. Previously, it discussed what happens to planets in themselves and what happens to planets in relation to the luminaries. Here, it determines what happens to the seven planets in relation to each other. And it can be divided into as many parts as there are modes of interaction that happen to planets with each other. And it can be reduced to 16 modes according to the meaning of the text, which are: conjunction, void of course, swiftness, slowness, translation of light, return of light, prohibition, pulsation of nature, reception, return of nature, refranation, frustration, cutting off of light, veneration, destruction, obsession, love, and hatred. The author explains these in the text in order until the end of this difference. The meaning of the first part is this: Conjunction is when two planets are in two signs that aspect each other with some aspect, and the lighter (i.e., faster in motion) of them has passed fewer degrees from its sign than the heavier (i.e., slower in motion) from its sign, so that this difference is 6 degrees or less. And then it is said that the lighter goes to the conjunction of the heavier. For example: I assume that the Sun is in Aries and has passed 6 degrees from Aries, and Mars is in Leo and has passed 12 degrees from it. These two signs aspect each other with a trine aspect. And the Sun, which is faster in motion than Mars, has passed fewer degrees from Aries than Mars from Leo, and they are within a difference of 6 degrees. In the proposed example, the Sun goes to the conjunction of Mars by trine aspect. And if the Sun were in the 12th degree of Aries, and Mars in the 12th degree of Leo, then the conjunction would be complete. And if the Sun were beyond the 12th degree of Aries, with Mars existing in the 12th of Leo, then there would be a separation between them. And this is called a conjunction of longitude. Conjunction of latitude, however, is when two planets are in one sign, corporally conjoined, and are of equal latitude from the ecliptic, so that the latitude of one is ascending to the north and the other descending to the north, or one ascending to the south and the other descending to the south. Or if there is a conjunction from some aspect, and the latitude of one is northern ascending, and the other southern ascending. For example: I assume that the Moon is conjoined to Saturn in some sign, and in some degree and minute of it, so that Saturn is near its genzabar, going north in latitude, and its latitude is 3 minutes, and the Moon approaches the Dragon's Tail, coming from the north, decreasing its latitude, so that its latitude is similarly 3 minutes. This is a very true conjunction as to longitude and latitude. And in this case, the Moon would eclipse Saturn, unless the diversity of the Moon's aspect were so great as to impede it. Although the author says that the latitude of one should be ascending and the other descending, it seems to me that in a corporal conjunction, in every case where it can happen that the latitudes of both are equal to the same part (I mean that both latitudes are northern or both southern), there will be a true conjunction of latitudes; and in an opposition aspect, if the latitudes are equal in different parts, so that one is southern and the other northern, there will be a true aspect as to latitudes. And when one planet separates from another. Here it places void of course. It says that when a planet has been conjoined to another by body or aspect, and after separating from it, it does not conjoin to any other by body or aspect as long as it is in that sign, then it is said to be void of course. For example: I assume that the Moon joins Jupiter corporally in Sagittarius; and after its separation from Jupiter, there is no other planet in Sagittarius to which it can be conjoined, nor the rays of any other that it can aspect. In this case, the Moon is void of course until it leaves Sagittarius and joins another by body or aspect. And if a planet is in some sign. It says that when a planet is in some sign where no other planet is, nor the rays of any other, that planet, as long as it remains in that sign in such a state, is said to be feral, or wild, in the likeness of a man having no society with anyone. And when it has separated. It says that when a faster-moving planet has separated itself from a slower-moving planet, and in separating from it, it joins another, then it transfers the nature of the first to the second. For example: I assume that the Moon separates from a corporal conjunction of Jupiter; and before the separation is complete, it conjoins Saturn by some aspect, or corporally. The Moon then transfers the nature of Jupiter to Saturn. Translation also occurs in another way, namely, when a light planet conjoins a heavier one, and that heavier one conjoins another still heavier one, then the middle one transfers the nature of the light one to the heavier one. For example: I assume that the Moon aspects Jupiter with some aspect, and Jupiter aspects Saturn. Jupiter in this case transfers the nature of the Moon to Saturn. But if it does not conjoin two planets. It says that when they are in this way...

author: Alchabitius
title: Return of Light
text: pofiti quod unus eorum no iungitur alteri sed ambo iungitur alicui tertios tunc si ille tertius aspiciat aliquë locú ex locis circuli pijcit lumen illoy duos rum ad illuz locú. Et si aliqs planeta fuerit in loco radion suor reddit illi lu men illorum duorum. Uerbi gratia:pono quod sol sit in capricomo z venus in piscibus:ita y no aspiciant se. Deinde pono qo mars sit in cácro aspiciés solé de opposito:z veneré de tertio.in 5 casu mars recipit lumé solis z vene ris:z reddit lumina ipsorum ad loca circuli in qbus sunt radij martis:z hoc vo catur redditus luminis. Fit etiam alio modo redditus luminis quádo duo planete nó aspiciunt se: sed tertius transsert lamen vnius ad alterum itaq sepetur ab vno z iungatur alteri: tunc idem est e translatio. Sequitur probibitio. Dicit quádo tres planete sue, rit in uno signo:sed in diuersis gradibus z ille qui fuerit tardios motu traiuerit plures gradus illius signi qua aliquis alion duop tunc medius eox phibet cõiunctionem leuioris cõ ponderofo.verbi gratia:pono quod saturnus sit in.i2.gradu virginis:z pos no qo mars sit in quinto gradu eiufdem virginis.in hoc casu mars petit cõ sunctioné saturni. Sed pono quod luna sitin.8.gradu eiufdem virginis in 15 casu nó esset iudicandum o mars cõiungeretur saturno:quia luna est in me dio 7 phibet eius coniictiõem. Fit etiá phibitio alio modo:ita y duo pla nete sint in vno signo z leuioriungitur ponderosiozi z alter planeta aspiciat eidem póderoſum z aspectus eius sit ita ppe sicut cõiunctio corporalis al terius:tunc ille qui corporaliter iungitur phibet aspiciente ne aspiciat. Et si in hoc casu ynus illoy fuerit ppinquior az alter ille qui fuerit ppinquior ob tinet z dicitur iungi põderofion.verbi gratia:pono quod saturnus sit in.i 2. gradu virginis: mars in 8.gradu eiufdem. Et pono quod iupiter proijcias radios suos ad octaui gradum virginis in hoc casu mars phibet aspectus iouis. Sed si iupiter proijceret radios suos ad nonum gradum esset iudicas dum quod iupiter aspiceret saturnu:7 cum hoc phiberet coniuctioné mar tis. Et si iupiter proijceret radios suos ad.7.gradu eétiudicandů o mare iű geretur saturno z cum hoc probiberet aspectu iouis. Et si iungitur planeta domino illius signi Dicit quido aliquis planeta iungitur per aspectu domino illius signi in quo fuerit uel domino exaltaltiois uel trip icitatisuel termini uel faciei di cicif mittere natura dni illi dignitatis ad ipsum cuius fuerit dignitas.verbi gratia:pono q sol sit in capricomo aspiciés saturnú erntez in virgine.in hoo casu sol est in domo saturni z mittit natura ipsius saturni saturno. Et si leuis planeta fuerit in aliquo loco vbi ba dignitaté aliquá. verbi gra: qö sit doma sua vel exaltatio zé.7 aspiciat aliquë planeta ponderosioré se q nulla babeat Dignitatem in loco eus dicit leuis mittere suá sonitudiné ponderoso: verbi gra:pono y luna sit in tauro.s.in exaltatóe sua a aspiciat saturni existétez in virgine de trino.in boc casu luna natura suam que virtute mittit ipsi saturno licer saturnus nulla babeat dignitate in loco lune. Sed si saturn beat digni tate in loco lune uerbi gra:pono o luna sit in.24.gradu tauri in exaltatione sua z in termino z facie saturni z aspiciat saturni mittit saturno naturam p priam z natură saturni ratione Termini z faciei sue. Et quádo fuerint aliqua dignitatum suay. Dicit planeta leuior aspicit poderofiorem se z leuior habet dignitatez in loco in quo est z ille qué aspicit etiam hz ibidem aliqua dignitaté túc leuis mittit ponderoſo vtrafos naturas.f.sua ppridiz illi qué aspicit:z hec missio vocat receptio.verbi gra:pono q sol sit in ariete in exaltatióe sua z in domo martis z aspiciat matrem aliquo aspect.in hoc casu sol mittit marti naturaz suam ppriaz cũ hoc natură martie róne domus suej mars recipit solem a domo sua. Et si cotingat q inferior sit in dignitate superioris z ex superiorin Dignitate inferioris illa est melior receptio qelſe pót.verbi gra:sit solin arie te in domo martis 7 mars in leone in domo solis z sol aspiciat martem aspe ctu tertio talis receptio integra a perfecta. Hinc sequitur redditus. Hic afferior plante afpicit quem afpicit est combustus sub radijo:vel retrogradus.in 5 casu supior reddit infes riozi virtuté quá sibi misit:q: pp debilitaté suá nó pór retinere eam. Et si in tas li casu vteros fuerit in angulo vl'in succedéti túc reddit sit cu psicuo, Et si ins ferior fuerit in angulo z supior no ita ti g supior recipiat inferioré talis red dit etiá sit cú picuo. Et si inferior fuerit cades ab angulo z supior in angulo sit redditus cũ pricuo. Et si ambo fuerint cadentes reddit sit cu detrimento. vbi gra:pono q luna sit in tauro z aspiciat mercuriû existérez in capricorno cóbustu in hoc casu luna mittit mercurio natura suá. Sed qe mercurius est cóbus nō pot retinere naturá sibi misia:z reddit lume illud qo recipit ab ea z si in hoc casu ambo fuerint in angulis redditus est proficuus ze. mon Deinde sequitur almene. oz vult cõiungi supion: sed anteqz cópleať cõiunctio inferiori sit retrogradus hoc vocat resrenatio verbi gratia:pono e saturnus sit in. 2. gradu virginis:z venus in principio virginis vades ad cõiunctioné saturni. Sed cum puenerit venus ad.8.gra dum uel nonum.s.anteqz cotingat saturnú incipit retrocedere.in tali casu re frenatur venus ne cõiungatur saturno:z idem intellige de aspectu si iret ad aspectu:sed anteqz aspectus compleret inciperet retrocedere. Hacsequitur alcborad.i.cótrarietas. Dicit quod cótrarietas est quádo tres planete fuerint in vno signo quorum ynus sit ponderosus:z alj duo leues:it vnus duorum leuium trasiverit pon derosum z alter petat coniüctioné ponderoſi:sed anteqz coniungať ponde rofoille qui trasiverit ponderosum sit retrogradus: coniungitur pondes rofo per retrogradationem:z obuiat alteri leuistic retrogradus destruit cõ iunctionë illius cui obuiat cu poderoso. Uerbi gratia:pono quod sit saturn in.8.gradu virgis:z mars sit in.i 2.gradu eiufdez: z iupiter sit in pncipio vir ginis petens coniunctionem saturni:sed antequá coniungatur sibi:mars ins cipiat retrogradariz coniungi saturno per retrogradationem z obuiet loui bij
translation: Suppose that one of them is not joined to the other, but both are joined to some third planet. Then, if that third planet aspects some place in the circle, it projects the light of those two to that place. And if any planet is in the place of its rays, it returns to it the light of those two. For example, I assume that the Sun is in Capricorn and Venus in Pisces, so that they do not aspect each other. Then I assume that Mars is in Cancer, aspecting the Sun by opposition and Venus by trine. In this case, Mars receives the light of the Sun and Venus, and returns their lights to the places of the circle where the rays of Mars are. This is called the return of light. The return of light also occurs in another way when two planets do not aspect each other, but a third planet transfers the light of one to the other, so that it separates from one and joins the other. Then it is the same as translation. Next follows prohibition. It says when three planets are in one sign, but in different degrees, and the one that is slower in motion has traversed more degrees of that sign than either of the other two, then the middle one prohibits the conjunction of the lighter with the heavier. For example, I assume that Saturn is in the 12th degree of Virgo, and I assume that Mars is in the 5th degree of the same Virgo. In this case, Mars seeks the conjunction of Saturn. But I assume that the Moon is in the 8th degree of the same Virgo. In this case, it would not be judged that Mars would be conjoined to Saturn, because the Moon is in the middle and prohibits its conjunction. Prohibition also occurs in another way: so that two planets are in one sign and the lighter one joins the heavier one, and another planet aspects the same heavier one, and its aspect is as close as the bodily conjunction of the other. Then the one that is corporally joined prohibits the aspecting planet from aspecting. And if in this case one of them is closer than the other, the one that is closer obtains and is said to join the heavier. For example, I assume that Saturn is in the 12th degree of Virgo, and Mars in the 8th degree of the same. And I assume that Jupiter projects its rays to the 8th degree of Virgo. In this case, Mars prohibits the aspect of Jupiter. But if Jupiter projected its rays to the 9th degree, it would be judged that Jupiter would aspect Saturn, and with this, it would prohibit the conjunction of Mars. And if Jupiter projected its rays to the 7th degree, it would be judged that Mars would be joined to Saturn, and with this, it would prohibit the aspect of Jupiter. And if a planet joins the lord of that sign, it says when any planet is joined by aspect to the lord of that sign in which it is, or to the lord of exaltation, or triplicity, or term, or face, it is said to send the nature of the lord of that dignity to the one whose dignity it is. For example, I assume that the Sun is in Capricorn, aspecting Saturn existing in Virgo. In this case, the Sun is in the house of Saturn and sends the nature of Saturn itself to Saturn. And if a light planet is in some place where it has some dignity, for example, that it is its house or exaltation, etc., and it aspects some heavier planet that has no dignity in its place, it says the light one sends its strength to the heavier. For example, I assume that the Moon is in Taurus, that is, in its exaltation, and aspects Saturn existing in Virgo by trine. In this case, the Moon sends its nature, which is virtue, to Saturn itself, although Saturn has no dignity in the place of the Moon. But if Saturn has dignity in the place of the Moon, for example, I assume that the Moon is in the 24th degree of Taurus, in its exaltation and in the term and face of Saturn, and aspects Saturn, it sends its own nature and the nature of Saturn by reason of its term and face. And when there are some of its dignities, it says a lighter planet aspects a heavier one, and the lighter one has dignity in the place where it is, and the one it aspects also has some dignity there, then the light one sends both natures to the heavier, that is, its own and that of the one it aspects. And this sending is called reception. For example, I assume that the Sun is in Aries in its exaltation and in the house of Mars, and aspects Mars by some aspect. In this case, the Sun sends its own nature to Mars, and with this, the nature of Mars by reason of its house, and Mars receives the Sun from its house. And if it happens that the inferior is in the dignity of the superior, and the superior in the dignity of the inferior, that is the best reception that can be. For example, let the Sun be in Aries in the house of Mars, and Mars in Leo in the house of the Sun, and the Sun aspects Mars by a trine aspect. Such reception is complete and perfect. Hence follows the return. Here it is affirmed that the planet that aspects the one it aspects is combust under the rays, or retrograde. In this case, the superior returns to the inferior the virtue it sent to it, because due to its weakness it cannot retain it. And if in such a case both are in an angle or in a succedent house, then the return is with profit. And if the inferior is in an angle and the superior is not, so that the superior receives the inferior, such a return is also with profit. And if the inferior is cadent from an angle and the superior is in an angle, the return is with profit. And if both are cadent, the return is with detriment. For example, I assume that the Moon is in Taurus and aspects Mercury existing in Capricorn, combust. In this case, the Moon sends its nature to Mercury. But because Mercury is combust, it cannot retain the nature sent to it, and returns that light which it receives from it. And if in this case both are in angles, the return is profitable, etc. Next follows almuten. It wants to join the superior, but before the conjunction is completed, the inferior becomes retrograde. This is called refranation. For example, I assume that Saturn is in the 2nd degree of Virgo, and Venus is at the beginning of Virgo, going to the conjunction of Saturn. But when Venus reaches the 8th or 9th degree, that is, before it reaches Saturn, it begins to retrograde. In such a case, Venus is refranated so that it does not join Saturn. And understand the same for an aspect if it were going to an aspect, but before the aspect was completed, it began to retrograde. Next follows alchoborad, that is, contrariety. It says that contrariety is when three planets are in one sign, of which one is heavy, and the other two are light, so that one of the two light ones has passed the heavy one, and the other seeks the conjunction of the heavy one. But before it joins the heavy one, the one that has passed the heavy one becomes retrograde, and joins the heavy one by retrogradation, and meets the other light one, and being retrograde, it destroys the conjunction of the one it meets with the heavy one. For example, I assume that Saturn is in the 8th degree of Virgo, and Mars is in the 12th degree of the same, and Jupiter is at the beginning of Virgo, seeking the conjunction of Saturn. But before it joins it, Mars begins to retrograde and joins Saturn by retrogradation and meets Jupiter.

author: Alchabitius
title: Frustration and Abscission of Light
text: in boc cafu mars deftruit coniunctione iouis cũ faturno. Sequitur alpbariz id eft fruſtratio. o frustratio est qn planeta leuis petit cõiunctionem ponderoſiſed antequá compleat coniunctio ponderofus intrat aliud fignum z fint radij alicuius al terius planete in pncipio illius ſigni tunc quando leuis volens coniungi põ derofo intrat illud fignum cóiungit radijs illius planete ibi afpicientisiz fic annullat coniunctio pma:verbi gra:pono quod faturnus fit in.29 gradu le onis z mars fit in.24.gradu eiufdé petens cõiunctionem faturni. Sed and iungat mars faturno faturnus intret virginez:z pono qu iupiter afpiciat pri mum gradum iginis.in hoc cafu mars exiens de leone cóiungit radijs ios uis:z fic annullat coniunctio martis z ſaturni. IDanc quoqz fequitur abſciſiolumis. Dicit q abſciſio luminis eft qn aligs planeta leuis petit cõiōctioné póderofi 7 in ſcoo figno a figno põderoſifit alter planeta ſed anteqz leuis iūgať pôde roſo ille qui est in ſevo signo fit retrograd z lūgiť poderoso.itali cafu retros gradus abfcindit lumé póderoſi a plaeta qui volebat ei ligi. Et eft qſi idé qu prietas fed differt in boc quin prietate planeta retrograd no folú iungiť pô deroſo fed etiam iungit leui qui volebatiungi ponderofo per obuiationem z pót exemplificari ficut ibi fuit dictum. Fit etia alio modo abſciſio luminis.f. quando planeta leuis petit cõiunctionem ponderofi zille ponderofus petit cóiunctioné alteri ponderofſious 7 iungit elanteqz lenis iungat þmo póde rofo.verbi gra:pono qõ mars petat cõiûctionéiouis z iupiter patet piūctio nem faturni z anteqz mars iungat toui iupiter iungat ſaturno:in tali caſu ſas turnus abſcindit lumen iouis a marte. Sunt quoque bis planetis.urd planete in fortant zin gbufdam debilitant z in abuſda locis fiunt fortune zin qbufda ifortune.Fiunt fortune in afpectu ſextili vel tertio bonorú z vt in fortune fint cadentes ab eis iuxta o intelligendű est q qn alige planeta eſt in codé figno cum alio vel in ärto vel in ſeptimo vel decimo ab ipfo dicit effe in angulo ab illo. Et fi fuerit in froo ſigno ab ipſo velin auto vel in octauo vel in. 1.dicitur eé.abipfo in fuccedenti. Et fi fuerit ab ipso in tertio figno vel in.6.vel.9.vel 12. dicit effe in cadenti ab ipfo.fiunt etiam fortune quando feparantur ab vna fortuna z iunguntur alteri aut quod fint inter radios duarum fortunas rum a boc vocatur veneratio a quibufdam. Fiunt etiam fortune quando funt vniti fole in codem gradu:aut quod fint in eius afpectu tertio vel ſexti li vel in afpectu trino vel ſextili lune:z etiam vt fint aucti numero z lumine:7 quod fint in aliqua dignitatum fuarum velin ſuo baiz. Et planete fiunt for tiores quando afcendunt in circulo augis 7 quádo funt feptentrionales vel afcenduntin ſeptentrione. Similiter fortificantur quando funt in Ratione ſe cunda euntes ad directionem.Similiter quando exeunt deſubradijs folie quando funt in angulis vel in fuccedétib". Ettres ſupiores fonificat quá do funt orientales. Et fi afpiciunt folem aſpectu ſextili augmetatur cop forti tudo. Similiter quádo fuerit in quartis masculinis vel in ſignis masculinis. Fortitudo folis etiam augmétatur in quartis z in fignis masculinis preraz in libra.ibi enim no fonificat ppter cafum ab exaltatione licet libra fit fignu masculinum z etia in quadra maſculina. Et tres inferiores planete ſcilz exis ftentes fub fole fignificantur quado funt occidétales a folert in quartis zin fignis femininis. Et luna fortificatur cum fuerit in nocte fuper terra:z in die fub terra z cum fuerit in ſigno z in loco femininor cum fuerit in exaltatio ne folis fcilicet in ariete. Ex ifortunio nero planetarum ici quod planete hút infortune idest infortunant quando funt in aſpectibus malox:z quádo con iungūtur malis per corpus vel per aſpectuzita qo inter eos 7 corpus mali velinter eos z radios mali fit minus termino vnius planete. Et infortunan tur qn ſunt in terminis malop aut in domibus maloy. Uerbi gratia:iupiter vel venus infortunatur in terminis ſaturni z martis:z in domibeop.Etifo: tunátur qn ifortune eleuant fuper ipfos ita qå fint infortune in.io.vel.ii.fis gno ab ipfis.
