Juxta quintelligédum est quod planeta exiftens in.io.figuo ab alio dicit eleuari ſuper ipſum:ficut exiftens in.io.domo eleuatur ſuper afcés dens:z si istud fiat fine receptióe eſt peius. Uerbi gra:pono quod iupiter fit in principio ſagittariiz ſaturnus in principio virginis.in boc cafu faturnus eleuat ſuper iouem. Uirgo enim est decimú fignum a ſagittario. Infotună tur etiá quádo funt in coniuctione ſolis: vel in oppofito folis:velin afpectu eius quarto. Etiam infortunatur quádo funt in capitibus fuorum genzabar ideft in interfectionibus deferentiu cozum cũ eclyptica fine in capite vel cau da diaconis con:vel ppe becloca per i 2. gradus vel infra maxime si in bis locio luna fuerit impedita ab eis: vel fol ſcilicet vt luna:vel ſol iungat eis in bis locis vraſpiciat eos aſpectu inimico. Ratio aut quare ifortunant in bis locis eft quia poffunt ibi eclypfari. Infortunant etiá quádo diſtát a ſole per quattuor grad ante vretro. Et quádo fuerint inter corpa vel radios duo rum malozu:ita qo ſeperent ab vno malo corpore vel afpectu ziungantur alteri malo:aut fuerit malus:uel radij eius in ſigno quod est ante cum z altt malus uel radij eius in ſigno quod eſt poſt eum.túc enim dicitur obfeffus. Uerbi gra:pono giupiter fit in virgine zin.ii.gradu:z mars vel radij mar tis fint in.io.gradu eiufdé z ſaturn vel mars fint in.i2.gra.eiufdez in 15 cafu lupiter diceret obfeffus a duab infortunis. Eodé mo dicit de fignis q funt obſeſſa qn vnus malus eſt in eius pncipio z alter in eius fine. Sed qu fol vt fortuna afpicit planeta vel fignuz obſeſſum aſpectu ſextili:vel tertio ira y fit aspect ad.7.gradus prope vel infra:tunc per talem afpectu ſoluit obſeio. Infortunatur ét quádo funt retrogradi vel combufti aut cadentes ab angu lis. Et debilitant qn funt tardi curſus quod vadát minus medio motu eop. Et qui funt in statione pma euntes ad retrogradatiões:aut o fint in gradib tenebrosis:aut planete masculini in fignis vi gradib femininisiz in die fub terra z in nocte fuper terram:aut feminini in ſignis masculinisz in gradib q lij
translation: In this case, Mars destroys the conjunction of Jupiter with Saturn. This is followed by 'alphariz', that is, frustration. Frustration is when a light planet seeks a conjunction with a heavy planet, but before the conjunction is completed, the heavy planet enters another sign, and there are rays of some other planet at the beginning of that sign. Then, when the light planet, wanting to conjoin with the heavy one, enters that sign, it conjoins with the rays of that planet aspecting there, and thus the first conjunction is annulled. For example, I assume that Saturn is in the 29th degree of Leo, and Mars is in the 24th degree of the same sign, seeking a conjunction with Saturn. But before Mars conjoins Saturn, Saturn enters Virgo, and I assume that Jupiter aspects the first degree of Virgo. In this case, Mars, leaving Leo, conjoins with the rays of Jupiter, and thus the conjunction of Mars and Saturn is annulled. This is also followed by the abscission of light. It is said that the abscission of light occurs when a light planet seeks a conjunction with a heavy planet, and in the second sign from the heavy planet's sign, there is another planet. But before the light planet can conjoin with the heavy one, the planet in the second sign becomes retrograde and conjoins with the heavy one. In such a case, the retrograde planet cuts off the light of the heavy planet from the planet that wanted to conjoin with it. This is almost the same as 'prietas', but it differs in this: in 'prietas', the retrograde planet not only conjoins with the heavy one but also conjoins with the light one that wanted to conjoin with the heavy one by opposition, and it can be exemplified as was said there. Abscission of light also occurs in another way, namely, when a light planet seeks a conjunction with a heavy planet, and that heavy planet seeks a conjunction with another heavier planet and conjoins with it before the light planet conjoins with the first heavy one. For example, I assume that Mars seeks a conjunction with Jupiter, and Jupiter seeks a conjunction with Saturn, and before Mars conjoins Jupiter, Jupiter conjoins Saturn. In such a case, Saturn cuts off the light of Jupiter from Mars. There are also these planets. Planets are strengthened and in some places weakened, and in some places they become fortunate, and in some places unfortunate. They become fortunate in a sextile or trine aspect of benefics, and they are fortunate when they are cadent from them, according to what is to be understood: that when any planet is in the same sign as another, or in the fourth, seventh, or tenth from it, it is said to be in an angle from it. And if it is in the second sign from it, or in the fifth, eighth, or eleventh, it is said to be in a succedent from it. And if it is in the third sign from it, or in the sixth, ninth, or twelfth, it is said to be cadent from it. They also become fortunate when they separate from one benefic and conjoin with another, or when they are between the rays of two benefics, and this is called 'veneratio' by some. They also become fortunate when they are united with the Sun in the same degree, or when they are in its trine or sextile aspect, or in the trine or sextile aspect of the Moon, and also when they are increased in number and light, and when they are in any of their dignities or in their hayz. And planets become stronger when they ascend in the circle of their apogee, and when they are northern or ascend in the north. Similarly, they are strengthened when they are in the second station, going direct. Similarly, when they emerge from under the Sun's rays, when they are in angles or succedents. And the three superior planets are strengthened when they are oriental. And if they aspect the Sun with a sextile aspect, their strength increases. Similarly, when they are in masculine quarters or in masculine signs. The strength of the Sun also increases in masculine quarters and in masculine signs, except in Libra. For there it is not strengthened because of its fall from exaltation, even though Libra is a masculine sign and also in a masculine quarter. And the three inferior planets, namely those existing below the Sun, are strengthened when they are occidental to the Sun and in feminine quarters and in feminine signs. And the Moon is strengthened when it is above the earth at night, and below the earth during the day, and when it is in a feminine sign and place, and when it is in the exaltation of the Sun, namely in Aries. Regarding the misfortune of planets, know that planets are unfortunate, that is, they are made unfortunate, when they are in the aspects of malefics, and when they conjoin with malefics by body or by aspect, so that between them and the body of the malefic, or between them and the rays of the malefic, there is less than the term of one planet. And they are made unfortunate when they are in the terms of malefics or in the houses of malefics. For example, Jupiter or Venus is made unfortunate in the terms of Saturn and Mars, and in their houses. And they are made unfortunate when malefics are elevated above them, so that the malefics are in the tenth or eleventh sign from them. According to what is to be understood, a planet existing in the tenth sign from another is said to be elevated above it, just as one existing in the tenth house is elevated above the ascendant; and if this happens without reception, it is worse. For example, I assume that Jupiter is at the beginning of Sagittarius and Saturn is at the beginning of Virgo. In this case, Saturn is elevated above Jupiter. For Virgo is the tenth sign from Sagittarius. They are also made unfortunate when they are in conjunction with the Sun, or in opposition to the Sun, or in its quartile aspect. They are also made unfortunate when they are in the heads of their 'genzabar', that is, in the intersections of their deferents with the ecliptic, whether in the head or tail of the dragon, or near these places by 12 degrees or less, especially if in these places the Moon is impeded by them, or the Sun, that is, if the Moon or Sun conjoins them in these places or aspects them with a hostile aspect. The reason why they are made unfortunate in these places is because they can be eclipsed there. They are also made unfortunate when they are distant from the Sun by four degrees before or behind. And when they are between the bodies or rays of two malefics, so that they separate from one malefic body or aspect and conjoin with another malefic; or if a malefic or its rays are in the sign before it, and another malefic or its rays are in the sign after it. Then it is said to be besieged. For example, I assume that Jupiter is in Virgo and in the second degree, and Mars or the rays of Mars are in the tenth degree of the same sign, and Saturn or Mars are in the twelfth degree of the same sign. In this case, Jupiter would be said to be besieged by two malefics. In the same way, it is said of signs that they are besieged when one malefic is at its beginning and another at its end. But if the Sun, as a benefic, aspects a besieged planet or sign with a sextile or trine aspect, so that the aspect is within 7 degrees or less, then by such an aspect the siege is resolved. They are also made unfortunate when they are retrograde or combust or cadent from angles. And they are weakened when they are slow in course, so that they move less than their mean motion. And when they are in the first station, going to retrogradation; or when they are in dark degrees; or masculine planets in feminine signs or degrees, and during the day below the earth and at night above the earth; or feminine planets in masculine signs and in degrees.

author: Alchabitius
title: Debilitation of Planets
text: masculinis 7 in die ſuper terram z in nocte ſub terra. Poc.n.eft cótrariuba 1z ideft fimilitudini eorum. Et debilitantur fiue infortunant qui fut in locis op pofitis dignitată fuay:auto fint in latitudine meridiana ab eclyptica maxie Afuerint defcédentes in eadé latitudine: aut fint in via combusta.f.a medies tate libre vios ad mediú ſcorpidis:aut fint iuncti planete retrogrado:aut ca denti:aut quod nó fint recepti. Et tres fuperiores.f.faturnus iupiter z mars debilitantur cum fuerint occidentales a fole:aut in quartis femininis:z fol debilitatur in ſignis zin quartis femininis:nifi fuerit in nona domo:quin illa gaudet. Tres inferiores ſcilicet ven mercuri z luna debilitant in fignie zin quartis masculinis. Et dicit de planetis quod funt quida cop ſeinuicé Diligentes.Dicit quod quidam planete diligunt feinuicemiz quidam babet fe odio:7 patet in fra.z fic completa et expofitio tertie differétie alcbabicij introductozij ad iudicium. Ifferentia quarta in expoſitó nenomini astrologicorn. ferentijs buius libri in gbus auctor determinauit de eſſe cir, culi ſignoy effentiali z accidétali in prima differétia:z de na turis ſeptem planetay in.ba differentia:z de bis que accidunt ſeptem plane tis in femetipfisiz adinuicem ſin tertia differétia. Sequitur quarta differés tia in qua exponit nomina quibus vtútur magiſtri iudicioruz 2 docet ea ap plicare ad opus.Et poteſt tota illa differétia diuidi in.i6.partes ſcom.i6.ca pitula que ponit in ea. In primo capitulo loquik de cóiunctionibus magnio connumerado eas. In ſecúdo capitulo docet inueftigare gradum afcende tem alicuius natiuitatis. In tertio capitulo docet eligere locum vite in nati uitatib. In quarto caplo docet eligere datorem annor vitein natiuitatib. In.5.docet eligere planeta dñatoré natiuitatis q peeft nato post bylech z al cochode. Jn.6.tractat de pfectu annoy natiuitatis z múdi. Jn.7.c.docet di rigere fignificatoré quelibet ad quélibet locum circuli. Jn.8.docet dirigere gradu aſcenderez in nativitatib.In.9.docet inuenire duodenarias planeta rum z domoy. Jn.i o. docet inuenire nouenarias.In.ii.docet inuenire deca nú. Jn.i 2.docet iuenire dim orbis fiue bore. Jn.i 3.docet inueire annos fris darie in natiuitatib.In.i4.docet cognofcere as planetap eleuat fup alter. Ini 5.docet inuenire qn fit aptio potay. Jn.i 6.logt de bois fortunat z ins fortunatis:vbi pres incipiant patebit in preffu. In pmo ergo.c.loquik genes raliter de magnis cóiunctoibor dicit y cóiunctiões magne fut res fignifică tes destructiões fine mutatiões, qfiunt in 13 feculo:z funt.6.in nuero. Cpu ma maior oib? cóiunctõib? eft piuctio iouis z faturni in pncipio ariet z B fit in.960.annis femel. Scda pinctio est mutatio piñctióisiouis z faturni & una triplicitate ad alia 7 boc fit in.24.0.annis femel. Et qn venit piuctio cop ad'alios triplicitaté anteqz ereatilla vuodecies pingunt. Uerbi gratia:piun crio iouis z faturni nunc eft in triplicitate aerea t incipit in figno gemino0z anno dni.i 32 5.ab illo the in.20.annis.lano dni.i 345.coiungent in aquas rio z ab illo in.20.annis conlungentur in libra z poftea in geminisiz ita m bunc ordinē donecin bis tribus fignis duodecies cõiungantur z fi multipli caueris.20.per.i2.inuenies.240.annos poft quos coniunctio mutabit ſe z intrabit triplicitaté aquatica z ſtabit ibi fimiliter p.240.ános z fic de alijs tri plicitatib itelligas:7 itelligas q ficut dicit o piuctio maria reuertif in. 960. anis ad pncipiú arietj.i.ad triplicitaté igneanta pinctio intrás pncipiű alicui alterius triplicitatis no reuertiť ad pncipium eiufdé nifi poft 960.annos ſta bit igit vt fupra dcm est in ölibet triplicitate.240.annis triplicitates füt quat tuor.multiplica igit.240.per attuo: z inuenies.960. anos. Tertia piŭctio é martis z faturni in cancro in gbuflibet.30.inis.Quarta cóiunctio eft iouis z Saturni in qcüqz signo fuerit:z 15 fit in.20.anis femel. Quita coiunctio é ins troitus magni luminaris in primuz minutuz equinorij vernalis idest ariets. Sexta cõiunctio eſt ſolis z lune z eorum oppofitio. Et quando dicitur afcens dens cõiunctionis magne intelligit de afcendere ſcilicet introit folis in arie tem in illo anno in quo 03 fieri illa cóiunctio.Uerbigratia:coiunctio iouis z faturni fuit in geminis anno dni.iz25. Et afcendens introitus solis in ariete in illo anno fuit taurus.20.gradus:taurus ergo eſt aſcendens illius coniun ctioms. Iſte cóiunctiones que dicte funt vocantur cõiunctiões magne. Alie multe funt piunctiones in planetis quarū numerus eft. i 20.fm Ptolemeű in.50.ppoſitióe centiloquij.Dicit enim ibi, no obliuifcaris eſſe.i 20.piunctio nes que fiút in ftellis erraticis. In illis.n.est maior ſcia cop 5 siût in 5 múdo fufcipiéti incremétum a decrementum. Daly ibi in comento numerat easiz Dicit quod quedam funt coniunctiones binarie z quedam trinarie quedam quatrinarie quedam quinarie quedam fenarie quedam feptenarie. Binaria ant cóiunctio eft quádo duo planete tantum piungitur:z hoc poteft.2i.mo dis variari. Ternaria cõiunctio est quando coniungūtur tres planete z boc poteſt variari triginta quinqz modis. Quatrinaria coniuntio est qn cóun guntur quattuor planete:z boc poteſt variari fimiliter.35.modis. Quinaria coniunctio eft quádo coniúgunt quinqs planeteiz boc poteſt variari.2i.mo dis.Senaria coniunctio eft quádo cóiunguntur fer planete: 7 boc poteſt va riari.7.modis.Septenaria nó eft nifi vna ſcilicet coniunctio omnium ſeptez planetarum. Et si computaueris ſen addideris hos numeros predictos ad inuicem inuenies.i 20.Qui volunt fingulas numerare inueniét cas numes ratas in comento ppoſitionis preallegate. Tex hoc annimodar quod est. In hoc capitulo auctor docet iueftigare gradum afcédenté alis cuius natiuitatis fuppofito qu ſciat fignú afcédés z ignoref gra dus: Boocet ſecunduſententia Ptolomei non fm propria inte tionem.Dicit enim Ptolemeus in.34. ppofitione centiloquij Almusteli sus per locum coniunctionis vel preventionis eſt in primili gradu anguli ex ans gulis omnis natiuitatis humane que fuerit in illa coniunctõe vel puentide. Et omnis gradus qui nó eft fimilis gradui almuté ſuper locú coniunctióis yel puentionis nõ eſt angulus alicuius natiuitatis humane. Sentétia littere biiij
translation: Masculine planets are debilitated when they are above the earth during the day and below the earth at night. This is contrary to their nature and similarity. They are debilitated or unfortunate if they are in places opposite to their dignities, or if they are in southern latitude from the ecliptic, especially if they are descending in the same latitude, or if they are in via combusta (from the middle of Libra to the middle of Scorpio), or if they are conjoined with a retrograde planet, or a falling planet, or if they are not received. The three superior planets (Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars) are debilitated when they are occidental to the Sun, or in feminine quarters. The Sun is debilitated in feminine signs and quarters, unless it is in the ninth house, which it enjoys. The three inferior planets (Venus, Mercury, and the Moon) are debilitated in masculine signs and quarters. It is said of the planets that some of them love each other, and some hate each other, as will be shown below. Thus is completed the exposition of the third difference of Alchabitius's Introduction to Judgment. The fourth difference is the exposition of the names of astrologers. In the differences of this book, the author determined the essential and accidental being of the circle of signs in the first difference, and the natures of the seven planets in this difference, and what happens to the seven planets in themselves and to each other in the third difference. The fourth difference follows, in which he explains the names used by the masters of judgments and teaches how to apply them to practice. This entire difference can be divided into 16 parts, according to the 16 chapters he places in it. In the first chapter, he speaks of the great conjunctions, enumerating them. In the second chapter, he teaches how to investigate the ascending degree of a nativity. In the third chapter, he teaches how to choose the place of life in nativities. In the fourth chapter, he teaches how to choose the giver of years of life in nativities. In the fifth, he teaches how to choose the planet ruling the nativity, which presides over the native after hyleg and alcochoden. In the sixth, he treats the perfection of the years of the nativity and the world. In the seventh chapter, he teaches how to direct any significator to any place of the circle. In the eighth, he teaches how to direct the ascending degree in nativities. In the ninth, he teaches how to find the duodenaries of the planets and houses. In the tenth, he teaches how to find the novenaries. In the eleventh, he teaches how to find the decans. In the twelfth, he teaches how to find the half-orbits or hours. In the thirteenth, he teaches how to find the years of the firdaria in nativities. In the fourteenth, he teaches how to know which planet is elevated above another. In the fifteenth, he teaches how to find when the opening of the gates occurs. In the sixteenth, he speaks of fortunate and unfortunate men, where the beginning will be evident in the text. Therefore, in the first chapter, he speaks generally of the great conjunctions, saying that great conjunctions are things signifying destructions or changes that occur in the world, and there are six in number. The first, greater than all conjunctions, is the conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn at the beginning of Aries, and this occurs once every 960 years. The second conjunction is the change of the conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn from one triplicity to another, and this occurs once every 240 years. And when their conjunction comes to another triplicity, before it occurs twelve times, they conjoin. For example, the conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn is now in the airy triplicity and begins in the sign of Gemini in the year of the Lord 1325. From that time, in 20 years (the year of the Lord 1345), they will conjoin in Aquarius, and from that time, in 20 years, they will conjoin in Libra, and then in Gemini, and so on in this order until they conjoin twelve times in these three signs. If you multiply 20 by 12, you will find 240 years, after which the conjunction will change and enter the watery triplicity and remain there similarly for 240 years, and so you should understand for the other triplicities. And understand that just as it is said that the great conjunction returns in 960 years to the beginning of Aries, that is, to the fiery triplicity, a conjunction entering the beginning of any other triplicity does not return to its beginning until after 960 years. It will therefore remain, as stated above, in each triplicity for 240 years. There are four triplicities. Therefore, multiply 240 by four, and you will find 960 years. The third conjunction is of Mars and Saturn in Cancer in any 30 years. The fourth conjunction is of Jupiter and Saturn in whatever sign it may be, and this occurs once every 20 years. The fifth conjunction is the entrance of the great luminary into the first minute of the vernal equinox, that is, Aries. The sixth conjunction is of the Sun and Moon and their opposition. And when the ascendant of a great conjunction is mentioned, it refers to the ascendant, namely, the entrance of the Sun into Aries in that year in which that conjunction is to occur. For example, the conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn was in Gemini in the year of the Lord 1325. And the ascendant of the Sun's entrance into Aries in that year was Taurus, 20 degrees. Therefore, Taurus is the ascendant of that conjunction. These conjunctions that have been mentioned are called great conjunctions. There are many other conjunctions among the planets, the number of which is 120, according to Ptolemy in the 50th proposition of the Centiloquium. For he says there, "Do not forget that there are 120 conjunctions that occur in the erratic stars." For in them is greater knowledge than in those that occur in this world, receiving increase and decrease. Daly there in the commentary enumerates them and says that some are binary conjunctions, some ternary, some quaternary, some quinary, some senary, and some septenary. A binary conjunction is when only two planets conjoin, and this can vary in 21 ways. A ternary conjunction is when three planets conjoin, and this can vary in 35 ways. A quaternary conjunction is when four planets conjoin, and this can vary similarly in 35 ways. A quinary conjunction is when five planets conjoin, and this can vary in 21 ways. A senary conjunction is when six planets conjoin, and this can vary in 7 ways. A septenary conjunction is only one, namely, the conjunction of all seven planets. And if you compute or add these predicted numbers to each other, you will find 120. Those who wish to enumerate them individually will find them enumerated in the commentary on the aforementioned proposition. The text here indicates that in this chapter, the author teaches how to investigate the ascending degree of a nativity, assuming that the ascending sign is known but the degree is unknown. He teaches this according to Ptolemy's opinion, not according to his own intention. For Ptolemy says in the 34th proposition of the Centiloquium, "The almuten over the place of conjunction or prevention is in the first degree of the angle from the angles of every human nativity that was in that conjunction or prevention. And every degree that is not similar to the degree of the almuten over the place of conjunction or prevention is not an angle of any human nativity." The meaning of the letter is here.

author: Alchabitius
title: Finding the Ascendant for a Nativity
text: talis.eft cum volueris inuenire gradum afcedentem natiuitatis alicuius pi dera cóiunctionem solis z lune que preffit natiuitatem vel oppofitioné fi pre ceffit oppofitio.Si preffit coniunctio natinitas dicit coniunctionalto:i prefs fit oppofitio fiue puentio qo idem est natiuitas vocatur puentionalis, Sings tiuitas fuerit cõiunctionalis cófidera gradum in quo cõiunguntur lumina ria.f.gradum 30diaci inquo fuit coniunctio predens illam natiuitatem.z vis de go planetarum sit fortior in gradu per multitudinem teftimoniorum.ille enim est almutem fup locum coiunctionis. Equa ergo eum ad boram natiui tatis quo equato coſtitue afcendens ad bozam natiuitatis ſcom estimatione ita ti quod sis certus de ſigno afcendente. Si enim deficeres in signo becre gula no valeret tibi. Dabito igik figno ascendente bora natiuitatis: cófidera utrum locus planete quem equaſti tali'dispõe ſtante, sit ppingos gradui afcé dentis vel gradui decime domus a cui eorú fuerit ppingor hunc angulum facies similem gradui planete.boc eft:pone tot gradus signi exiftentis in an gulo quot gradus ptranfiuit planeta de signo in quo est. Et vt euidenti pa= teat sententia littere pono in boc exemplum.CPono quod in boza natiuita tis alicuius afcendens fuit libra ſed nescio quis gradus eius:7 pono & nati, uitas fuit cóiunctionalis z cõiunctio precedens eam fuit in.6.gradu aquarij video o ſaturnus est fortior in illo gradu:ergo equabe ſaturnum ad boram natiuitatis z ponatur & inueniam eum in.20.gradu virginis video oſatur nus in boc caſu est ppinquior afcendenti iz medio celi ſcom afcendens estis matum:faciam ergo angulum afcendentis ad similitudinem gradus ſatur ni.boc eft ponam vigefimum gradum libre in afcendente a si fuiffet,ppingos medio celi feciffem angulum medij celi vigesimum gradum figni ibidem exi stentis.Si autem natiuitas puentionalis fuerit operaberis per gradum ops poſitionis sicut nunc tibi dixi de gradu cõiunctionis ideft confiderabis plas netam bitem plures fortitudines in gradu oppofitionis.Sed dubium eft de gradu puentionis cum in puentione fiue oppofitione sol sit in vno gradu luna in oppofito quem ergo gradum accipiem pro grabu puentióis utrú gradum solis vel gradum lune. Dicit auctor ſcom intentionem Ptolemeig gradum illius luminario accipere debemus qo fuerit fug terram.Sed ad buc reſtat dubium. Ponat o vnum luminare sit in ostu reliquum in occasu 7 fic neutrum erit sap terram nec sub terra. Dicit auctore qdam sapiens di xerunt debemus opari per gradum illius luminaris qo fuerit in onéte.De inde dicit auctor of Ptolemeus dixit si plures planete cóuenirent in dño los ci cõiunctionis vel puentionis.f.g duo planete veltres in loco illo bérét eős les furnitudines videlicet y vous béret ibi tot fortitudines quot alter: tunc Debemus accipere illum qui fuerit in ſuo baim:z ſi glibet eorum fuerit in suo baim tunc debemus accipe illum qui citius deberet mutari ad melius eéiil Inm qui citi itrabit signum in quo bét plures fortitudines qz in illo signo in quo est. Et si fuerint orientales a sole eligas eum qui fuerit foli ppingor:dus - mô nô sit cõbustus. Juxta istam ptem notandum est op alter eft modus in uestigandi gradum afcendentis natiuitatis z ille modus accipit a snia pro, lemeiin.si.ppone centiloquij vbi dicit. Locus lune in natiuitate est ipse gra
translation: This is how you find the ascendant degree of someone's nativity. Consider the conjunction of the Sun and Moon that preceded the nativity, or the opposition if an opposition preceded it. If a conjunction preceded it, the nativity is called 'conjunctional'; if an opposition or 'prevention' (which is the same) preceded it, the nativity is called 'preventional'. If the nativity is conjunctional, consider the degree in which the luminaries conjoined, that is, the degree of the zodiac where the conjunction preceding that nativity occurred. See which planet is strongest in that degree by the multitude of testimonies. This planet is the almuten over the place of the conjunction. Then equate it to the hour of the nativity. Once it is equated, establish the ascendant for the hour of the nativity according to estimation, so that you are certain of the ascendant sign. For if you were to err in the sign, this rule would not be valid for you. Therefore, with the ascendant sign of the nativity's hour known, consider whether the place of the planet you equated, given its disposition, is closer to the ascendant degree or to the degree of the tenth house. Whichever of these it is closer to, you will make that angle similar to the planet's degree. That is, place as many degrees of the sign existing in the angle as the planet has traversed of the sign it is in. And so that the meaning of the letter may be more evident, I provide an example here. I assume that at the hour of someone's nativity, the ascendant was Libra, but I do not know its degree. And I assume that the nativity was conjunctional, and the preceding conjunction was in the 6th degree of Aquarius. I see that Saturn is stronger in that degree; therefore, I will equate Saturn to the hour of the nativity, and let us assume I find it in the 20th degree of Virgo. I see that Saturn in this case is closer to the ascendant than to the midheaven according to the estimated ascendant. Therefore, I will make the ascendant angle similar to Saturn's degree. That is, I will place the twentieth degree of Libra in the ascendant. If it had been closer to the midheaven, I would have made the midheaven angle the twentieth degree of the sign existing there. However, if the nativity is preventional, you will work with the degree of the opposition, just as I have now told you about the degree of the conjunction. That is, you will consider the planet that has more strengths in the degree of the opposition. But there is doubt about the degree of prevention, since in a prevention or opposition, the Sun is in one degree and the Moon in the opposite. Which degree, then, should we take as the degree of prevention? The degree of the Sun or the degree of the Moon? The author says, according to Ptolemy's intention, we should take the degree of the luminary that is above the earth. But even then, doubt remains. Suppose one luminary is rising and the other setting, and thus neither will be above the earth nor below the earth. The author says that some wise men have said we should work with the degree of the luminary that is in the east. Then the author says that Ptolemy said if several planets agreed in the lord of the place of conjunction or prevention, that is, if two or three planets had equal strengths in that place, meaning one had as many strengths there as another, then we should take the one that is in its own 'baim'. And if each of them is in its own 'baim', then we should take the one that would change for the better more quickly, that is, the one that will enter a sign where it has more strengths more quickly than in the sign it is currently in. And if they are oriental to the Sun, choose the one that is closer to the Sun, provided it is not combust. In addition to this part, it should be noted that there is another method of investigating the ascendant degree of a nativity, and that method is taken from Ptolemy's Centiloquy, where it says: The place of the Moon in the nativity is the degree itself.

author: Alchabitius
title: Duration of Gestation and Hyleg Selection
text: dus afcedens de circulo in bora cafus ſpm̃atis in matricem a long lune bo ra caſus ſematis eſt gradus afcendens bora natiuitatis.Eft etiam itelligédu ad euidentiam dicendorum & more infantum in vteris matrum non sunt eq les ſcom o dicit Daly in comentopposicionis iam allegate. Moze auté funt Distincte in.3.f.in maiorem moram z in mediáz in minorem. Minoz autem mora continet.258.dies Media mora continet.273.Maior mora continet 288 dies. Et differentia inter maiorem z minorem funt.30.dies.Jſtud eft in telligédum de his que nafcunt ſcom curfum naturalem.De abotiuis aut̃e z de his qui naſcunt in ſeptimo menſe bec regula non tenet. Quantitas aut̃ more infantis in vtero accipitur a loco lune in figura bora natiuitatis.Si eni in boza natiuitatis luna fuerit in pncipio ſeptime domus ſuper terram f.fus pra horizontem occidentalem tunc infans fuit in utero matris per mora mis noem.f.per.258.dies. Et si luna fuerit in boza natiuitatis in gradu afcenden te tunc infans ſtetit in vtero per moram media.f.per.273.dies.Etfiluna fue rit boza natiuitatis in gradu qui est ante gradum occidentis tunc infans ſtes tit in vtero per mora maioré.1.288.dies. Et nota o cum luna in natiuitate eſt in pte occidentali ppe horizontem ſi aſtronomus credit eam effe ſup ters ram qri eſt fub terra vel econtra errabit in boza conceptionis per.30.dies. Si vero luna fuerit ſuper terram elongata ab occidente ideſt inter oriens z occidés moza in vtero eſt maior minori moza 7 minor media. Dico y eſt ma ioz minori mora ſcom quantitatem elongationis lune ab occidente. Et ſi luña fuerit ſub terra elongata ab afcendente mora in utero eſt maior media z mi nor maiori z eſt maior media ſcom quantitatem elongationis ab afcendens te. Lum ſcom hanc viam volueris gradum afcendentis natiuitatis alicuius inuueſtigare conſtitue afcendens ad hozam natiuitatis ſcom eſtimationes pe pingorem quantum poteris veritati 7 equa lunam ad illam hozam:7 vide in quo loco ceciderit in figura: ſi ceciderit in pncipio ſeptime domus ſubtra he ab boza natiuitatis.258.dies. Et remanebit hora cóceptionis. Si vero ce ciderit inter ſeptimam domum z afcédens.f.ſup terra vide per quot gradus ſit elongata a gradu occidentis 7 pro gbuslibet.i2.gradibus accipe vnú diez z pro quolibet gradu qui'fuerit ultra.i2.accipe duas horas quos dies z hos ras adde ſup mora minore.i.fup.258.dies.Et totum ſubtrahe ab hora nati nitatis z remanebit tpus cóceptiouis. Si vero ceciderit in afcendéte ſubtra he ab hoza natiuitatis.273.dies a remanebit tps cóceptióis. Si vero cecide rit inter afcendés z ſeptima.f.fub terra cõſidera qñtum ſit elongata a gradu afcendente z pro qbuslibet.i2.gradib? accipe vnum diem z p20 quolibet gra du ſeſiduo duas horas 7 numerum dierum z bozarum puenienté adde ſup moram mediam z totum ſubtrabe ab hoza natiuitatis. Si vero luna cecide rit in fine fexte domus ſubtrabe ab hoza natiuitatis moram maioré z rema net hora cóceptionis. Dora igit cóceptionis nota equa lunam ad illam hos ram z gradus in quo inuenta fuerit fuit afcendens in hoza natiuitatis.Licet hec via fit uera ſcoum intentionem fere oium ſapientum. Uidetur tn mihi 7 certus fum q non fufficit ad inueſtigandum gradum in caſu ubi non habeo gradum afcendentem niſi per eſtimatiõem.Supponit enim q ſciam elon. gationem lune a gradu occidétis vel ab afcédente:z ſi ignorem graduz afce dente z per cófequens gradum occidentis quomodo poterofcire diſtantia lune ab aliquo illorum neſcio. Poſito enim q̃ eſtimatio mea deficiat per.12. gradus diſtantia lune ab occidente vel ab afcendente etia deficiet per.i2.gra dus 7 pro illis deberet accipi vna dies z fic deficiet hora cóceptionis in vna Die z defectus ultimus.f.in gradu afcendentis erit qñtú luna mouer vna die. In hoc capitulo auctor 001 cet eligere bylecb.i.ſignifica torem vite in natiuitatib. Snia littere eſt iſta. In eligendo ſignifi catorem vite debemus incipe a ſole ſi natiuitas fuerit in die z fi Ier hoc bylech.b inuenerim ipm ante gradum afcendentem per.5.gradus uel in fra.i.fi inuenerimus ipm ſolem in.5.graibus qui afcéderūt ſup horizõtem.Г. in fine.i2.dom aut ſi inuenerimus ipm in.io.domo vel.i i.domo accipiemus ſolem pro bylech fiue fuerit in ſignis masculinis fiue in ſignis femininis. Si vero nó fuerit in his ſignis debemus inſpicere ſi fuerit in.7.vel.8.vel.9.do mo in fignis masculinis adhuc accipiem ipm pro bylech. Si vero his tri bus locis ia dictis.f.in.7.domo vel.8.vel.9.effet in ſignis femininis non effet aptus ad hoc effet bylech. Si igit ſol nó fuerit aptus bylech aſpiciemus ad lunam ſi eam inuenerimus in afcendente vel in.2.vel in.3.domo aut in.7. vel.8.domo ipſa erit bylech.fiue fuerit in ſigno maſculio ſiue feminino Si vero non fuerit in his locis aſpiciem i inuenerim? eam ante gradum ſigni afcendentis per.5.gradus velinfra:aut in.io.domo aut.4.aut in.5.in ſigno ſe minino adhuc ipſa erit bylech. Si aút i his locis eēt in ſignis maſculinis nõ effet apta bylech. Si vero natiuitas fuerit in nocte debemus incipere pmo a luna. Si inuenerim? eam ante gradum occidentem per.5.gradus vel ifra i.in illis.5.gradib qui funt fub terra er pte occidétis aut i aliquo alior loco rum pdictorum ſcom modum dictum ipſa erit bylech. Et ſi ipſa non fuerit apta aſpiciemus ad ſolem qui ſi fuerit ante gradum ſeptimedomus per.5. gradus velinfra.f.ſub terra aut fuerit in. 4.vel 5.domo erit aptus bylech. fiue ſigna fint maſculina ſiue feminina. Et ſi fuerit in aſcendente vel in.2.do mo in ſigno maſculino erit aptus bylech in feminino vero non. Si vero lu minaria non fuerit apta vt ſint bylech aſpiciemus utrum coniunctio pceffit natiuitatem vel oppoſitio: fi pceffit coniunctio aſpiciemus gradum coniū ctionis.ſillum gradum in quo luminaria fuerunt cõiuncta ante natiuitates: 7 ſi inuenerimus ipſum gradum in aliquo õttuor angulorum velin aliquo fuccedentium accipiemus ipſum pro bylech. Et ſi non inuenerimus ipfi in aliquo angulorum neqs ſuccedentiú. Debemus afpicere ad gradu pris for tune qui ſi fuerit in aliquo angulop vel ſuccedétiú debem ipm accipere pro bylech. Si vero nó fuerit in aliquo hor locop debem ultimo accipere gras du aſcédété natiuitatis. Si autem oppoſitio pceffit natiuitatem debemus af picere ad gradum opponis ficut dictú eſt de gradu cõiunctionis z poſtea ad ptem fortune z vltimo ad gradú aſcédété natiuitatis:nec opzin gradu coiuns ctionis vel puentionis vel pris fortune aſpicere vtrúſigna ſint maſculina vel feminina. Et ſic patet q ſol pot eé bylech in die, z nocte ſup terra z ſub terra in.ii.locis. Potest enim eé ſup terram bylech in ſex locis z ſubterrain. 5. Et luna ſik̃r poteſt eſſe bylech in.i i.locis. Poteſt enizſub terra eſſe bylech in ſex locis z ſup terram in.5.Siautem luna in aliquo pdictorum locorum fuerit combusta ſub radijs ſolis nó erit apta bylech. Et cum fuerint domus eáte ſe cundum doctrinam traditam in canonibus aſtrolabij z tabularum pmí mo bilis oninis planeta qui fuerit ante afcendens vel ante pncipiú alicuins alte rius domus per quos gradus vel infra erit fouitudo ei valida in domo que ſuccedit. Et omnis locus ex locis pdictis nó poteſt eſſe bylech niſi aſpiciat eñ aligo planeta habens dignitatem in loco illo.f. ſi iſt diuus domus vel domi nus exaltatóis aut triplicitatis aut termini aut faciei. Zota ſnia littere poteſt reduci ad pauca verba. Quins sunt loca a qbus recipiť bylech ideſt ſignifi cator vite que funt bec:fol:luna gradus coniunctionis vel puentionis:pars fortune gradus afcendens natiuitatis. Si natiuitas fuerit in die incipienduz eſt a ſole. Et si no fuerit in locis aptis ſcom o pdictum eſt recipienda est luna Et ſi non fuerit apta recipiendus eſt gradus coniunctionis vel puentionis qui ſi non fuerit aptus recipienda est pars fortune. Et ſi non fuerit apta reci piendus eſt gradus afcendentis natiuitatis.Dicit Ptolemeus o bylech mi qz debeat accipi ab aliquo loco exiſtente ſub terra.Dicit enim z puenit vt no opemur in re ta nobili ſicut iſta per id totú quod eſt ſubterra ſed femp per id quod afcendit z apparet ſup terram. Nec conuenit nos operari per ſignum quod afcendens non aſpi
translation: The ascendant of the circle at the hour of conception in the womb, from the Moon's place at the hour of conception, is the ascendant at the hour of birth. It is also to be understood, for the clarity of what is to be said, that the durations of infants in their mothers' wombs are not equal, according to what Daly says in the commentary on the opposition already cited. Moreover, they are distinctly divided into three durations: a greater duration, a medium duration, and a lesser duration. The lesser duration contains 258 days. The medium duration contains 273 days. The greater duration contains 288 days. And the difference between the greater and lesser is 30 days. This is to be understood for those born according to the natural course. For abortions and those born in the seventh month, this rule does not hold. The quantity of the infant's duration in the womb is taken from the Moon's place in the figure at the hour of birth. For if at the hour of birth the Moon was at the beginning of the seventh house above the earth, that is, above the western horizon, then the infant was in the mother's womb for the lesser duration, that is, for 258 days. And if the Moon was at the hour of birth in the ascendant degree, then the infant remained in the womb for the medium duration, that is, for 273 days. And if the Moon was at the hour of birth in the degree before the western degree, then the infant remained in the womb for the greater duration, that is, 288 days. And note that when the Moon at birth is in the western part near the horizon, if the astrologer believes it to be above the earth when it is below the earth, or vice versa, he will err in the hour of conception by 30 days. But if the Moon was above the earth, elongated from the west, that is, between the east and west, the duration in the womb is greater than the lesser duration and less than the medium. I say that it is greater than the lesser duration according to the quantity of the Moon's elongation from the west. And if the Moon was below the earth, elongated from the ascendant, the duration in the womb is greater than the medium and less than the greater, and it is greater than the medium according to the quantity of elongation from the ascendant. When you wish to investigate the ascendant degree of someone's birth by this method, establish the ascendant at the hour of birth according to the closest possible estimation to the truth, and equate the Moon to that hour; and see in what place it falls in the figure: if it falls at the beginning of the seventh house, subtract 258 days from the hour of birth. And the hour of conception will remain. But if it falls between the seventh house and the ascendant, that is, above the earth, see by how many degrees it is elongated from the western degree, and for every 12 degrees, take one day, and for every degree beyond 12, take two hours. Add these days and hours to the lesser duration, that is, to 258 days. And subtract the total from the hour of birth, and the time of conception will remain. But if it falls in the ascendant, subtract 273 days from the hour of birth, and the time of conception will remain. But if it falls between the ascendant and the seventh, that is, below the earth, consider how much it is elongated from the ascendant degree, and for every 12 degrees, take one day, and for every remaining degree, two hours. Add the resulting number of days and hours to the medium duration, and subtract the total from the hour of birth. But if the Moon falls at the end of the sixth house, subtract the greater duration from the hour of birth, and the hour of conception will remain. Therefore, knowing the hour of conception, equate the Moon to that hour, and the degree in which it is found was the ascendant at the hour of birth. Although this method is true according to the intention of almost all wise men, it seems to me, and I am certain, that it is not sufficient for investigating the degree in a case where I do not have the ascendant degree except by estimation. For it assumes that I know the Moon's elongation from the western degree or from the ascendant; and if I ignore the ascendant degree, and consequently the western degree, how can I know the Moon's distance from any of them? I do not know. For if my estimation errs by 12 degrees, the Moon's distance from the west or from the ascendant will also err by 12 degrees, and for those, one day should be taken, and thus the hour of conception will err by one day, and the ultimate error, that is, in the ascendant degree, will be as much as the Moon moves in one day. In this chapter, the author teaches how to choose the hyleg, that is, the significator of life in nativities. The meaning of the letter is this: In choosing the significator of life, we must begin with the Sun if the nativity was in the day, and if I find it before the ascendant degree by 5 degrees or less, that is, if we find the Sun in the 5 degrees that have risen above the horizon, that is, at the end of the 12th house, or if we find it in the 10th house or 11th house, we will take the Sun as the hyleg, whether it is in masculine signs or feminine signs. But if it is not in these signs, we must look if it is in the 7th, or 8th, or 9th house in masculine signs, we will still take it as the hyleg. But if in these three places already mentioned, that is, in the 7th house, or 8th, or 9th, it were in feminine signs, it would not be suitable to be the hyleg. Therefore, if the Sun is not a suitable hyleg, we will look to the Moon. If we find it in the ascendant, or in the 2nd, or in the 3rd house, or in the 7th, or 8th house, it will be the hyleg, whether it is in a masculine sign or a feminine sign. But if it is not in these places, we will look if we find it before the degree of the ascendant sign by 5 degrees or less, or in the 10th house, or 4th, or in the 5th in a feminine sign, it will still be the hyleg. But if in these places it were in masculine signs, it would not be a suitable hyleg. But if the nativity was at night, we must begin first with the Moon. If we find it before the western degree by 5 degrees or less, that is, in those 5 degrees that are below the earth on the western side, or in any of the other aforementioned places according to the said manner, it will be the hyleg. And if it is not suitable, we will look to the Sun, which, if it is before the degree of the seventh house by 5 degrees or less, that is, below the earth, or if it is in the 4th or 5th house, it will be a suitable hyleg, whether the signs are masculine or feminine. And if it is in the ascendant or in the 2nd house in a masculine sign, it will be a suitable hyleg, but not in a feminine sign. But if the luminaries are not suitable to be hyleg, we will look whether the conjunction preceded the nativity or the opposition: if the conjunction preceded, we will look at the degree of the conjunction, that is, that degree in which the luminaries were conjoined before the nativity; and if we find that degree in any of the four angles or in any of the succedent houses, we will take it as the hyleg. And if we do not find it in any of the angles nor succedent houses, we must look at the degree of the Part of Fortune, which, if it is in any of the angles or succedent houses, we must take it as the hyleg. But if it is not in any of these places, we must finally take the ascendant degree of the nativity. But if the opposition preceded the nativity, we must look at the degree of the opposition as was said about the degree of the conjunction, and then to the Part of Fortune, and finally to the ascendant degree of the nativity; nor is it necessary in the degree of conjunction or opposition or Part of Fortune to look whether the signs are masculine or feminine. And thus it is clear that the Sun can be hyleg by day and night, above the earth and below the earth, in 11 places. For it can be hyleg above the earth in six places and below the earth in 5. And the Moon can likewise be hyleg in 11 places. For it can be hyleg below the earth in six places and above the earth in 5. But if the Moon in any of the aforementioned places is combust under the Sun's rays, it will not be a suitable hyleg. And when the houses are calculated according to the doctrine handed down in the canons of the astrolabe and the tables of the first mobile, every planet that is before the ascendant or before the beginning of any other house by a few degrees or less will have strong fortitude in the succeeding house. And no place from the aforementioned places can be hyleg unless some planet having dignity in that place aspects it, that is, if it is the lord of the house or the lord of exaltation or triplicity or term or face. Note that the meaning of the letter can be reduced to a few words. There are five places from which the hyleg, that is, the significator of life, is taken, which are these: the Sun, the Moon, the degree of conjunction or opposition, the Part of Fortune, the ascendant degree of the nativity. If the nativity is by day, one must begin with the Sun. And if it is not in suitable places as aforesaid, the Moon is to be taken. And if it is not suitable, the degree of conjunction or opposition is to be taken, which, if it is not suitable, the Part of Fortune is to be taken. And if it is not suitable, the ascendant degree of the nativity is to be taken. Ptolemy says that the hyleg should not be taken from any place existing below the earth. For he says that it is not fitting that we operate in such a noble matter as this through anything that is below the earth, but always through that which ascends and appears above the earth. Nor is it fitting for us to operate through a sign that does not aspect the ascendant.

author: Alchabitius
title: Determining the Hyleg and Alcochodon
text: cit nec per ſignum quod afcendit ante afcendens quod dicit dom laborioſi.i.domus.i2.qrest cadens ab angulo 7 plus etiam: quoniam fumofitates que eleuantť ab humoribus terre turbant virtutez ei que venit ab ea ad terram cum ſpiſſitudine ac lugubritate ſua:7 danát ca pp quod apparent colores 7 magnitudines ſtellarú exiſtentium in hac domo al terius maneriei qz ſint per naturam eorum. Daly Abenragel dicit ſapientes in hoc funt plurimum diſcordati:tamen id in quo maior pars cowz2cordat eſt incipiunt pmitus in natiuitatibus diurnis a fole z icipiût ſi eſt in aliquo angulop vel ſuccedenti zin ſigno masculino z in orta maſculina z aſpexerit aliquam dignitatum fuarum erit ipſe bylechir fi ita non fuerit nec afpexerit aliquam dignitatum ſuarum nó erit ipſe aptus pro bylech:z pquires tñby lechia luna z ſi eam inueneris in angulo vel in ſuccedenti z in ſigno ſemini no z in ärta feminina z aſpexerit aliquam dignitatum ſuarum accipe ca p̃ro bylech alioquin non erit apta:tunc aſpice natiuitatem ſi fuerit coniunctiona lis accipe bylech a gradu afcendente:z si dominus aliquis habens ibi aliqua Dignitatem aſpexerit gradum illum accipies eum pro bylech. Et ſi aſcendés non fuerit aptum pergras bylech a parte fortune. Et ſi pars fortune no fue rit apta pergras bylech a loco coniunctionis vel oppoſitionis precedentio natiuitaté. Et ſi non fuerint apti natiuitas no habebit bylech. Et oportebit re neceffario reuerti ad dirigendum per athazir loca bylech ad loca fortuna₹ z ad radios earum z ibi inuenies locum finis. Et si natiuitas fuerit nocturna pquiras pmit? bylech a luna ſicut incepifti pergrere de die a ſole. Et ſi eas in ueneris in angulo vel in ſuccedenti in ſigno feminino z in ärta feminina z ar pexerit aliquam dignitatum ſuarum ipſa erit bylech. Alioquin perquiras by lech a ſole z ſicum inueneris in augulo vel in ſuccedéti in ſigno maſculino z in quarta maſculina z aſpexerit eum habés dignitatem ibidez accipias eú pro bylech alioquin nó erit aptus. Et túc ſi natinitas fuerit puentiõalis per quiras bylech a parte fortune ſicut pquiſiuiſti a luna z ſole. Et si pars fortue nó fuerit apta pquiras bylech a gradu afcendéte. Et ſi aliquis eor non fues rit aptus p bylech pergras bylech a gradu cõiunctionis vel preuentionis p cedentis natiuitaté. Et ſi fuerit in angulo uel in ſuccedéti:z fuerit aſpect ab aliquo habéte ibi aliqua ex qnqz dignitatibus accipe eum p bylech. Et quá do ſol fuerit bylech z nó habuerit alcochodé pergras bylech a gradu lune. z ſi ille.i.gradus lune fuerit bylech z nó babuerit alcochodé perquiras bys lech a gradu aſcendéte ſi natiuitas fuerit cõiunctionalis:7 ſi gradus ille fue ri bylech z nó habuerit alcochoden pquiras bylech a parte fortune.Lamé ſi natiuitas fuerit preuétionalis incipe pmitus a parte fortune: fi fuerit by lech z nó habuerit alcochoden aligs hox perquiras bylec a gradu coniuns ctionis uel oppoſitióis antecedétis natiuitaté:z ſi nullus eop habuerit alcos choden quoniam natus nó babuit bylech nec alcochodé eſt ſignificatio vi te hac durabilitatis modice. Et ſcias q in bylech qui pquirit ab aſcédente ₹ a parte fortune z a gradu cõiunctiõis vel puentionis no aſpicitur maſculini tas nec femininitas ſignon neq3 quartar. Et dicit maior pars ſapientum lu na in tertia domo apta eſt ut ſit bylech:quia gaudet in ea z Dicut ſimilit̃r q̃ ſol in nona domo cõuenit eſſe bylech quia gaudet ibidem. Tex hoc alcochoden. In hoc capitulo docet auctor elis gere alcochodé qui eſt datoz annop vite.Snia littere eſt talis cú volueris ſcire q̃s planetay eſt dator annox vite.debemus afpice re quis planetarũ habeat plures dignitates fiue fortitudines in loco bylech. Uerbi gla:fi ſol fuerit bylech debemus reſpicere planetam for tiorez in loco ſolis. Et ſi luna fuerit bylech debemus reſpicere fortiore in los co lane z ſic de alijs locis inteligédú est. Et ſi talis planeta afpexerit loci by lech a quocus aſpectu debem ipſum accipe p alcochoden. Si vo no aſpe xerit debemus reſpicere alium poſt illu.f.planetaz qui bz in loco bylech ali, quas fortitudines:ſed no tot ſicut primus dis gradus.Uerbi gra:fi vn has heat in loco bylech ſex fortitudines z alius habeat ibi.5.vel 4.fortitudies t pmus no afpexerit locú bylech nó poterit eſſe, alcochoden.tuc ſi ſecundus aſpexerit locú bylech debemus ipſum accipere p alcochoden:z ſi ille etiam no afpexerit debemus aſpicere iterum ad aliú hritem pauciores dignitates z ſic debemus procedere:Donec inueniam aliqué aſpicienté locum bylech qui babeat aliquá dignitaté in loco bylech:z ſi nulla planetaz inuenerimus aſpicientem locú bylech q babeat ibi dignitaté vel dignitates ille locus non potuit effe bylech:ideo in boc caſu oportet querere aliud bylech ſcom mos dum prius dictuz. Uerbi gratia:ſi natiuitas fuerit diurna z ſol fuerit in loco apto ad hoc q ſit bylech:ſi nullus habet dignitatem in loco ſolis aſpiciens ipſum ſol no poterit eſſe bylech:quia nó habet alcochode:ideo oportet túc perquirere bylech a luna. z ſi ipſa fuerit in loco apto bylech z nullus habés In loco eius dignitatem fit aſpiciens et os pergrere bylech a pte fortune vel a gradu cõiunctionis vel puentionis vel gradu aſcédente ſcom ordines pre dictum.Et videbat qbusdam effe incipiédum a dño domus ita videlicet q ſi dñs domus loci bylech aſpexerit bylech accipiebant euz p20 alcochoden z nó aſpexerunt aliquem alium.Si aut dominus domus no aſpexerit afpis ciebantad dñm exaltationis qui ſi aſpexerit ponebant illum alcochoden.Et ſi ille non aſpexerit aſpiciebant dñm triplicitatis.deinde ad dñm termini:des inde z vltimo ad dominum faciei.Dorothius aſpexit pmo dñm termini z ppofuit eum dño domus:ſed hoc non videt veritati coſonum. Et qdam di xerunt ſi duo vel tres vel plures planete conueniant dignitatib? itaq habes ant equales fortitudines eétiales in loco bylech z oés aſpexerint illú locu accipiendus eſt ille quippinqus aſpicit:z ſi in hoc etiam cõueniant q̃o raro cõtingit accipiédus eſt ille qui fuerit ineffe laudabilia ſoleita o ſit cõiunct cum ſole in vno gradu z no fit elongatus ab ipſo per.i6.minuta. Uel accipi endus eſt ille qui fuerit ppinquior angulo aut ille qui fuerit in initio dni ma tutini.f.qui incipit apparere in mane ante ortum ſolis aut ille qui fuerit in ſta tione ſua ſt̃ba z iens ad directioné aut qui fuerit in alio eſſe laudabili a ſole.f q̃o ſit orientalis ſi fuerit aliquis trium ſupiorum aut occidentalis ſi fuerit ali qs trium inferior. Si vo contingat q̃o ſol fuerit bylech z fuerit in leone vel in ariete.f.in domo vel exaltatione ſua erit ipſe bylech z alcochode. Et ſi luna fuerit bylech z fuerit in cancro vel in tauro erit ipſa bylech z alcochoden z non oportet cõſiderare aliqué alium.Ptolemeus dicit q ille eſt alcochoden qui h3 plures fortitudines in loco bylech ſiue aſpiciat ſiue no aſpiciat ipm locum.ſed nullus ſapiétum cõcordat cum eo.ideo tutius videf mibi tenere viam ſapiẽtu. De annis vero quos dat alcochoden licet auctor nihil dicat: volo ti ſcom ſententiam baly abenragel pauca z veritati magis coſona nars rare.Dicit baly abenragel maior pars ſapientum huius ſcientie dicút q̃ qúa do alcochoden fuerit in angulis dat annos ſuos maiores:z qn in ſuccedenti bus dat annos ſuos medios.z qn in cadentibus dat annos ſuos minores nec dat melioramentum alicui ſuccedentium ſup aliú z est error Dicit baly abenragel.ſcias y de ſecretis coopertis z ſignalibus celatio huius ſcie est qn alcochoden fuerit in gradu decime domus dat annos ſuos maiores.Et quando fuerit in gradu yndecime domus dat annos ſuos medios. Et quan do remouet ab vno iſtorum duorum duorum locorum z vadit ad aliuz aſpie ce quot gradus funt ab vno ad aliú z diuide per eos annos qui ſunt inter an nos maiores z annos medios. Uerbi gra:pono q gradus.io.domus ſitter tius gradus piſcium:7 pono q venus ſit alcoboden z ſit in.1z.gradu piſcius: est ergo elongata a puncto.io.dom per.io.gradus.z pono q a pncipio.io. domus vſq ad pncipium.ii.funt.30.gradus.Scio ſi eſſet in pncipio.io.do mus daret annos maiores qui ſunt.82.Et ſi eſſet in pncipio.ii.domus daret annos ſuos medios qui ſunt. 45.nunc autem in
translation: nor through the sign that ascends before the ascendant, which is called the house of labor, i.e., the 12th house, which is also cadent from the 7th angle, even more so: because the fumes that rise from the humors of the earth disturb the virtue that comes from it to the earth with their thickness and gloom: and they damage it because the colors and magnitudes of the stars existing in this house appear of a different kind than they are by their nature. Daly Abenragel says that the wise men are very discordant in this; however, that in which the greater part of them agree is that they begin first in diurnal nativities with the Sun, and they begin if it is in any of the angles or succedent, and in a masculine sign, and in a masculine quarter, and if it aspects any of its dignities, it will be the hyleg. If it is not so, and it does not aspect any of its dignities, it will not be suitable for the hyleg; and then you will seek the hyleg from the Moon, and if you find it in an angle or succedent, and in a feminine sign, and in a feminine quarter, and if it aspects any of its dignities, take it for the hyleg, otherwise it will not be suitable. Then look at the nativity; if it is conjunct, take the hyleg from the ascendant degree; and if any lord having any dignity there aspects that degree, you will take him for the hyleg. And if the ascendant is not suitable, you will seek the hyleg from the Part of Fortune. And if the Part of Fortune is not suitable, you will seek the hyleg from the place of the conjunction or opposition preceding the nativity. And if they are not suitable, the nativity will not have a hyleg. And it will be necessary to return to directing the places of the hyleg to the places of the fortunes and to their rays by athazir, and there you will find the place of the end. And if the nativity is nocturnal, you will first seek the hyleg from the Moon, just as you began to seek from the Sun by day. And if you find it in an angle or succedent, in a feminine sign, and in a feminine quarter, and if it aspects any of its dignities, it will be the hyleg. Otherwise, you will seek the hyleg from the Sun, and if you find it in an angle or succedent, in a masculine sign, and in a masculine quarter, and if one having dignity there aspects it, you will take it for the hyleg, otherwise it will not be suitable. And then if the nativity is preventional, you will seek the hyleg from the Part of Fortune, just as you sought from the Moon and the Sun. And if the Part of Fortune is not suitable, you will seek the hyleg from the ascendant degree. And if none of them is suitable for the hyleg, you will seek the hyleg from the degree of conjunction or prevention preceding the nativity. And if it is in an angle or succedent: and there is an aspect from one having any of the five dignities there, take it for the hyleg. And when the Sun is the hyleg and does not have an alcochodon, you will seek the hyleg from the degree of the Moon. And if that, i.e., the degree of the Moon, is the hyleg and does not have an alcochodon, you will seek the hyleg from the ascendant degree if the nativity is conjunct; and if that degree is the hyleg and does not have an alcochodon, you will seek the hyleg from the Part of Fortune. However, if the nativity is preventional, begin first with the Part of Fortune: if it is the hyleg and none of these has an alcochodon, you will seek the hyleg from the degree of conjunction or opposition preceding the nativity: and if none of them has an alcochodon, because the native did not have a hyleg nor an alcochodon, it is a signification of a short life and durability. And know that in the hyleg sought from the ascendant and from the Part of Fortune and from the degree of conjunction or prevention, the masculinity or femininity of the signs nor of the quarters is not considered. And the greater part of the wise men say that the Moon in the third house is suitable to be the hyleg: because it rejoices in it, and they similarly say that the Sun in the ninth house is suitable to be the hyleg because it rejoices there. The alcochodon. In this chapter, the author teaches how to choose the alcochodon, who is the giver of the years of life. The meaning of the letter is this: when you want to know which of the planets is the giver of the years of life, we must look at which planet has more dignities or strengths in the place of the hyleg. For example: if the Sun is the hyleg, we must look at the stronger planet in the place of the Sun. And if the Moon is the hyleg, we must look at the stronger in the place of the Moon, and so on for other places. And if such a planet aspects the place of the hyleg from any aspect, we must take it as the alcochodon. If, however, it does not aspect, we must look at another after it, i.e., the planet that has some strengths in the place of the hyleg, but not as many as the first degree. For example: if one has six strengths in the place of the hyleg and another has 5 or 4 strengths there, and the first does not aspect the place of the hyleg, it cannot be the alcochodon. Then if the second aspects the place of the hyleg, we must take it as the alcochodon; and if it also does not aspect, we must look again at another having fewer dignities, and so we must proceed: until we find one aspecting the place of the hyleg who has some dignity in the place of the hyleg: and if we find no planet aspecting the place of the hyleg that has dignity or dignities there, that place could not be the hyleg: therefore, in this case, it is necessary to seek another hyleg according to the previously stated method. For example: if the nativity is diurnal and the Sun is in a suitable place to be the hyleg: if no one has dignity in the place of the Sun aspecting it, the Sun cannot be the hyleg: because it does not have an alcochodon: therefore, it is then necessary to seek the hyleg from the Moon. And if it is in a suitable place for the hyleg and no one having dignity in its place aspects it, then seek the hyleg from the Part of Fortune or from the degree of conjunction or prevention or the ascendant degree according to the order stated above. And it seemed to some that one should begin with the lord of the house, so that if the lord of the house of the hyleg aspects the hyleg, they took him as the alcochodon and did not look at any other. But if the lord of the house did not aspect, they looked at the lord of the exaltation, who, if he aspected, they placed him as the alcochodon. And if he did not aspect, they looked at the lord of the triplicity, then at the lord of the term, then and finally at the lord of the face. Dorotheus first looked at the lord of the term and preferred him to the lord of the house: but this does not seem consistent with the truth. And some said that if two or three or more planets agree in dignities so that they have equal essential strengths in the place of the hyleg and all aspect that place, the one who aspects closest is to be taken: and if they also agree in this, which rarely happens, the one who is in a praiseworthy state is to be taken, provided that it is conjunct with the Sun in one degree and is not distant from it by 16 minutes. Or the one who is closer to an angle or the one who is at the beginning of the morning lord, i.e., who begins to appear in the morning before sunrise, or the one who is in its station, standing and going direct, or who is in another praiseworthy state from the Sun, i.e., who is oriental if it is one of the three superior planets or occidental if it is one of the three inferior planets. If, however, it happens that the Sun is the hyleg and is in Leo or Aries, i.e., in its house or exaltation, it will be the hyleg and alcochodon. And if the Moon is the hyleg and is in Cancer or Taurus, it will be the hyleg and alcochodon, and it is not necessary to consider any other. Ptolemy says that he is the alcochodon who has more strengths in the place of the hyleg, whether he aspects that place or not. But none of the wise men agrees with him. Therefore, it seems safer to me to follow the way of the wise men. Regarding the years that the alcochodon gives, although the author says nothing, I want to narrate a few things according to the opinion of Balhy Abenragel, which are more consistent with the truth. Balhy Abenragel says that the greater part of the wise men of this science say that when the alcochodon is in the angles, it gives its greater years; and when in the succedents, it gives its middle years; and when in the cadent houses, it gives its lesser years, and it does not give improvement to one succedent over another, and this is an error, says Balhy Abenragel. Know that among the hidden secrets and signals of this science is that when the alcochodon is in the degree of the tenth house, it gives its greater years. And when it is in the degree of the eleventh house, it gives its middle years. And when it moves from one of these two places to another, look at how many degrees there are from one to the other and divide by them the years that are between the greater years and the middle years. For example: I assume that the degree of the 10th house is the third degree of Pisces: and I assume that Venus is the alcochodon and is in the 12th degree of Pisces: it is therefore distant from the point of the 10th house by 10 degrees. And I assume that from the beginning of the 10th house to the beginning of the 11th are 30 degrees. I know that if it were at the beginning of the 10th house, it would give the greater years, which are 82. And if it were at the beginning of the 11th house, it would give its middle years, which are 45. But now in

title: Planetary Conditions and Life Expectancy

Istud exemplum poteris applicare ad alios angulos t'domos. Dicit baly abenragel quando alcochoden fuerit combustus z ipſeiens ad solem ita y no appareat perdit totum datum suum.qr qui est in hoc statu nó dat ni si dies vel horas vel res que nomen nó habent. z terminus combustiōis est iste. Postqz saturnus fuerit occidentalis a sole ⁊ sol fuerit ad eum z fuerit in ter eos.is.gradus donec remanserit z orientalis fuerit z fuerint inter eos de cem gradus completi. Et iupiter illud idem. Mars tamen postqz inter euz solem in occidente fuerint.is.gradus quoufqz sit orientalis z inter eos sint nouem gradus. Mercurius tamen facit facta sua z dat dona sua cópleta q diu fuerit directus:dummodo non sit appropinquatus soli ad minus dese prem gradibusz a septem gradibus usqzad.5.dat.nó tamen complete Sed a pncipio septem graduum donec transeat solem per quines gradus perdit suum datum totum.z a quinqs gradibus usqz ad cóplementum septem gra duum dat modicum:verum postqz transiuit.7.gradus dat suum datum có pletum.z hoc idem facit venus excepto y quádo apparet visibiliter ad mi nus horum quinqs graduum dat tunc datum suum cópletum. Luna tamen qn applicat soli eundo ad eum z fuerint inter eos.is.gradus debilitat eius factum z dat modicam rem.f.dies vel qo huic assimilar. Et quando applicu erit soli ad.i2.gradus perdit totum eius datú donec transiuerit gradú solis per.io.gradus z tunc dat modicu datú dies.f.vel qo ei assimilat quoufqz trá huerit solé per.i2.gradus z túc dabit totú eius datum cópletum. Cafus auté planete qui est ptrarius exaltationi aufert mediuzdati z termin bui casus z ita sol cum fuerit in gnto gradu libre quoufs transeat.25 gradus eiufdez est in casu suo.residuu signi iudicabit sicut detrimeńtu. Saturn est in suo casu a pncipio duodecim gradus arietis donec trańseat.26.gradu eiufdem:z resi duum signi pro detrimeńto iudicabit. Mars est in suo casu a pncipio.i9 gradus cancri vsqs ad fine ipsius cá cri.r in pncipio ipsius signiiudicat sicut detrimétum. Et ven est in suo casu postqz trasrřit terminú Jouis vfqzad.2i.gradu vginis. Istud de venere qu dicit nó pót stare nisi itelligas sćom terminos ptolemei.sćom eniz terminos illos.i4.gradus virginis est finis termini iouis.sćom do terminos postz tos in alchabício nó est ita.ibi enim terminiouis finit in 2i.gradu virginis. Vel igit littera baly est corrupta vel no pot dictum suú itelligi nifi sćom ters minos ptolemei. Et mercuri est in suo casu a pncipio.8 gradus pisciŭ usqs ad copleméńtű.io.graduű ipsius:z in residuo eiufdé signi iudicat tanqz fieer in detrimento suo. Dicit baly qiupiter z venus qu cóiuncti fuerint corpaliter vel quando aspiciunt alcochode de tertio vel sexa tili addunt mumero annorum quos dedit alcochoden sćom numeruz anno norum suoy mumerozum annos vel méses vel dies vel horas iuxta vtutes vel debilitates comm:z ti ambe infortune saturnus 7 mars quiungunt cor pozaliter cú alcochoden vel ipm aspicût de opposito vel orto minuut ei nus merum annozű suop minori. translation: neither of these two points is. Therefore, I will subtract 45 and a half years from the greater years, which are 82. 37 years remain, which I will multiply by the distance of Venus from the beginning of the tenth house, which is 7. This results in 370. I will divide this by 30, which is the length between the tenth and eleventh houses, and 12 years will result, with a remainder of 10 after the division. I will multiply this by 12 and divide by 30, and four months will result. Therefore, I will subtract 12 years and four months from the greater years of Venus, and 69 years and 8 months will remain. These will be the years that Venus gives in this case. You can apply this example to other angles and houses. Balya Abenragel says that when the Alcochoden is combust and it is going towards the Sun so that it does not appear, it loses all its given years. For that which is in this state gives only days or hours or things that have no name. And the term of combustion is this: After Saturn is occidental to the Sun and the Sun is towards it, and there are 15 degrees between them until it remains and is oriental, and there are 10 complete degrees between them. And Jupiter is the same. Mars, however, after there are 15 degrees between it and the Sun in the west, until it is oriental and there are 9 degrees between them. Mercury, however, does its deeds and gives its complete gifts as long as it is direct, provided that it is not approached by the Sun by less than 7 degrees. And from 7 degrees to 5 degrees, it gives, but not completely. But from the beginning of 7 degrees until it passes the Sun by 5 degrees, it loses all its given years. And from 5 degrees until the completion of 7 degrees, it gives little. But after it has passed 7 degrees, it gives its complete given years. And Venus does the same, except that when it appears visibly at less than 5 degrees, it then gives its complete given years. The Moon, however, when it applies to the Sun by going towards it and there are 15 degrees between them, weakens its action and gives a small thing, that is, days or something similar to this. And when it applies to the Sun at 12 degrees, it loses all its given years until it has passed the Sun's degree by 10 degrees, and then it gives a small given, that is, days or something similar to it, until it has passed the Sun by 12 degrees, and then it will give its complete given years. The fall of a planet, which is contrary to its exaltation, takes away half of the given years, and the term of this fall is such that the Sun, when it is in the 5th degree of Libra until it passes the 25th degree of the same, is in its fall. The remainder of the sign will be judged as detriment. Saturn is in its fall from the beginning of the 12th degree of Aries until it passes the 26th degree of the same, and the remainder of the sign will be judged as detriment. Jupiter is in its fall from the 8th degree of Capricorn until the completion of the 20th degree, and the remainder of the sign will be judged as detriment. Mars is in its fall from the beginning of the 19th degree of Cancer until the end of Cancer, and at the beginning of that sign, it is judged as detriment. And Venus is in its fall after it has passed the term of Jupiter until the 21st degree of Virgo. What is said about Venus cannot stand unless you understand it according to Ptolemy's terms. For according to those terms, the 14th degree of Virgo is the end of Jupiter's term. But according to the terms in Alchabitius, it is not so. For there, Jupiter's term ends in the 21st degree of Virgo. Either the text of Balya is corrupt, or his statement cannot be understood except according to Ptolemy's terms. And Mercury is in its fall from the beginning of the 8th degree of Pisces until the completion of the 10th degree of the same, and in the remainder of the same sign, it is judged as if it were in its detriment. The retrogradation of the three superior planets from the beginning of their retrogradation until the opposition of the Sun, that is, until the middle of the retrogradation, takes away half of the given years, and the other half remains. And when it has passed the opposition of the Sun, see how many degrees and minutes it will go retrograde from the opposition of the Sun, and how many there are, see what proportional part it will have to all the degrees it has to retrograde from the opposition until the direction, and add that much to half of the given years, and direct those degrees correctly with their minutes in such a way that the planet that is at the beginning of its direction will give its complete given years correctly. I will give an example here: I assume Mars is the Alcochoden and its total given years are 60, according to the proportion previously made between the angle and the succeeding house, and I assume it is retrograde in the middle of its retrogradation. It has therefore lost half of these years in the middle of its retrogradation and before the middle of its retrogradation, and 30 years remain. And I assume it has gone beyond the middle of its retrogradation, that is, the opposition of the Sun, retrograding by 2 degrees. And I assume that from the opposition until the direction, it should go retrograde by 10 degrees. Therefore, I will multiply 2 degrees by half of the given years that it lost, that is, by 30 years, and 60 will result. I will divide 60 by 10, and 6 years will result. I will add these years to half of the given years, that is, to 30 years, and 36 years will result. This is the given of Mars in the proposed case. In Venus and Mercury, after each of them begins to retrograde, it loses half of the given years that it gives when it is direct. And afterwards, see how many degrees and minutes remain until its occultation, as accurately as you can, because Venus may appear when there are less than 5 degrees between it and the Sun. And when it has appeared, it will give half of the given years, unless it appeared when there were less than 5 degrees between it and the Sun, in which case that appearance does not harm it in this state. Afterwards, direct those degrees and minutes after it began to retrograde until it occults itself subtly and correctly, and divide by this half of the given years that remained in the aforementioned manner. For that which is occulted loses all its given years, as we said, and nothing remains for it. And it can be exemplified in a similar way to the example given for the retrogradation of the three superior planets. Balya says that Jupiter and Venus, when they are corporally conjoined or when they aspect the Alcochoden from a trine or sextile, add to the number of years that the Alcochoden gave according to the number of their own years, months, days, or hours, according to their strengths or weaknesses. And when both malefics, Saturn and Mars, are corporally conjoined with the Alcochoden or aspect it from an opposition or square, they diminish its number of years by their lesser number. But when Mercury is with the benefics, adding to the Alcochoden, it also adds its lesser number of years. And when it is with the malefics, diminishing the Alcochoden, it also diminishes its part. And when Mars and Saturn aspect from a trine in signs of short ascension or from a sextile in signs of long ascension, they diminish. And I say that the Sun in a square and opposition diminishes its lesser number of years, and adds from a trine and sextile. Many ancient and modern astrologers say that a fortunate Moon adds from a trine or sextile. And all the wise men of this science confess that when a native does not have a Hyleg and has one of the two benefics in the ascendant or midheaven, it is possible that he will live according to the quantity of the lesser years of that benefic that signified life, unless the ascending degree and the Moon are unfortunate, or the same benefic is the lord of the house of death, which signifies a short duration and life. And the Almuten who presides over the nativity. This author teaches how to choose the Almuten, that is, the giving planet in the figure from which the native's being begins after the Hyleg and Alcochoden. The meaning of the text is clear.

author: Alchabitius
title: Calculating the Perfection of Conjunctions
