translation: where the strength is to the place where the planets conjoin. And unless this is understood, it is impossible. For it assumes that one knows the degree of the ascendant at the hour of the conjunction of the superior planets. But this is impossible, as Abraham Avenezra says, for he says it is impossible to know the day on which the conjunction should occur; and this would be a great thing. It is also done in another way, namely, it is taken from the degree of the Midheaven of the revolution of that year in which the conjunction occurred, up to the Sun, and projected from the degree of Jupiter. The second part, by which it is known how long the king's reign should last. Here, the hour of the king's election is taken from the Sun in the 15th degree of Leo. And it is projected from the Moon. It is also taken from the Moon in the 15th degree of Cancer and projected from the Sun. This part is about the time of the king's election. Here, the hour of the king's election is taken on the day from Jupiter to Saturn, and at night the opposite, and projected from the ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the king rose. And if Jupiter is in a common sign and the revolution is diurnal, and with this Jupiter is cadent from an angle, then it is taken from Saturn to Jupiter, and 30 degrees are added above, and it is projected from the ascendant. If, however, Jupiter and Saturn are opposite each other and both are cadent from the ascendant, half of the degrees between them are taken and projected from the ascendant. And if Jupiter is in its exaltation and the revolution is at night, it is counted from it to Saturn and projected from the ascendant. Then the author says that there are two main parts from which the election of the king and its duration are extracted. The first of these is that you look at the hour of the king's election and see where the perfection of the year comes from the conjunction of the triplicity that signified that sect in which that kingdom is, giving to every 30 degrees a year, and to every two and a half degrees a month. And thus, the mixed proportion is to the hour of the election. And when you know in what degree of the sign it is, keep that degree, for from it you will go to the first part. And when you want to calculate it, you will apply the ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the elected one rose. Afterwards, look at Saturn and Jupiter, and whichever of them is oriental from the Sun, take from that one up to the degree of the equation of the first part that you kept, and project from the ascendant of the revolution, and where the number comes, that is the place of the first part. And note, the author says, take from the planet oriental from the Sun, either Saturn or Jupiter, and does not say what should be done when both are oriental. I believe that then it should be taken from the one closest to the Sun. Also, it does not say what should be done if neither of them is oriental. To be brief, I do not know what should be done, as I have not seen these two parts set out in this way in any other book. The second part is known in this way: Look at the conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn in which the king rose or began to reign, to what sign and degree the perfection of the year comes, giving to each year 30 degrees, and to each month 2 and a half degrees, up to the day on which the king rose, and that is the place of the equation of the first part, keep it. After this, take from Saturn or Jupiter, whichever is oriental, up to the place of the second part, and project from the ascendant of the revolution of the year, and where the number comes, that is the place of the second part. These are the parts that signify the strength of the king and his duration. Balya Abenragel says: Look at the revolution of the years of the world for the enthronement of the enthroned. First from Saturn, which is the first part, and from Jupiter, which is the second. And how many degrees and how many minutes are between them, and note this. Afterwards, consider in what sign the lord of the Midheaven is, and reduce those degrees to the ascensions of that sign in which it is, and what comes out from there will be what lasts for years or months or days. And if it is in its houses or exaltations, it will be years; and if they are peregrine in succedent houses, it will be months; otherwise, it will be days. Similarly, when the Sun applies to Mars, and Mars is void of course, not applying to Saturn, it signifies that soldiers will move against the enthroned one and will come upon him with raised weapons, and because of this, sadness and anxieties will befall him, and perhaps they will capture him. But if Mars with this applies to Saturn, their natures conjoin, and no one will move against him. Here, there are also other excetutory parts of the world by which it is known which things will be expensive and which cheap. The meaning of the natal letter. When you want to know whether a thing will be very expensive or cheap, or little, consider the part of that thing and see in which planet's house or exaltation or term or triplicity that part falls. If that planet is combust or retrograde or in some malignant place, that thing will become cheap and of little value. But if it is in a place of strength or in an angle, and especially in the Midheaven, that thing will be expensive and of greater value. And if the lord of that house comes to the place of its descent, that thing will become cheap. He means to say that if the planet has more strengths in the place of the part, in any of its dignities, and especially in its exaltation, or if it is in any of the angles, and especially in the Midheaven, that thing will be expensive. And according to the quantity of the planet's strength in its dignities or in angles or succedent houses, will be the quantity of the dearness. But if the planet itself is in its fall from its house or exaltation, or is in a cadent house from an angle, that thing will become cheap and of little value. Look also at the aspect of the benefics and malefics to it, that is, to the part and to the Moon, and who its lord is and how it aspects that part. If a benefic and the Moon aspect that part, the price of that thing will multiply. And if malefics aspect them, that thing will suffer detriment and will be cheap and of little value. I tell you that if the planet has more strengths in the place of the part in the revolution of the year, and is retrograde, and with this is in any of its dignities or in an angle, it signifies that that thing will be expensive, but not at the beginning of the year, as long as the lord of the part is retrograde. And when that planet begins to become direct, then that thing will begin to be expensive. In the same way, if the planet lord of the part is in its fall and is direct, it signifies the cheapness of the thing, but that cheapness will be greatest when the planet begins to be retrograde. Then he sets out the projections and begins with the part of wheat and says that the part of wheat is taken from the Sun to Mars and projected from the ascendant: from which places they are taken and from which places they are projected is clear in the text. And so that the method may be clearer, I will give an example of the part of wheat in the figure of the preceding conjunction of the Sun's ingress into Aries, and in the figure of the revolution of this year, that is, 1331. I say that in Paris, according to the truth of Alfonsine tables, at the hour of the conjunction of the Sun and Moon preceding the Sun's ingress into Aries, the ascendant will be the first degree of Cancer. The Sun's place at that hour will be 5 signs, 6 degrees, 42 minutes, 36 seconds. The true place of Mars was 1 sign, 44 degrees, 17 minutes, 28 seconds. I will see what is between the Sun and Mars by subtracting the Sun's place from Mars's place, and I find 1 sign, 47 degrees, 34 minutes, 52 seconds, which number I will compute from the ascendant, and the number terminates in Libra, 18 degrees, 10 minutes, 6 seconds. Then I will see what is strongest there, and I find Saturn stronger in that place, which is its exaltation, by virtue of which it has four strengths there. It is its triplicity, by virtue of which it has three strengths. It is also its face, by virtue of which it has one strength. Therefore, Saturn has 8 strengths in the place of the part of wheat. After Saturn is Venus, which is its house, and it has 5 strengths there. And Jupiter has its term there. I will look at the state of each of these in the figure, and according to their state, I will see about the fate of wheat. First, I will look at Saturn, what state it has in the figure, and I find it in the angle of the earth, not distant from the angle by 4 degrees. This signifies that wheat should be expensive, but not too much, because the angle is not strong, for the strongest of the angles in this regard is the Midheaven, then the ascendant, then the angle of the earth, and finally the western angle. But because Saturn is retrograde and peregrine in its place, having no dignity in its place, it signifies that wheat will be of little value at the beginning of the year. And when Saturn begins to become direct, its price will rise and it will be more expensive. This is the signification of Saturn. Then I will look at the state of Venus, and I find it in the 2nd house, its own house. This similarly signifies that wheat will be somewhat expensive. I will also look at the same part in the figure of the revolution of the same year. I say that the ascendant of the revolution in the aforesaid tables will be the fourth degree, 15 minutes, 26 seconds of Libra. The distance between the Sun and Mars will be 1 sign, 45 degrees, 3 minutes, 59 seconds. I will compute this number from the ascendant, and the number terminates in Capricorn, 19 degrees, 19 minutes, 25 seconds. Mars has its exaltation and triplicity in the place of this part, so it has seven strengths there. Saturn has its house and face there, so it has six strengths there. Mars is stronger in the place, and it is in the angle of the Midheaven. This signifies that wheat should be expensive, but because Mars is in its fall from its exaltation, it will not be expensive until it leaves its fall. This writing on Alchabitius, ordered by John of Saxony in the town of Paris, in the year 1331, is finished and corrected.
